title: LAST: Bridging Vision-Language and Action Manifolds via Gromov-Wasserstein Alignment
summary: We take a Gromov-Wasserstein perspective on Vision-Language-Action (VLA) learning, where the goal is to make the relational geometry of action representations compatible with the semantic geometry of VL embeddings. However, this alignment is non-trivial due to the mathematical heterogeneity between the domains: the semantic space of vision-language is topologically linear and isotropic, whereas the physical manifold of robotic action is non-Euclidean and anisotropic. Their disjoint metric structures render direct regression ill-posed. To resolve this incompatibility, we introduce LAST (Lie-algebraic Action Space Tokenizer), which reconstructs the action space to establish local metric compatibility with the VL modality via a two-stage transformation: (1) Global Topological Linearization: linearizing the action manifold via Lie-algebraic mapping, converting trajectories into a fixed-length, physically additive representation. (2) Local Metric Discretization: hierarchically discretizing the representation into schemas and whitened residuals, yielding approximately isotropic local charts that are statistically aligned with the semantic metric. By resolving the structural mismatch at both global and local levels, LAST enables VLA models with superior convergence and generalizability.

AI.tldr: LAST introduces a Lie-algebraic action space tokenizer that resolves the geometric mismatch between semantic VL embeddings and physical action manifolds via global topological linearization and local metric discretization, improving VLA model convergence and generalizability.
AI.motivation: Vision-language embeddings are linear and isotropic, while robotic action manifolds are non-Euclidean and anisotropic. Direct regression between these heterogeneous spaces is ill-posed due to disjoint metric structures.
AI.method: Two-stage transformation: (1) Global Topological Linearization via Lie-algebraic mapping to convert trajectories into fixed-length additive representations; (2) Local Metric Discretization via hierarchical schemas and whitened residuals for approximately isotropic local charts.
AI.result: Enables VLA models with superior convergence and generalizability by resolving the structural mismatch at both global and local levels.
AI.conclusion: The Gromov-Wasserstein perspective on VLA learning, implemented via LAST, effectively bridges the semantic-action geometry gap, making action representations compatible with VL embeddings.

title: CFCamo: A Counterfactual Detect-or-Abstain Framework for Camouflaged Object Detection
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, 5 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/suhang2000/CFCamo
summary: Vision-language reinforcement learning has recently shown strong target-present localization for camouflaged object detection (COD). Yet localization is only one side of the decision: when the agent faces an ordinary image with no camouflaged target, will it still claim that a camouflaged object exists? Standard COD training and evaluation data are positive-only, so agents optimized under this setting can acquire an over-detect bias, a task-specific form of object hallucination that standard COD evaluation leaves unmeasured. To quantify this target-absent behavior, we construct Counterfactual COD (CF-COD), a paired benchmark that removes the camouflaged target from each held-out COD evaluation image while preserving a plausible background. CF-COD evaluates whether a model detects the target on the original image and abstains on the target-absent counterfactual, summarized by Pair Accuracy (PA). We further introduce CFCamo, a paired counterfactual framework for COD with abstention. For training, CFCamo optimizes a Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct agent with Counterfactual Sequence Policy Optimization (CSPO), which samples paired original-counterfactual rollouts and uses a Counterfactual Paired Reward (CPR) to couple original-image detection with counterfactual abstention. On CAMO-test, CFCamo improves S_alpha by +3.7 pp over the prior RL-based COD baseline; across CF-COD, it reaches 80.0-90.8% PA. Ablations show that removing counterfactual coupling reduces PA to 1.4-5.2% despite strong target-present COD scores, showing that target-present evaluation alone does not characterize detect-or-abstain behavior. Overall, these results indicate that CFCamo improves COD agents by coupling target-present detection with target-absent abstention, rather than merely strengthening target-present localization. Code and data are available at https://github.com/suhang2000/CFCamo.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that standard camouflaged object detection (COD) has an over-detect bias due to positive-only training data. To address this, the authors construct CF-COD, a paired benchmark with target-absent counterfactual images, and introduce CFCamo, a framework using a Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct agent with Counterfactual Sequence Policy Optimization (CSPO) and Counterfactual Paired Reward (CPR) to couple detection with abstention. Results show significant improvements in Pair Accuracy and COD scores.
AI.motivation: Standard COD training uses only positive examples (images containing camouflaged objects), leading to an over-detect bias where models hallucinate camouflaged objects even on plain images. The authors aim to quantify and mitigate this target-absent hallucination, which is unmeasured in standard COD evaluation.
AI.method: 1) Constructs CF-COD, a paired benchmark that removes the camouflaged target from each held-out COD image while preserving background. 2) Introduces CFCamo framework using Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct agent with Counterfactual Sequence Policy Optimization (CSPO), which samples paired original-counterfactual rollouts and uses a Counterfactual Paired Reward (CPR) to couple detection on original images with abstention on counterfactual images.
AI.result: On CAMO-test, CFCamo improves S_alpha by +3.7 pp over prior RL-based COD baseline. Across CF-COD, it achieves 80.0-90.8% Pair Accuracy (PA). Ablations show removing counterfactual coupling reduces PA to 1.4-5.2%, despite strong target-present COD scores.
AI.conclusion: CFCamo improves COD agents by coupling target-present detection with target-absent abstention, rather than merely strengthening target-present localization. Target-present evaluation alone does not characterize detect-or-abstain behavior.

title: OSCS-SupCon: Orthogonal Sigmoid-based Common and Style Supervised Contrastive Learning for Robust Feature Disentanglement
summary: Supervised Contrastive Learning (SupCon) has achieved strong performance by explicitly modeling pairwise relationships among samples. However, existing SupCon-based methods suffer from two key limitations: negative-sample dilution induced by the standard InfoNCE loss, and feature-space entanglement caused by the lack of explicit constraints separating category-relevant (common) and category-irrelevant (style) features. These limitations reduce feature discriminability and generalization ability. To address these issues, we propose OSCS-SupCon (Orthogonal Sigmoid-based Common and Style Supervised Contrastive Learning), a unified framework that combines a sigmoid-based pairwise contrastive objective with explicit orthogonality constraints. Specifically, we introduce a sigmoid-based contrastive loss with two learnable parameters, temperature and bias, which adaptively modulate pairwise decision boundaries and alleviate negative-sample dilution. Furthermore, we enforce orthogonality between common and style feature subspaces via a linear projection with ReLU nonlinearity, thereby reducing feature overlap and improving disentanglement of style-irrelevant representations. Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets demonstrate that OSCS-SupCon consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised contrastive learning methods across multiple backbone architectures. In particular, on the fine-grained CUB200-2011 dataset with a ResNet-18 backbone, the proposed method achieves a 3.4% improvement in classification accuracy over CS-SupCon, highlighting its robustness and generalization capability. Ablation studies further confirm the effectiveness of each component.

AI.tldr: OSCS-SupCon improves supervised contrastive learning by replacing InfoNCE with a sigmoid-based loss (handling negative-sample dilution) and adding orthogonality constraints (separating common vs. style features), achieving up to 3.4% higher accuracy on CUB200-2011.
AI.motivation: Existing SupCon methods suffer from (1) negative-sample dilution due to InfoNCE loss, and (2) feature-space entanglement from no explicit separation of category-relevant (common) and category-irrelevant (style) features, which reduces discriminability and generalization.
AI.method: Proposes OSCS-SupCon with two components: (1) a sigmoid-based contrastive loss with learnable temperature and bias to adaptively modulate pairwise decision boundaries, and (2) orthogonality constraints enforced via linear projection with ReLU to disentangle common and style feature subspaces.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art SupCon methods on six benchmark datasets across multiple backbones; on CUB200-2011 (ResNet-18), achieves 3.4% accuracy improvement over CS-SupCon. Ablation studies confirm each component's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: OSCS-SupCon effectively addresses negative-sample dilution and feature entanglement, leading to more discriminative and generalizable representations for supervised contrastive learning.

title: Traits Run Deeper: Trait-Specific Asymmetric Fusion for Personality Assessment
summary: Personality assessment aims to infer stable personality traits from dynamic behaviors across language, voice, and facial cues. Since different personality dimensions are revealed through distinct behavioral perspectives, modeling trait-specific evidence is challenging. However, most existing approaches adopt a uniform multimodal fusion strategy across all dimensions, assuming identical modality contributions. This overlooks trait-specific modality preferences and introduces cross-modal interference. To address this issue, we propose a novel personality assessment framework called Traits Run Deeper, which consists of three components. Specifically, the Multimodal Foundation Representation (MFR) module constructs personality-oriented multimodal inputs and leverages psychology-informed semantic templates as anchors, enabling foundation models to capture trait-relevant information. Building upon MFR, the Trait-Specific Modality Fusion (TSMF) module acts as an asymmetric fusion mechanism, allowing each dimension to selectively exploit different modality pathways from modality-specific modeling to complementary fusion. Thus, TSMF captures heterogeneous modality preferences while reducing cross-modal contamination. Furthermore, the Distribution-Calibrated Personality Regression (DCPR) module mitigates label imbalance and central tendency bias through target distribution calibration, improving robustness and stability. Experimental results on the AVI Challenge 2026 validation set demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework, reducing mean squared error (MSE) by approximately 25% compared with the baseline. Consistent improvements are observed on the official test set, where our method achieves the best performance and ranks first in the Personality Assessment Track. The source code will be made available at https://github.com/MSA-LMC/AVI2026.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes "Traits Run Deeper," a personality assessment framework that addresses trait-specific modality preferences in multimodal fusion. It includes three components: MFR (psychology-informed semantic anchors for foundation models), TSMF (asymmetric fusion for trait-specific modality selection), and DCPR (distribution calibration for label bias). Achieves ~25% MSE reduction and top ranking on AVI 2026 Challenge.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal personality assessment methods use a uniform fusion strategy across all personality dimensions, ignoring that different traits are best inferred from different modalities. This causes cross-modal interference and suboptimal performance.
AI.method: Three components: (1) MFR uses psychology-informed semantic templates as anchors for foundation models to capture trait-relevant info; (2) TSMF provides asymmetric fusion allowing each dimension to selectively use different modality pathways; (3) DCPR calibrates target distribution to handle label imbalance and central tendency bias.
AI.result: Reduces MSE by ~25% vs baseline on AVI 2026 validation set; achieves best performance and ranks 1st on official test set in Personality Assessment Track.
AI.conclusion: Trait-specific multimodal fusion and distribution calibration are effective for personality assessment, outperforming uniform fusion approaches.

title: EventRadar: Long-Range Visual UAV Discovery through Spatiotemporal Event Sensing
summary: Unauthorized unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) activity around airports, public venues, and other sensitive sites has made protected-airspace monitoring increasingly important. A practical sensing system must search a wide angular region, find small long-range targets, and return both bearing support and UAV-specific evidence before a restricted perimeter is breached. Existing UAV detection paths often rely on spatially organized evidence, such as body extent, silhouette, or track continuity. At long range, however, these cues become difficult to preserve and verify as the target footprint weakens and its image-plane support shrinks. EventRadar follows a complementary cue: propeller-induced temporal periodicity, which recent event-camera sensing studies have shown can reveal UAV-specific motion after appearance becomes weak. We extend this cue to kilometer-scale active sensing with an event-camera prototype. Scene-Anchored Geometry Evidence (SAGE) fuses scanning events with IMU pose to maintain a bearing-indexed scene memory, separating transient candidate support from persistent background clutter. Comb-guided Harmonic-Group Learned Iterative Shrinkage and Thresholding Algorithm (CHG) then treats each candidate as a weak high-rate timing signal and recovers phase-insensitive harmonic evidence with fixed compute. Compared with related event-camera baselines on 700-1500 m UAV event recordings, EventRadar achieves 0.990 mAP$_{.3}$ and 0.949 F1$_{.3}$, reduces FN$_{.3}$ to 0.009, and shows real-time feasibility in prototype profiling.

AI.tldr: EventRadar is an event-camera-based UAV detection system that exploits propeller-induced temporal periodicity for long-range (700-1500m) sensing. It uses Scene-Anchored Geometry Evidence (SAGE) for bearing-indexed scene memory and Comb-guided Harmonic-Group LISTA (CHG) for harmonic evidence recovery, achieving 0.990 mAP@0.3 and real-time feasibility.
AI.motivation: Unauthorized UAV activity near sensitive sites requires long-range detection systems that can search wide angular regions and provide bearing-specific evidence before perimeter breaches. Existing appearance-based methods fail at long range due to weak target footprints and small image-plane support.
AI.method: EventRadar uses an event-camera for active sensing, extending the cue of propeller-induced temporal periodicity to kilometer-scale. It employs Scene-Anchored Geometry Evidence (SAGE) to fuse scanning events with IMU pose for bearing-indexed scene memory, and Comb-Guided Harmonic-Group Learned Iterative Shrinkage and Thresholding Algorithm (CHG) for phase-insensitive harmonic evidence recovery with fixed compute.
AI.result: On 700-1500m UAV event recordings, EventRadar achieves 0.990 mAP@0.3, 0.949 F1@0.3, reduces false negative rate at 0.3 IoU to 0.009, and demonstrates real-time feasibility in prototype profiling.
AI.conclusion: The complementary cue of temporal periodicity from propellers is effective for long-range UAV detection when appearance cues are weak, and EventRadar's pipeline offers practical, real-time performance for protected-airspace monitoring.

title: i1: A Simple and Fully Open Recipe for Strong Text-to-Image Models
summary: Diffusion models have consistently driven progress in text-to-image generation. However, it is challenging to attribute recent progress to specific modeling and data choices: state-of-the-art open-weight models provide limited ablations, and do not disclose their training data and full training details. The research community needs fully open (weights, data, and code) models as a foundation for further research; yet existing fully open models still fall significantly short of leading models in performance. In this project, we conduct a systematic investigation of the modeling and data design choices in text-to-image diffusion training and inference with 300+ controlled experiments totaling 700K+ TPU v6e hours. Our experiments highlight several empirical findings (e.g., equal weighting is a strong default for mixing curated datasets) and simple design decisions (e.g., larger text encoder adapters improve performance with minimal added parameters) for training strong models. Guided by these insights, we train i1, a 3B-parameter text-to-image diffusion model using only publicly available datasets. i1 is competitive with leading models on five representative benchmarks (GenEval, DPG, PRISM, CVTG-2K, and LongText), and outperforms the best existing fully open model by 29.5 absolute percentage points on average. We provide the i1 checkpoints, training and inference code, and the data processing pipeline. Together, our findings and the i1 recipe establish a practical foundation for future open research in text-to-image diffusion models. Our code is available at https://github.com/zlab-princeton/i1.

AI.tldr: The paper presents i1, a fully open 3B-parameter text-to-image diffusion model trained on public datasets, achieving competitive performance with leading models after systematic investigation of 300+ controlled experiments covering modeling and data design choices.
AI.motivation: Current open-weight models lack transparency - they don't disclose training data, full training details, or provide sufficient ablations. The research community needs fully open models (weights, data, code) as a foundation for further research, but existing fully open models significantly lag behind leading models in performance.
AI.method: Conducted 300+ controlled experiments (700K+ TPU v6e hours) to systematically investigate modeling and data design choices in text-to-image diffusion training and inference. Key findings include: equal weighting as a strong default for mixing curated datasets, and larger text encoder adapters improving performance with minimal added parameters. Used these insights to train i1, a 3B-parameter diffusion model using only publicly available datasets.
AI.result: i1 is competitive with leading models on five benchmarks (GenEval, DPG, PRISM, CVTG-2K, LongText) and outperforms the best existing fully open model by 29.5 absolute percentage points on average.
AI.conclusion: The findings and i1 recipe establish a practical foundation for future open research in text-to-image diffusion models. The authors fully open-source checkpoints, training/inference code, and data processing pipeline.

title: TRON: Tracing Rays to Orchestrate a Neural Renderer for 3D Gaussian Reconstructions
summary: We introduce TRON, a rendering framework that combines 3D Gaussian ray tracing with neural rendering to enable realistic and controllable rendering of real-world 3D scenes under novel lighting, dynamic object motion, object insertion, and material editing. Prior approaches that rely solely on physically based rendering (PBR) of Gaussian representations struggle to achieve realistic relighting due to imperfections in reconstructed geometry, material estimates, and light transport estimation. At the same time, neural rendering methods often lack an explicit scene representation, limiting their ability to support interactive editing with fine-grained manipulation. TRON bridges these two paradigms. We use intrinsic decomposition priors from a learned inverse rendering model to regularize the material properties of a Gaussian field, and repurpose a ray tracer to provide radiometric guidance rather than final pixels. By treating this output as a structured 3D scaffold, we empower a lightweight neural renderer to bridge the domain gap between shading-model constrained estimates and photorealistic output. Our key insight is that the combination of explicit 3D knowledge with robust material priors provides speed and controllability, while neural rendering enables the synthesis of photorealistic images. To support real-world scenarios, we train our neural renderer with a multi-stage strategy consisting of large-scale pretraining and targeted fine-tuning on a newly constructed dataset of 2.1M rendered synthetic and real-world frames from 3D reconstructions. TRON outperforms Gaussian-based relighting methods in realism, and prior neural renderers in editability and speed. To the best of our knowledge, TRON is the first method to enable practical interactive applications in captured 3D environments, offering realistic appearance under dynamic geometric, lighting and material conditions.

AI.tldr: TRON is a rendering framework that combines 3D Gaussian ray tracing with neural rendering to achieve realistic, controllable rendering of real-world 3D scenes under novel lighting, dynamic motion, object insertion, and material editing. It uses intrinsic decomposition priors for material regularization and a ray tracer for radiometric guidance, then applies a neural renderer to bridge the domain gap. Trained on 2.1M frames, it outperforms prior methods in realism, editability, and speed.
AI.motivation: Prior PBR-based Gaussian methods struggle with realistic relighting due to imperfect geometry, material, and light transport estimates, while neural rendering methods lack explicit scene representations for fine-grained interactive editing.
AI.method: TRON combines 3D Gaussian ray tracing with neural rendering by: (1) using intrinsic decomposition priors from a learned inverse rendering model to regularize material properties of a Gaussian field, (2) repurposing a ray tracer to provide radiometric guidance as a structured 3D scaffold, and (3) employing a lightweight neural renderer to bridge the domain gap. Training uses a multi-stage strategy with large-scale pretraining and fine-tuning on 2.1M rendered frames.
AI.result: TRON outperforms Gaussian-based relighting methods in realism and prior neural renderers in editability and speed. It is the first method enabling practical interactive applications in captured 3D environments under dynamic geometric, lighting, and material conditions.
AI.conclusion: Combining explicit 3D knowledge with robust material priors provides speed and controllability, while neural rendering enables photorealistic output—bridging the gap between physically-based and neural rendering paradigms for real-world scenes.

title: Semantic Segmentation of Node and Edge Diagrams for Assistive Technology
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, 1 table. In Proceedings of the 23rd Conference on Robots and Vision (2026)
summary: In this paper, we present a novel set of related models for semantic segmentation of node-link diagrams. These diagrams are frequently used to represent mathematical graphs, relationships between concepts, and flowcharts. Such diagrams are difficult to access non-visually; while some assistive interfaces have been designed for node-link diagrams, they rely upon a machine-readable representation of the diagram, whereas such diagrams will generally be made available as bitmap images. Our compact deep learning models show excellent quantitative and qualitative performance on a large synthetic dataset of node-link diagrams, reaching per-pixel accuracy over 93\%.

AI.tldr: The paper presents deep learning models for semantic segmentation of node-link diagrams from bitmap images, achieving over 93% per-pixel accuracy on synthetic datasets, addressing accessibility challenges for non-visual users.
AI.motivation: Node-link diagrams (mathematical graphs, flowcharts) are difficult to access non-visually. Existing assistive interfaces require machine-readable formats, but diagrams are typically available only as bitmap images. The authors aim to bridge this gap.
AI.method: The authors propose compact deep learning models designed for semantic segmentation of node-link diagrams. They train and evaluate on a large synthetic dataset of such diagrams.
AI.result: The models achieve excellent quantitative and qualitative performance, reaching over 93% per-pixel accuracy on the synthetic dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed compact deep learning models can effectively segment node-link diagrams from bitmap images, potentially enabling better accessibility tools for non-visual users without requiring machine-readable diagram formats.

title: DarkVGGT: Seeing Through Darkness Using Thermal Geometry without Daylight Tax
summary: Recent feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods have demonstrated strong performance and flexibility in efficient end-to-end scene geometry estimation from image streams. However, their reliance on visible-light appearance makes them vulnerable in dark and low-visibility environments, where RGB cues are severely degraded and geometric evidence becomes ambiguous. To address this challenge, we propose DarkVGGT, an RGB-T feed-forward geometry framework that uses physics-aware thermal modeling for robust 3D estimation in low-light scenes. DarkVGGT introduces two complementary modules. First, physics-inspired thermal factorization extracts emissive-dominant, geometry-consistent thermal cues while isolating sparse reflective residuals that may introduce geometric ambiguity. Second, geometry-shared thermal routing isolates modality-invariant geometric structures from thermal-specific patterns, selectively injecting reliability-aware structural guidance into the RGB stream. Together, these components enable accurate thermal-informed geometry estimation under degraded RGB conditions while largely preserving performance in well-lit environments. Experiments on low-visibility RGB-T benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in both depth and camera pose estimation over existing feed-forward geometry baselines.

AI.tldr: DarkVGGT is an RGB-T feed-forward 3D reconstruction framework that uses physics-aware thermal modeling to achieve robust geometry estimation in dark/low-visibility environments, outperforming existing methods on low-visibility benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods rely on visible-light appearance (RGB), which fails in dark and low-visibility environments where RGB cues are degraded and geometric evidence is ambiguous.
AI.method: DarkVGGT introduces two modules: (1) physics-inspired thermal factorization that extracts emissive-dominant, geometry-consistent thermal cues while isolating sparse reflective residuals; (2) geometry-shared thermal routing that isolates modality-invariant geometric structures from thermal-specific patterns and injects reliability-aware structural guidance into the RGB stream.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in both depth and camera pose estimation over existing feed-forward geometry baselines on low-visibility RGB-T benchmarks, while preserving performance in well-lit environments.
AI.conclusion: Physics-aware thermal modeling enables robust 3D estimation in degraded RGB conditions, and the proposed framework generalizes well to both dark and well-lit environments.

title: NSVQ: Mitigating Codebook Collapse by Stabilizing Encoder Drift in Vector Quantization
summary: Vector quantization is central to modern generative modeling pipelines, but large-codebook VQ models often suffer from codebook collapse. We identify encoder drift as a key driver of this failure: as the encoder moves the latent distribution, sparsely updated code vectors can lag behind, lose assignments, and increase quantization error, creating a feedback loop through the straight-through estimator. We propose NSVQ, a non-stationary-aware VQ training strategy that combines a dense non-stationary embedding loss, codebook replacement, and stage-wise encoder freezing. NSVQ first helps the codebook track encoder drift during early training, then freezes the encoder to consolidate the codebook under a fixed latent geometry, and finally reintroduces adversarial refinement. Experiments on ImageNet-1k show that NSVQ improves reconstruction quality while maintaining full codebook utilization. On ImageNet-1k at 128$\times$128 with 65,536 codes, NSVQ reduces rFID from 2.39 to 2.10 compared with SimVQ, while both methods maintain 100\% utilization. Additional latent diffusion experiments show that NSVQ also improves downstream ImageNet generation FID.

AI.tldr: NSVQ addresses codebook collapse in VQ models by tackling encoder drift through a non-stationary embedding loss, codebook replacement, and stage-wise encoder freezing, achieving better reconstruction and 100% codebook utilization on ImageNet-1k.
AI.motivation: Large-codebook VQ models often suffer from codebook collapse; the paper identifies encoder drift as a key driver of this failure.
AI.method: NSVQ combines a dense non-stationary embedding loss, codebook replacement, and stage-wise encoder freezing. It first helps the codebook track encoder drift, then freezes the encoder, and finally reintroduces adversarial refinement.
AI.result: On ImageNet-1k (128x128, 65,536 codes), NSVQ reduces rFID from 2.39 to 2.10 vs SimVQ, both achieving 100% codebook utilization. Downstream generation FID also improves.
AI.conclusion: NSVQ effectively mitigates codebook collapse by addressing encoder drift, improving reconstruction and downstream generation quality.

title: From Simulation to Real-World: An In-Field 6D Pose Dataset and Baseline for Robotic Strawberry Harvesting
summary: Robotic strawberry harvesting requires precise 6D pose estimation; however, collecting 6D pose ground truth in real agricultural fields is inherently challenging. Existing 6D pose estimation methods have therefore relied solely on synthetic data that lacks scene-level realism, leaving their performance under real agricultural field conditions unquantified. In this work, we present, to the best of our knowledge, the first real-world 6D pose ground truth dataset of strawberries collected in actual agricultural fields (12,040 images). We also introduce a synthetic dataset rendered in NVIDIA Isaac Sim, featuring scene-level realism and domain randomization. Nevertheless, our experiments reveal that a significant sim-to-real gap persists, underscoring the necessity of real agricultural field data for reliable evaluation. We further quantify the sim-to-real gap through baseline 6D pose estimation results across backbone encoders, serving as a reference for future work. The real-world dataset will be made available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first real-world 6D pose ground truth dataset for robotic strawberry harvesting in agricultural fields (12,040 images) and a synthetic dataset with scene-level realism. Experiments reveal a persistent sim-to-real gap, highlighting the need for real field data in reliable pose estimation.
AI.motivation: Real agricultural fields lack 6D pose ground truth data for robotic strawberry harvesting; existing methods rely solely on synthetic data with insufficient scene-level realism, leaving real-world performance unquantified.
AI.method: Collected 12,040 real-world 6D pose ground truth images in actual strawberry fields, and created a synthetic dataset using NVIDIA Isaac Sim with scene-level realism and domain randomization.
AI.result: A significant sim-to-real gap persists even with improved synthetic data; baseline 6D pose estimation results across various backbone encoders quantify this gap.
AI.conclusion: Real agricultural field data is essential for reliable evaluation of 6D pose estimation; the dataset serves as a benchmark for future work.

title: DeceptionX: Explainable Deception Detection with Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Deception detection is a critical and highly challenging task within affective computing and behavioral analysis. Existing deep learning methods typically treat this task as a straightforward classification problem; however, this black-box approach lacks interpretability and fails to capture the complex logical deduction processes utilized by human experts when identifying lies. While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown potential, applying them effectively requires a bridge between low-level audiovisual cues and high-level logical reasoning. In this paper, we propose DeceptionX, a novel MLLM framework that shifts the paradigm of deception detection from black-box classification to an interpretable Observe-Think-Summarize reasoning process. To address the scarcity of high-quality reasoning data, we first constructed DeceptChain, a high-quality dataset developed through a human-in-the-loop process. This dataset synthesizes fine-grained visual and auditory evidence (such as micro-expressions and vocal tremors) into structured chain-of-thought reasoning data. Furthermore, we propose a three-stage training pipeline and a Discrepancy-Aware Redundancy Elimination~(DARE) strategy for DeceptionX to further enhance the model's generalization capabilities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeceptionX not only outperforms existing MLLM baselines and state-of-the-art methods on standard real-world benchmarks but also provides transparent, expert-level reasoning paths, bridging the critical gap between accuracy and interpretability in multimodal deception detection.

AI.tldr: DeceptionX is a novel MLLM framework that transforms deception detection from black-box classification to an interpretable Observe-Think-Summarize reasoning process, using a human-in-the-loop dataset (DeceptChain), a three-stage training pipeline, and a Discrepancy-Aware Redundancy Elimination strategy to achieve both high accuracy and transparent, expert-level reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods treat deception detection as a simple classification problem, lacking interpretability and failing to capture the complex logical deduction processes that human experts use. MLLMs show potential but need a bridge between low-level audiovisual cues and high-level reasoning.
AI.method: DeceptionX framework with an Observe-Think-Summarize reasoning pipeline; DeceptChain dataset built via human-in-the-loop process with fine-grained visual/auditory evidence and chain-of-thought reasoning; three-stage training pipeline; Discrepancy-Aware Redundancy Elimination (DARE) strategy for better generalization.
AI.result: Outperforms existing MLLM baselines and state-of-the-art methods on standard real-world benchmarks, while providing transparent, expert-level reasoning paths.
AI.conclusion: DeceptionX bridges the critical gap between accuracy and interpretability in multimodal deception detection, shifting the paradigm from black-box classification to interpretable reasoning.

title: A Scalable PyTorch Abstraction for Multi-GPU Gaussian Splatting
comment: 14 pages, 6 tables, 2 figures, and 1 listing. Includes supplementary material
summary: Gaussian splatting methods have become increasingly popular for neural reconstruction of the real world. However, they are often limited in scale and resolution due to compute and memory constraints. We present a multi-GPU Gaussian splatting approach that scales reconstruction to higher resolutions and larger scenes while abstracting away the code complexity typically associated with distributing a model. To accomplish this, we propose a PyTorch backend that distributes the Gaussian parameters and splatting operators across GPUs via CUDA unified memory and NVLink. Because distribution occurs at the operator level, the model code requires no explicit cross-device communication. More broadly, the backend exposes multiple GPUs as an aggregate PyTorch device and supports other PyTorch operators. We demonstrate city-scale reconstructions with street-level detail consisting of over 1 billion Gaussian splats, more than 25 times as many as the current state of the art.

AI.tldr: A multi-GPU Gaussian splatting method that scales to city-scale reconstructions with over 1 billion Gaussian splats using a PyTorch backend with CUDA unified memory and NVLink, requiring no explicit cross-device communication in model code.
AI.motivation: Existing Gaussian splatting methods are limited in scale and resolution due to compute and memory constraints; the authors aim to overcome these limitations for large-scale, high-resolution neural reconstruction.
AI.method: Proposes a PyTorch backend that distributes Gaussian parameters and splatting operators across multiple GPUs using CUDA unified memory and NVLink, performing distribution at the operator level so model code needs no explicit cross-device communication.
AI.result: Achieves city-scale reconstructions with over 1 billion Gaussian splats, more than 25 times the previous state-of-the-art, with street-level detail.
AI.conclusion: Multi-GPU distribution via operator-level abstraction effectively scales Gaussian splatting to unprecedented sizes while keeping model code simple and supporting standard PyTorch operators.

title: 3D-CBM: A Framework for Concept-Based Interpretability in Generative 3D Modeling
summary:
This research introduces a framework for incorporating Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) into 3D generative architectures to address the inherent 'semantic gap' in deep geometric learning. As deep models become central to 3D content creation, explainability shifts from a peripheral feature to a fundamental requirement for trust and accountability in safety-critical domains such as healthcare and manufacturing. CBMs provide an intrinsic interpretability solution by constraining latent representations to align with human-defined concepts, yet their application to unstructured 3D data remains largely unexplored.
  We design, implement, and validate a formal 3D-CBM architecture that maps raw geometric inputs, including point clouds and meshes, into a multi-tiered taxonomy of interpretable primitives and functional attributes. The framework further identifies strategic datasets, such as PartNet and ShapeNet, specialized for concept-based supervision. Experimental results from a 3D part-manipulation proof-of-concept experiment demonstrate the framework's efficacy, achieving a concept prediction accuracy of 88.8\% and a Chamfer Distance of 0.0115. Critically, the model enables precise test-time intervention, allowing for the interactive correction of structural errors. This work establishes a foundation for semantically-steerable 3D generation and invites further exploration into collaborative human-in-the-loop design systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a 3D-CBM framework that integrates Concept Bottleneck Models into 3D generative architectures to bridge the semantic gap in deep geometric learning. It maps raw 3D data into interpretable concepts, achieving high concept prediction accuracy (88.8%) and low Chamfer Distance (0.0115), enabling test-time intervention for structural error correction.
AI.motivation: Deep models are increasingly used in safety-critical 3D applications (healthcare, manufacturing), yet lack interpretability. Existing CBMs work on images/text but unexplored for unstructured 3D data. The 'semantic gap' in deep geometric learning needs addressing for trust and accountability.
AI.method: A 3D-CBM architecture is designed that maps raw geometric inputs (point clouds, meshes) into a multi-tiered taxonomy of interpretable primitives and functional attributes. Strategic datasets like PartNet and ShapeNet are identified for concept-based supervision.
AI.result: In a 3D part-manipulation proof-of-concept, the framework achieves 88.8% concept prediction accuracy and 0.0115 Chamfer Distance. The model enables precise test-time intervention for interactive correction of structural errors.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a foundation for semantically-steerable 3D generation and invites further exploration into collaborative human-in-the-loop design systems.

title: Exploring Adaptive Masked Reconstruction for Self-Supervised Skeleton-Based Action Recognition
comment: Accepted by CVPR2026. The code is available at https://github.com/AshenOne1005/AMR
summary: Recently, masked skeleton reconstruction models have emerged as strong action representation learners, driving significant progress in self-supervised skeleton-based action recognition. However, existing state-of-the-art methods must predict an exceedingly large number of spatiotemporal patches, significantly prolonging training time. Besides, by treating all spatiotemporal regions equally during reconstruction, these models are distracted from learning the critical motion patterns that underlie action semantics. To address these challenges, we propose Adaptive Masked Reconstruction (AMR), a faster and stronger pre-training framework. We first decouple the decoder from the encoder, enabling flexible prediction of larger spatiotemporal patches and dramatically reducing reconstruction complexity. Given that larger patches contain more complex information, which is challenging to predict and consequently degrades performance, we accordingly introduce an adaptive guidance module. This module identifies regions of high motion informativeness, guiding the model to focus on the most discriminative parts of each patch and alleviating reconstruction difficulty. Experiments on NTU RGB+D 60, NTU RGB+D 120, and PKU-MMD datasets demonstrate that AMR not only accelerates pre-training substantially but also improves downstream recognition accuracy, surpassing current state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: AMR proposes a faster and stronger self-supervised skeleton-based action recognition framework by decoupling the decoder from the encoder for larger patch prediction and introducing an adaptive guidance module to focus on motion-informative regions, achieving superior accuracy and efficiency on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing masked skeleton reconstruction models suffer from (1) excessively long training time due to predicting too many spatiotemporal patches, and (2) learning distraction from treating all spatiotemporal regions equally, which hinders capturing critical motion patterns.
AI.method: (1) Decouple the decoder from the encoder to allow flexible prediction of larger spatiotemporal patches, reducing reconstruction complexity. (2) Introduce an adaptive guidance module that identifies high-motion-informativeness regions, guiding the model to focus on discriminative parts within each patch and alleviating reconstruction difficulty.
AI.result: AMR accelerates pre-training substantially and improves downstream recognition accuracy, surpassing state-of-the-art methods on NTU RGB+D 60, NTU RGB+D 120, and PKU-MMD datasets.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling encoder-decoder for larger patch prediction combined with motion-guided adaptive attention offers a more efficient and effective pre-training paradigm for skeleton-based action recognition.

title: PT-WNO: Point Transformer with Wavelet Neural Operator for 3D Point Cloud Semantic Segmentation
summary: Point cloud semantic segmentation requires architectures that capture both fine-grained local geometry and broad global scene structure. Transformer-based networks have demonstrated strong performance by focusing on detailed local feature aggregation; however, global context is conveyed primarily through skip connections across encoder-decoder stages, which we argue is insufficient for full scene understanding. We hypothesize that augmenting skip connections with a learnable global feature extraction module allows the network to acquire scene-level knowledge before descending into local detail, leading to richer and more contextually grounded representations. To this end, we propose Point Transformer with Wavelet Neural Operato (PT-WNO), which integrates a shared Wavelet Neural Operator (WNO) branch alongside the skip connections of a point cloud transformer backbone. At each encoder-decoder transition, point features are projected onto a dense 3D volumetric grid where the WNO captures multi-scale global spectral context through learnable wavelet decomposition and reconstruction. These global features are fused back into the network via lightweight adapters, complementing rather than replacing the existing skip connections. Experiments on four large-scale 3D point cloud benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of PT-WNO. On S3DIS (Area 5), PT-WNO achieves 71.59% mIoU, outperforming the Point Transformer v3 (PTv3) baseline by +1.03 points. On DALES it achieves 81.05% mIoU (+1.47 over the baseline). On ScanNet~v2, PT-WNO obtains 76.19% mIoU, remaining competitive with the baseline (76.36%).

AI.tldr: PT-WNO enhances point cloud semantic segmentation by adding a Wavelet Neural Operator branch to transformer skip connections, capturing global spectral context and improving performance on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Global context in point cloud transformers is conveyed primarily through skip connections, which is insufficient for full scene understanding. The authors hypothesize that augmenting skip connections with a learnable global feature extraction module can yield richer, contextually grounded representations.
AI.method: Proposes PT-WNO, which integrates a shared Wavelet Neural Operator (WNO) branch alongside skip connections. Point features are projected onto a dense 3D volumetric grid, where WNO captures multi-scale global spectral context via learnable wavelet decomposition and reconstruction. Global features are fused back via lightweight adapters.
AI.result: S3DIS (Area 5): 71.59% mIoU (+1.03 over PTv3). DALES: 81.05% mIoU (+1.47). ScanNet v2: 76.19% mIoU (competitive with baseline 76.36%).
AI.conclusion: Augmenting skip connections with a learnable global feature extraction module (WNO) effectively improves scene-level understanding in point cloud semantic segmentation, with consistent gains on multiple benchmarks.

title: Towards Fully Automated Exam Grading: Fairness-Aware Recognition of Handwritten Answers with Foundation Models
summary: Correcting handwritten exams by hand is time-consuming and error-prone, particularly for large cohorts, while fully digital exams tend to force a didactic narrowing towards closed question formats. A practical middle ground keeps paper-based, problem-oriented tasks but records the assessment-relevant answers as single capital letters in a table that a machine can read. The open question is whether this reading can be made accurate and, above all, fair enough for unsupervised grading. Earlier automated approaches reached only about 88%--91% recognition -- too low -- and failed on the cases that matter most: answers placed outside the cell, crossed out, or written in cursive. We show that general-purpose vision-language foundation models (VLMs), which interpret the page rather than match pixel templates, close this gap. On a benchmark of 61 anonymised exams (3141 answer positions) the best model reaches 98.4% accuracy, well above the previous baseline. Crucially, we centre the evaluation on fairness: we distinguish false negatives (a correct answer marked wrong, which disadvantages the student) from false positives, and a lightweight prompt that supplies the reference solution as context lowers the false-negative rate to 0.58%. Under an exemplary grading scheme only three of the 61 exams would be graded worse, all caught by a student self-review step. Fully automated, fairness-aware exam grading at scale is therefore defensible; we release the anonymised benchmark to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that vision-language models (VLMs) can accurately and fairly read handwritten answers from paper-based exams, achieving 98.4% accuracy and a false-negative rate of 0.58%, making unsupervised grading defensible.
AI.motivation: Hand-correcting handwritten exams is time-consuming and error-prone for large cohorts, while fully digital exams force a narrow focus on closed question formats. A practical hybrid approach records answers as machine-readable capital letters, but earlier automated methods (88-91% accuracy) were too inaccurate and unfair for unsupervised grading.
AI.method: The authors use general-purpose vision-language foundation models (VLMs) that interpret the whole page instead of matching pixel templates. They evaluate on 61 anonymised exams (3141 answer positions), use a lightweight prompt with the reference solution as context, and center evaluation on fairness by distinguishing false negatives from false positives.
AI.result: Best model achieves 98.4% accuracy (well above previous 88-91% baseline). False-negative rate is reduced to 0.58% using context-aware prompting. Under an exemplary grading scheme, only 3 of 61 exams would be graded worse, all catchable by student self-review.
AI.conclusion: Fully automated, fairness-aware exam grading at scale is defensible with modern VLMs. The anonymised benchmark is released to support reproducibility.

title: On the Study of Biometric Spoofing Detection using Deep Learning
summary: Biometric systems are increasingly deployed in security applications; however, they remain vulnerable to spoofing attacks, in which attackers exploit counterfeit biometric data to gain unauthorized access. This research evaluates the effectiveness of state-of-the-art machine learning models, MobileNetV2, DenseNet-121, Inception-v3, and Spoof Trace Disentanglement (STD) in detecting spoofing attacks within facial recognition systems. Using the CelebA-Spoof dataset, the study evaluates model effectiveness using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 Score. Cross-dataset validation is carried out on the MSU-MFSD dataset to assess generalizability. The results show MobileNetV2 as the most efficient model, achieving 92% accuracy while balancing computational effectiveness, making it appropriate for real-life applications. Inception-v3 shows moderate robustness, while DenseNet-121 and STD struggle with generalization. The findings highlight the need for advances in domain adaptation and hybrid architectures to enhance biometric security systems.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates multiple deep learning models (MobileNetV2, DenseNet-121, Inception-v3, STD) for detecting facial spoofing attacks, finding MobileNetV2 achieves the best balance of accuracy (92%) and computational efficiency, while other models struggle with generalization.
AI.motivation: Biometric systems are vulnerable to spoofing attacks using counterfeit biometric data, necessitating effective detection methods for security applications.
AI.method: Evaluated MobileNetV2, DenseNet-121, Inception-v3, and Spoof Trace Disentanglement (STD) on the CelebA-Spoof dataset using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 Score. Cross-dataset validation was performed on MSU-MFSD to test generalizability.
AI.result: MobileNetV2 achieved 92% accuracy with the best computational efficiency. Inception-v3 showed moderate robustness. DenseNet-121 and STD struggled with generalization across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Advances in domain adaptation and hybrid architectures are needed to improve biometric security systems against spoofing attacks.

title: SceneMiner: Identity-Preserving Multi-Task Fine-Tuning for Unified BEV Scene Mining
summary: Mining hard, safety-critical scenes from driving logs is bottlenecked by the absence of difficulty labels, and no single proxy, collision risk, trajectory ambiguity, or semantic rarity suffices to find such scenes on its own. We present SceneMiner, a unified, camera-only bird's-eye-view pipeline that emits complementary mining signals from a frozen vision-language backbone in a single forward pass, with no LiDAR or radar: a retrieval embedding for text-prompted scenario search, a multi-label scene-tag distribution, and a continuous physics-based risk score (a motion forecast is a byproduct, not a contribution). Building such a multi-head model exposes our central finding, a failure mode we term cross-task interference: adding or upgrading one head shifts a shared activation stream and degrades weight-frozen sibling heads, so freezing parameters alone is insufficient. Our contribution, identity-preserving multi-task fine-tuning, removes this interference by zero-initializing every new sub-module and freezing every parameter that feeds the shared stream. The mining heads are thereby preserved bit-identically while training only ~102k parameters. The tagging head reaches mAP 0.4614 (micro-F1 0.5557) on 20 scene tags by pooling each scene into 32 visual tokens, and the embedding head supports text-prompted retrieval, validated qualitatively. Code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/sceneminer_anonymous-64E5

AI.tldr: SceneMiner is a unified, camera-only pipeline that mines hard safety-critical driving scenes using a frozen vision-language model with multiple heads (retrieval, tagging, risk scoring), and introduces identity-preserving multi-task fine-tuning to solve cross-task interference where training one head degrades frozen sibling heads.
AI.motivation: Mining hard, safety-critical scenes from driving logs is bottlenecked by the absence of difficulty labels, and no single proxy (collision risk, trajectory ambiguity, or semantic rarity) suffices on its own.
AI.method: A camera-only BEV pipeline with a frozen vision-language backbone emitting three complementary signals in one forward pass: retrieval embedding for text-prompted search, multi-label scene-tag distribution, and continuous physics-based risk score. Cross-task interference is solved by identity-preserving multi-task fine-tuning: zero-initializing every new sub-module and freezing all parameters feeding the shared stream.
AI.result: Tagging head achieves mAP 0.4614 (micro-F1 0.5557) on 20 scene tags by pooling each scene into 32 visual tokens; embedding head supports text-prompted retrieval validated qualitatively; only ~102k parameters trained.
AI.conclusion: Freezing parameters alone is insufficient for multi-head models due to cross-task interference; identity-preserving multi-task fine-tuning preserves sibling heads bit-identically while training minimal new parameters.

title: VL-DINO: Leveraging CLIP Vision-Language Knowledge for Open-Vocabulary Object Detectio
summary: Vision-language models like CLIP can provide rich semantic priors for open-vocabulary object detection. However, jointly integrating both textual and visual knowledge into detection architectures remains challenging. In this paper, we propose VL-DINO, an open-vocabulary detector that enhances DINO through more effective exploitation of CLIP's vision-language knowledge. Specifically, a Query-guided Positive Sample Construction (QPSC) module is first developed to construct additional high-quality positive samples, enabling the vanilla DINO framework to better accommodate mixed training across heterogeneous data sources while providing more vision-language alignment signals, thereby incorporating richer textual knowledge during training. A Visual Semantic Encoder (VSE) module is then introduced to distill CLIP visual knowledge into backbone-extracted features, producing fused features for subsequent encoder refinement. Based on the fused features, an Object-Region Semantic Alignment (ORSA) module extracts object-centric region features and aligns them with the corresponding textual embeddings, further incorporating textual cues. In the zero-shot setting, VL-DINO-T and VL-DINO-L achieve 36.3 and 38.1 AP on the LVIS benchmark, respectively, consistently outperforming prior advanced approaches. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and competitive performance of the proposed design.

AI.tldr: VL-DINO enhances DINO with CLIP's vision-language knowledge for open-vocabulary object detection via three modules (QPSC, VSE, ORSA), achieving 36.3/38.1 AP on LVIS benchmark.
AI.motivation: CLIP provides rich semantic priors for open-vocabulary detection, but jointly integrating both textual and visual knowledge into detection architectures remains challenging.
AI.method: 1) QPSC module constructs high-quality positive samples for mixed training and richer vision-language alignment. 2) VSE module distills CLIP visual knowledge into backbone features. 3) ORSA module extracts object-centric region features and aligns them with textual embeddings.
AI.result: VL-DINO-T and VL-DINO-L achieve 36.3 and 38.1 AP on LVIS benchmark in zero-shot setting, outperforming prior methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed modules effectively exploit CLIP's vision-language knowledge, demonstrating competitive open-vocabulary detection performance.

title: Cross-Modal Benchmarking for Robotic Perception in Natural Environments
comment: Accepted to the IEEE ICRA Workshop on Open Challenges for Rigorous Robot Perception 2026
summary: Natural environments present a complex challenge to robotics perception systems. Current models, particularly vision foundation models, are largely trained on structured, urban environments leading to weaknesses in their perception for field robotics tasks. We showcase the limitations of current models using our recently released WildCross benchmark, a new cross-modal benchmark for place recognition and metric depth estimation in large-scale natural environments. WildCross comprises over 476K sequential RGB frames with semi-dense depth and surface normal annotations, each aligned with accurate 6DoF pose and synchronized dense lidar submaps. In this work, we provide an expanded analysis of the benchmark results from the recent WildCross benchmark, with particular emphasis on expanded metric depth estimation experiments. Access to the code repository and dataset for this work can be found at https://csiro-robotics.github.io/WildCross.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WildCross, a benchmark for place recognition and metric depth estimation in natural environments, analyzing limitations of vision foundation models trained on structured urban scenes, with expanded depth estimation experiments.
AI.motivation: Current vision foundation models are trained on structured urban environments, causing poor performance in field robotics tasks in natural environments. The authors aim to expose these weaknesses through a dedicated benchmark.
AI.method: The WildCross benchmark contains 476K+ sequential RGB frames with semi-dense depth and surface normal annotations, aligned with accurate 6DoF poses and synchronized dense LiDAR submaps. Expanded metric depth estimation experiments are conducted.
AI.result: Demonstrates limitations of existing models on natural environment perception tasks, particularly in cross-modal place recognition and metric depth estimation.
AI.conclusion: Vision foundation models trained on urban data underperform in unstructured natural environments, highlighting the need for domain-specific benchmarks like WildCross.

title: 4DP-QA: Scalable QA for 4D Perception in Vision Language Models
summary: Despite recent advances, Vision Language Models (VLMs) still struggle to grasp the dynamics of the world. We note that the ability to reason about a 4D scene, challenging in itself, is further complicated by two factors. First, VLMs observe motion indirectly via its projection onto 2D images. Second, existing datasets fail to disentangle object and camera motion. To address these challenges, we present a QA generation pipeline that focuses on motion-related scene understanding. We take particular care of the entanglement of camera and object motion by casting tracking in both the traditional way and in a novel, fixed reference system, dubbed True-Motion Tracking, which provides an intuitive description of motion. From this pipeline, we generate a large-scale training dataset of 400K samples, 4DP-QA (4D Perception QA), and a 2.2K-sample benchmark, 4DP-QA-Bench. Training existing models on our dataset yields performance improvements on an external benchmark, validating the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: VLMs have limited understanding of 4D motion dynamics. The authors tackle this by creating 4DP-QA, a 400K-sample training dataset and a 2.2K-sample benchmark (4DP-QA-Bench) using a QA generation pipeline with a novel True-Motion Tracking method that disentangles camera and object motion. Training on this dataset improves performance on external benchmarks.
AI.motivation: VLMs struggle to reason about 4D scenes because (1) motion is only observed via 2D projections, and (2) existing datasets fail to separate object motion from camera motion.
AI.method: A QA generation pipeline focused on motion understanding, featuring True-Motion Tracking—a novel fixed reference system that disentangles camera and object motion—to produce the 4DP-QA dataset (400K samples) and 4DP-QA-Bench (2.2K samples).
AI.result: Training existing VLMs on 4DP-QA yields performance improvements on an external benchmark, validating the approach's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset and benchmark effectively improve VLMs' 4D motion reasoning capabilities by disentangling camera and object motion.

title: FreqKD: Frequency-Decoupled Cross-Modal Knowledge Distillation for Infrared Object Detection
summary: Transfer learning from large-scale RGB foundation models to infrared (IR) imagery through knowledge distillation (KD) remains challenging due to fundamental differences in image formation physics. We investigate the spectral structure of the RGB--IR modality gap and observe that feature divergence is not uniform across spatial frequencies: low-frequency components (shape, layout) show greater cross-modal alignment than high-frequency components (texture, fine edges), which reflect modality-specific characteristics. Based on this analysis, we propose FreqKD, a frequency-decoupled distillation framework that applies asymmetric supervision adapted to each band's cross-modal consistency. The method employs strict mean squared error (MSE) on the low-frequency band to preserve shared structural information and a relaxed log-MSE loss (weighted at 0.1) on the high-frequency band to provide edge guidance while tolerating texture differences. Spectral divergence analysis on 500 paired samples shows that high-frequency divergence exceeds low-frequency divergence by a factor of 2.4x on average across all analysed transformer layers. On KAIST multispectral pedestrian detection, FreqKD achieves 64.1 mAP50, improving 2.4 points over the DINOv2 baseline. The learned representation transfers across datasets (FLIR ADAS, +2.1 mAP50), tasks (MFNet segmentation, +1.85 mean intersection-over-union), and architectures (ResNet-50, +1.0 mAP50). Code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/freq_decoupled_kd-5E5A

AI.tldr: FreqKD is a frequency-decoupled knowledge distillation framework that applies asymmetric supervision (strict MSE on low frequencies, relaxed log-MSE on high frequencies) to improve transfer learning from RGB foundation models to infrared imagery, achieving significant gains on detection, segmentation, and cross-dataset tasks.
AI.motivation: Transfer learning from RGB foundation models to infrared (IR) imagery via knowledge distillation is challenging because of fundamental differences in image formation physics; the authors aim to understand and exploit the spectral structure of the RGB-IR modality gap to improve KD performance.
AI.method: The authors first analyze the RGB-IR modality gap across spatial frequencies, finding low-frequency components align better cross-modally than high-frequency ones. They then propose FreqKD, which decouples features into low and high frequencies via spectral decomposition, applying strict MSE loss on low-frequency bands (preserving shared structure) and relaxed log-MSE loss (weighted 0.1) on high-frequency bands (providing edge guidance while tolerating texture differences).
AI.result: KAIST pedestrian detection: 64.1 mAP50 (+2.4 over DINOv2 baseline). FLIR ADAS: +2.1 mAP50. MFNet segmentation: +1.85 mIoU. ResNet-50: +1.0 mAP50. Spectral divergence analysis confirms high-frequency divergence exceeds low-frequency by 2.4x on average across transformer layers.
AI.conclusion: Frequency-decoupled distillation effectively addresses the RGB-IR modality gap by treating different spatial frequencies differently, leading to robust cross-modal representations that transfer across datasets, tasks, and architectures.

title: Understanding Cross-Sensor Feature Variations for Generalizable 3D Perception
summary: Radar-camera BEV perception often suffers from degraded performance when evaluated across datasets, as changes in driving scenes, sensor configurations, and environmental conditions can alter both the input observations and the internal fused representations. This work studies this issue from the perspective of source-domain variation modeling, aiming to improve the robustness of BEV-based 3D detectors without relying on target-domain samples. We introduce a framework that characterizes visual scene variations in the frequency domain and uses them to synthesize diverse source-domain views. By comparing the resulting fused BEV representations, the framework further captures how image-level variations influence multi-modal BEV features. These variation patterns are then used to regularize the detector, encouraging the learned fusion space to remain stable under latent scene changes. The proposed method is applied only during training and leaves the inference pipeline unchanged. Experiments on cross-dataset radar-camera 3D detection between View-of-Delft and TJ4DRadSet demonstrate consistent improvements over multiple BEV fusion backbones, and the gains remain effective when a small amount of target-domain data is available.

AI.tldr: This paper improves cross-dataset robustness of radar-camera BEV 3D detectors by modeling source-domain variations in the frequency domain and using them to regularize the fusion space, achieving consistent gains without requiring target-domain data.
AI.motivation: Radar-camera BEV perception suffers performance degradation across datasets due to changes in scenes, sensor setups, and environments, and existing methods lack robustness to these variations without target-domain samples.
AI.method: The framework characterizes visual scene variations in the frequency domain, synthesizes diverse source-domain views, compares fused BEV representations to capture variation patterns, and uses these patterns to regularize the detector for stable fusion under latent scene changes.
AI.result: Consistent improvements on cross-dataset radar-camera 3D detection between View-of-Delft and TJ4DRadSet across multiple BEV fusion backbones, with gains persisting even with limited target-domain data.
AI.conclusion: Source-domain variation modeling via frequency-domain analysis effectively enhances BEV detector robustness, is training-only (no inference overhead), and generalizes to low-target-data scenarios.

title: AVIS: Adaptive Test-Time Scaling for Vision-Language Models
summary: Modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) benefit from chain-of-thought prompting and test-time scaling, but these gains often come with prohibitive inference cost due to large visual contexts and long decoding chains. We view this cost through two coupled axes: Visual Context Scaling (VCS), which controls how much visual evidence is passed to the language model, and Visual Reasoning Scaling (VRS), which controls how much inference-time reasoning search is performed. Existing methods typically optimize one axis at a time, leaving the joint allocation of compute across these axes underexplored. We introduce Adaptive Visual Inference Scaling (AVIS), a lightweight policy that adapts both VCS and VRS per query. AVIS realizes VCS through Key Diversity Visual (KDV) pruning, a training-free $O(N)$ key-based rule for removing redundant visual tokens before prefilling, and realizes VRS through adaptive self-consistency, using a learned difficulty predictor to select the number of reasoning rollouts. AVIS is deployment-friendly and compatible with shared-prefill inference, where all rollouts reuse a single prefilling pass and KV cache. Across diverse image and video reasoning benchmarks, AVIS improves the accuracy--compute trade-off relative to VCS-only and VRS-only baselines, and remains effective on top of RL post-trained VLMs while keeping compute and latency low.

AI.tldr: AVIS adaptively scales both visual context (via Key Diversity Visual pruning) and visual reasoning (via adaptive self-consistency) per query, improving the accuracy-compute trade-off across image/video benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs waste compute by optimizing visual context scaling (VCS) and visual reasoning scaling (VRS) independently, overlooking joint allocation of compute across both axes.
AI.method: AVIS uses (1) KDV pruning: a training-free O(N) rule to remove redundant visual tokens before prefilling, and (2) adaptive self-consistency: a learned difficulty predictor selects the number of reasoning rollouts. Compatible with shared-prefill inference.
AI.result: AVIS improves accuracy-compute trade-offs over VCS-only and VRS-only baselines across diverse image and video reasoning benchmarks, and remains effective on RL post-trained VLMs with low latency.
AI.conclusion: Jointly adapting both visual context and reasoning scaling per query is more effective than optimizing either axis alone, offering a deployment-friendly solution for efficient VLM inference.

title: Contactless 3D Human Body Measurement Using Depth Cameras for Smart Health Monitoring
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures. Depth camera-based framework for contactless anthropometric measurement and geometric analysis using 3D point clouds
summary: Contactless body measurement technologies are becoming increasingly significant for smart health monitoring, digital health applications, and remote patient assessment. Traditional anthropometric measurements typically necessitate physical contact and trained personnel, which may constrain scalability in remote healthcare settings. In this study, we introduce a depth camera-based framework for estimating human body measurements utilizing 3D point cloud data. An Orbbec Astra 2 depth camera was employed to capture RGB images, depth maps, and 3D point clouds of participants. The captured point cloud was processed using Python-based tools, including Open3D, NumPy, and OpenCV, to segment the human body from the background. Key anthropometric measurements, such as height and arm span, were computed. The measurements were obtained through a combination of spatial filtering and landmark selection on the 3D point cloud, followed by the projection of the computed measurements onto the corresponding RGB image using camera intrinsic parameters. In addition to linear measurements, the approximate body volume and visible surface area were estimated using voxel-based occupancy analysis and mesh-based surface reconstruction methods. The experimental results from a single depth capture demonstrated that accurate body measurements and geometric estimates could be obtained from depth camera data without physical contact. This study provides a foundation for future real-time systems that integrate depth sensing with intelligent health monitoring and generative AI models for smart healthcare applications.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a depth camera-based framework (using Orbbec Astra 2) for contactless human body measurement from 3D point cloud data, achieving accurate anthropometric estimates (height, arm span, volume, surface area) and projecting results onto RGB images.
AI.motivation: Traditional anthropometric measurements require physical contact and trained personnel, limiting scalability for remote healthcare and smart health monitoring applications.
AI.method: An Orbbec Astra 2 depth camera captures RGB, depth, and 3D point cloud data. The point cloud is processed with Python tools (Open3D, NumPy, OpenCV) for body segmentation via spatial filtering and landmark selection. Linear measurements (height, arm span) are computed, and body volume/surface area are estimated using voxel-based occupancy and mesh-based reconstruction. Results are projected onto RGB images using camera intrinsic parameters.
AI.result: Experiments with single depth capture demonstrated that accurate body measurements and geometric estimates can be obtained from depth camera data without physical contact.
AI.conclusion: This study provides a foundation for future real-time systems integrating depth sensing with intelligent health monitoring and generative AI models for smart healthcare applications.

title: Spatially Coupled Phase-to-Depth Calibration for Fringe Projection Profilometry
summary: In fringe projection profilometry (FPP), depth is commonly recovered by fitting a phase-to-depth relation independently at each camera pixel. Although such pixel-wise calibration achieves high local accuracy, neighboring pixels can acquire markedly different calibration functions even when they observe the same smooth surface, producing spatially inconsistent geometry and structured surface artifacts. We propose a spatially coupled phase-depth transformation in which all pixels share a single low-dimensional mapping-global phase scalars combined with affine spatial terms on the undistorted reference-camera grid-rather than independent per-pixel fits, optionally augmented by a bounded, spatially smooth correction field. We further introduce a native-grid pairing scheme that constructs phase-depth calibration pairs directly on the reference-camera grid: when depth supervision comes from a rectified active-stereo pipeline, planes are fitted in stereo 3D and sampled back onto the camera grid along native rays, so the phase maps are never rectified. On a dental target with high-resolution scanner ground truth, the proposed model attains point-to-surface RMSE comparable to an active-stereo reference (about 12μm aggregate) while substantially improving spatial coherence over pixel-wise polynomial and rational calibration, and reduces the runtime mapping to a few element-wise operations per pixel with negligible parameter storage.

AI.tldr: Proposes a spatially coupled phase-to-depth transformation for fringe projection profilometry that uses a shared low-dimensional mapping across all pixels, improving spatial coherence and reducing artifacts compared to per-pixel calibration.
AI.motivation: Traditional pixel-wise calibration in FPP produces spatially inconsistent geometry and surface artifacts because neighboring pixels can learn markedly different calibration functions even when observing the same smooth surface.
AI.method: A spatially coupled phase-depth transformation using global phase scalars combined with affine spatial terms on the reference-camera grid, optionally augmented by a smooth correction field. Also introduces a native-grid pairing scheme for constructing calibration pairs without rectifying phase maps.
AI.result: Achieves ~12μm point-to-surface RMSE on a dental target, comparable to active-stereo reference, with improved spatial coherence over pixel-wise polynomial/rational calibration and reduced runtime to few element-wise operations per pixel.
AI.conclusion: The spatially coupled model effectively eliminates surface artifacts from per-pixel calibration while maintaining high accuracy and computational efficiency.

title: On Aligning Hierarchical Standardized Embedding for Audio-visual Generalized Zero-shot Learning
summary: Audio-visual Generalized Zero-shot Learning (AV-GZSL) is a challenging task that aims to classify both seen and unseen objects or scenes by integrating data from audio and visual modalities. Recent studies primarily focus on fusing or aligning audio and visual features to generate more informative audio-visual embeddings. Also, aligning the audio-visual and textual features of most existing methods relies solely on the optimization objectives. However, those methods neglect the inherent distributional and structural differences between audio-visual and textual modalities. To address this limitation, we propose a method termed Aligning Hierarchical Standardized Embedding (AHSE), which enables hierarchical alignment of standardized audio-visual and textual embeddings within a shared embedding space. Specifically, we first apply Z-score standardization to the fused audio-visual and textual embeddings to reduce distributional mismatches. We then introduce a hierarchical alignment strategy that minimizes discrepancies at the semantic, class, and batch levels, thereby constructing a more robust and well-structured embedding space. This strategy not only preserves semantic and inter-class relationships but also maintains spatial consistency within each batch. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets: VGGSound-GZSL, UCF-GZSL, and ActivityNet-GZSL, demonstrate that AHSE achieves competitive performance in zero-shot learning.

AI.tldr: AHSE proposes hierarchical alignment via Z-score standardization to reduce distributional differences between audio-visual and textual modalities for AV-GZSL, achieving competitive results on three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing AV-GZSL methods neglect inherent distributional and structural differences between audio-visual and textual modalities, relying solely on optimization objectives for alignment.
AI.method: (1) Z-score standardization on fused audio-visual and textual embeddings to reduce distributional mismatches. (2) Hierarchical alignment strategy at semantic, class, and batch levels to construct robust embedding space.
AI.result: Competitive zero-shot learning performance on VGGSound-GZSL, UCF-GZSL, and ActivityNet-GZSL benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical standardized embedding alignment effectively addresses cross-modality distributional gaps and improves AV-GZSL performance.

title: Frozen Foundation-Model Embeddings Discard Small-Lesion Signal in Chest Radiography: Implications for Pre-Deployment Evaluation
summary: Frozen vision-transformer (ViT) foundation-model embeddings increasingly serve as the substrate for downstream chest-radiography (CXR) pipelines, yet where small-scale, low-contrast signal is retained or lost in the frozen forward pass has not been systematically quantified across architectures, pretraining domains, and objectives. We probed five frozen ViTs (RAD-DINO, DINOv2-B/14, DINOv3 ViT-7B, BiomedCLIP, MedSigLIP) and a frozen DINO-pretrained ResNet-50 architectural control across three large CXR cohorts (NIH-CXR14, MIMIC-CXR, Emory-CXR; aggregate pool n=492,724) and ChestX-Det10 (n=3,543; 1,462 small-lesion bounding boxes across Calcification, Nodule, Mass). Each model was evaluated with a small-scale-perturbation panel and a region-aware bounding-box-stratified probe on real lesions, comparing three pooling modes from the same forward pass: classification token (CLS), patch-mean (mean over all final-layer patch tokens), and bounding-box-restricted patch-local. On the perturbation panel, CLS embeddings sat at the chance floor (area under the ROC curve [AUC] 0.500-0.524); patch-mean was indistinguishable from CLS on iso-blur and reticular-fine cells but rose with CLS on larger directional-blur footprints, while disease AUC on globally decided tasks ranged 0.642-0.913.
