Patch-local probes recovered AUC ~1.0 from the same forward pass (per-model mean improvement +0.412 to +0.488); the ResNet-50 control reproduced the chance floor. On ChestX-Det10, image-level CLS classification showed within-class small-versus-large stratum gaps up to +0.243 AUC; bounding-box-level patch-local pooling on the same forward pass recovered AUC >= 0.899 on every (model x class) cell. Frozen ViT embeddings silently suppress small-scale signal at the global-aggregation step; the signal is recoverable from patch tokens conditional on a region of interest.

AI.tldr: Frozen ViT embeddings lose small-scale, low-contrast signal (e.g., small lesions in chest X-rays) at the global-aggregation step (CLS or patch-mean pooling), but this signal is recoverable from patch tokens when conditioned on a region of interest, with patch-local probes achieving near-perfect AUC across models and datasets.
AI.motivation: To systematically quantify where and how small-scale, low-contrast diagnostic signals (e.g., small lung lesions) are retained or lost in frozen ViT forward passes across different architectures, pretraining domains, and objectives.
AI.method: Probed five frozen ViTs and a ResNet-50 control across three large CXR cohorts and ChestX-Det10; used a perturbation panel, bounding-box-stratified probes on real lesions, and compared three pooling modes: CLS token, patch-mean, and bounding-box-restricted patch-local.
AI.result: CLS and patch-mean embeddings performed at chance level for small-scale signals (AUC 0.500-0.524), while patch-local probes recovered AUC ~1.0 (improvement +0.412 to +0.488). Image-level CLS showed large small-vs-large stratum gaps (up to +0.243 AUC), but patch-local pooling achieved AUC ≥0.899 on all model-class combinations.
AI.conclusion: Frozen ViT embeddings silently suppress small-scale signal at global aggregation, but the signal is recoverable from patch tokens given a region of interest.

title: Adv-TGD: Adversarial Text-Guided Diffusion for Face Recognition Impersonation Attacks
summary: The widespread adoption of face recognition (FR) technologies raises serious privacy concerns, as facial data can be exploited without consent. To address this challenge, we propose Adv-TGD, a generative adversarial attack framework that synthesizes photorealistic faces capable of impersonating target identities and deceiving face recognition systems. Built upon Stable Diffusion, Adv-TGD performs per-sample LoRA fine-tuning conditioned on concise textual prompts to generate natural yet adversarially manipulated identities. Unlike conventional identity-attack approaches, our method optimizes lightweight cross-attention adapters for each source-target pair within a single-step denoising process. Latent blending is constrained by a face-local heatmap mask to ensure spatially precise identity manipulation while preserving non-sensitive regions. We introduce a composite objective that integrates masked epsilon-MSE reconstruction, thresholded identity divergence in FR embedding space, directional feature alignment, and source-similarity suppression to balance adversarial attack and visual realism. Optionally, LLaVA-generated attribute prompts enhance fine-grained semantic details without reintroducing identity cues. Under the black-box evaluation protocol, Adv-TGD attains an average attack success rate (ASR) of 85.90% across IR152, IRSE50, MobileFace, and FaceNet, surpassing the semantic SOTA baseline Adv-CPG by +6.25 points, diffusion-based makeup method DiffAIM by +3 points, and noise-based P3-Mask by +16 points. Despite its strong attack efficacy, Adv-TGD preserves high visual fidelity (PSNR = 27.15 dB, SSIM = 0.981). Furthermore, we demonstrate the flexibility of our framework by successfully extending it to in-the-wild datasets (LADN), general object classification (ImageNet), and transformer-based diffusion models (FLUX.1).

AI.tldr: Adv-TGD is a generative adversarial attack framework using Stable Diffusion to create photorealistic faces that impersonate target identities, achieving 85.90% attack success rate across face recognition systems while maintaining high visual fidelity (PSNR=27.15, SSIM=0.981).
AI.motivation: Face recognition technologies raise serious privacy concerns due to potential exploitation of facial data without consent, motivating the need for effective adversarial attacks that can protect identity privacy.
AI.method: Adv-TGD performs per-sample LoRA fine-tuning on Stable Diffusion with textual prompts, optimizes lightweight cross-attention adapters in a single-step denoising process, uses latent blending constrained by face-local heatmap masks, and employs a composite objective combining masked epsilon-MSE reconstruction, thresholded identity divergence, directional feature alignment, and source-similarity suppression. Optionally uses LLaVA for attribute prompts.
AI.result: 85.90% average ASR across IR152, IRSE50, MobileFace, and FaceNet; outperforms Adv-CPG by +6.25 points, DiffAIM by +3 points, and P3-Mask by +16 points; maintains high visual fidelity (PSNR=27.15 dB, SSIM=0.981); successfully extends to in-the-wild datasets, general object classification, and transformer-based diffusion models.
AI.conclusion: Adv-TGD effectively balances adversarial attack strength with visual realism, demonstrating strong performance and flexibility across diverse datasets and model architectures for privacy protection.

title: Precision-Aware Illumination-Disentangled Vision Transformer for Spacecraft 6D Pose Estimation
summary: Vision sensors provide a lightweight solution for spacecraft proximity operations, but monocular spacecraft 6D pose estimation remains difficult under illumination variation, specular reflection, shadowing, weak texture, and background interference. These factors make local visual evidence spatially unreliable and can destabilize pose regression. This article proposes a Precision-Aware Illumination-Disentangled Vision Transformer (PAID-ViT) for robust spacecraft pose estimation.The proposed model separates pose-relevant structure tokens from illumination-sensitive appearance tokens, estimates patch reliability before pose aggregation, and uses foreground mask supervision to preserve silhouette cues. A parameter-free geometric recovery module converts normalized crop coordinates, log-depth, and a continuous 6D rotation representation into camera-frame rotation and translation. Experiments on SPEED+ V2, the SPEED+ validation/lightbox/sunlamp evaluation configuration used in this study, suggest that PAID-ViT reduces translation error and improves robustness in the challenging sunlamp domain, while ablation studies support the complementary roles of illumination disentanglement, reliability-aware token aggregation, mask supervision, and training-side regularization.

AI.tldr: PAID-ViT is a Vision Transformer for spacecraft pose estimation that disentangles illumination-sensitive appearance tokens from pose-relevant structure tokens, estimates patch reliability before aggregation, and uses foreground mask supervision, achieving improved robustness under challenging lighting conditions on SPEED+ V2 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Monocular spacecraft 6D pose estimation is unreliable under illumination variation, specular reflection, shadowing, weak texture, and background interference, as local visual evidence becomes spatially unstable.
AI.method: PAID-ViT separates pose-relevant structure tokens from illumination-sensitive appearance tokens, estimates patch reliability before pose aggregation, applies foreground mask supervision for silhouette preservation, and uses a parameter-free geometric recovery module converting normalized crop coordinates, log-depth, and continuous 6D rotation to camera-frame pose.
AI.result: On SPEED+ V2 (validation/lightbox/sunlamp configurations), PAID-ViT reduces translation error and improves robustness in the challenging sunlamp domain.
AI.conclusion: Illumination disentanglement, reliability-aware token aggregation, mask supervision, and training-side regularization play complementary roles in improving robustness.

title: Adapting Vision-Language Models from Iconic to Inclusive for Multi-Label Recognition Without Labels
summary: Understanding multi-label images remains a challenging task in computer vision. With the rapid progress of vision-language multimodal learning, vision-language models (VLMs) enable zero-shot recognition without labeled data. However, due to their intrinsic design, these models often prioritize the most iconic object and omit other contextual positives. This intrinsic bias conflicts with the nature of multi-label learning, thereby limiting their applicability. In this work, we propose an unsupervised framework that adapts VLMs from iconic recognition toward inclusive understanding, enabling label-free multi-label image recognition. Our approach consists of two key stages, ``cutting'' and ``sewing'': In the cutting stage, we present the multi-sampling response estimator to prevent the model from concentrating only on one single object. In the second sewing stage, the multi-object blend adaptation is introduced to adjust the labels to better conform to the multi-label distribution while preserving the intrinsic characteristics of the original model within only one epoch. Extensive experiments show that our framework significantly outperforms existing unsupervised approaches on four public datasets, even surpassing several representative weakly supervised baselines. These results demonstrate the potential of adapting pre-trained VLMs for more comprehensive visual understanding without manual annotations. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/iCVTEAM/TailorCLIP.

AI.tldr: TailorCLIP proposes an unsupervised framework (cutting & sewing) to adapt VLMs from iconic to inclusive multi-label recognition, outperforming existing unsupervised and even weakly-supervised methods on four datasets.
AI.motivation: VLMs inherently prioritize the most iconic object in an image and ignore other contextual positives, which limits their applicability for multi-label image recognition where multiple objects co-exist.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) Cutting stage - Multi-sampling Response Estimator prevents VLM from focusing on a single object; (2) Sewing stage - Multi-object Blend Adaptation adjusts labels to fit multi-label distribution while preserving original model characteristics, all within one epoch.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms existing unsupervised approaches on four public datasets, and even surpasses several representative weakly supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: Adapting pre-trained VLMs for more comprehensive visual understanding without manual annotations is feasible and effective, demonstrating strong potential for label-free multi-label recognition.

title: Motion Reinforces Appearance: RGB-Skeleton Gated Residual Fusion for Micro-Gesture Online Recognition
summary: Micro-gesture analysis attracts increasing attention for inferring spontaneous emotion from subtle body movements. Micro-gesture online recognition, which localizes and classifies each gesture instance in untrimmed videos, is a core task in the 4th EI-MiGA-IJCAI Challenge. Compared with typical temporal action detection, MGR emphasizes the localization and classification of actions, requiring the model to output the start time, end time, and category of each micro-gesture. Moreover, since micro-gestures are highly spontaneous, relying solely on a single modality makes it difficult to capture the complete and accurate multi-modal cues. In this work, we propose DyFADet+, which extends DyFADet into a dual-stream RGB-skeleton framework. In our model, both modalities are projected into shared multi-scale temporal embeddings and fused through a gated residual module, which adaptively injects skeleton motion into the RGB representation rather than using naive concatenation. Finally, these fused features are decoded by a Dynamic TAD head for online classification and boundary regression. On the SMG dataset, our method achieves an F1 score of 40.88, ranking 2nd in the Micro-gesture Online Recognition track.

AI.tldr: DyFADet+ extends DyFADet into a dual-stream RGB-skeleton framework for micro-gesture online recognition, using a gated residual module to fuse modalities and a Dynamic TAD head for classification and boundary regression, achieving 40.88 F1 score (2nd place) on the SMG dataset.
AI.motivation: Micro-gesture online recognition is challenging because micro-gestures are highly spontaneous and rely on subtle body movements; single-modality approaches fail to capture complete multi-modal cues needed for accurate localization and classification in untrimmed videos.
AI.method: DyFADet+ uses a dual-stream RGB-skeleton framework: both modalities are projected into shared multi-scale temporal embeddings, fused via a gated residual module (adaptively injecting skeleton motion into RGB representation), then decoded by a Dynamic TAD head for online classification and boundary regression.
AI.result: On the SMG dataset, DyFADet+ achieves an F1 score of 40.88, ranking 2nd in the Micro-gesture Online Recognition track of the 4th EI-MiGA-IJCAI Challenge.
AI.conclusion: The gated residual fusion of RGB and skeleton modalities is effective for micro-gesture online recognition, outperforming naive concatenation, as demonstrated by the 2nd-place performance in the challenge.

title: Learning Instance-Adaptive Low-Rank Orthogonal Subspaces for Clothes-Changing Person Re-Identification
comment: Accepted to the ICML 2026 Workshop on CoLoRAI
summary: Clothes-changing person re-identification (CC-ReID) aims to recognize individuals despite drastic appearance changes caused by clothing variation. While existing methods rely on adversarial learning to disentangle clothing features, we propose Ortho-ReID, which explicitly models a low-rank clothing subspace from VLM text descriptions and extracts clothing-invariant representations via direct geometric constraints. A critical component is our transformer-based Basis Maker, which refines a shared, low-dimensional clothing prior into an instance-adaptive low-rank subspace through cross-attention with image patches, enabling robust clothing feature extraction even under varying visibility conditions. This instance-adaptive subspace is supervised via alignment with clothing text embeddings, while identity features are extracted via a learnable projection head and geometrically constrained to be strictly orthogonal to it. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on PRCC (+5.9% top-1), Celeb-reID-light (+3.5%), and LaST (+5.3%), with competitive results on LTCC.

AI.tldr: Ortho-ReID introduces a novel method for clothes-changing person re-identification by modeling a low-rank clothing subspace from VLM text descriptions and enforcing geometric orthogonality between clothing and identity features, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing CC-ReID methods rely on adversarial learning to disentangle clothing features, which can be unstable and ineffective. The authors aim to explicitly model clothing variation using a more principled geometric approach.
AI.method: Proposes a transformer-based Basis Maker that refines a shared clothing prior into an instance-adaptive low-rank subspace via cross-attention with image patches. Identity features are extracted via a learnable projection head and geometrically constrained to be strictly orthogonal to the clothing subspace.
AI.result: Outperforms prior methods on PRCC (+5.9% top-1), Celeb-reID-light (+3.5%), and LaST (+5.3%), with competitive results on LTCC.
AI.conclusion: Direct geometric constraints (orthogonality) are more effective than adversarial disentanglement for CC-ReID, and leveraging VLM text descriptions helps build robust clothing subspaces.

title: ARGUS: Stacked Multi-View Identity Mosaic Injection for Subject-Preserving Video Generation
summary: Subject-preserving video generation is not solved by frontal-face similarity alone: a generated person must remain recognizable across motion, large viewpoint changes, expression shifts, occlusion, scale variation, and conflicts among text, first-frame, and identity references. We argue that the central bottleneck is the point-reference paradigm, which collapses identity into a single static observation entangled with pose, accessories, lighting, background, and camera statistics. We introduce Argus, a Wan-based framework centered on Stacked Multi-View Identity Mosaic Injection (SMII). SMII converts MLLM-selected image/video identity evidence into a 3*3 stacked mosaic, synchronizes the mosaic with the current diffusion time, and injects it as negative-time read-only memory in Wan's native token space. This turns identity from an external clean adapter or a single reference image into a compact dynamic distribution. Around SMII, an MLLM Identity Director selects informative identity moments and resolves condition conflicts, while no-cross-pair counterfactual training, Temporal Identity Annealing, and Adaptive Self-Likeness Guidance improve robustness without paired subject-video supervision. We further release HardID-Celeb, a public-figure identity-stress benchmark, and introduce YawScore and OccScore to probe large-yaw and first-frame-occlusion robustness. Argus achieves state-of-the-art results on OpenS2V-Eval Human-Domain, reaching 64.38 Total Score, 71.86 FaceSim, 51.62 NexusScore, and 79.14 NaturalScore. On HardID-Celeb, Argus obtains 76.80 FaceSim and improves YawScore and OccScore by 12.60 and 15.10 points over the strongest baselines, demonstrating that dynamic identity memory and large-scale counterfactual self-supervision are highly effective for subject-preserving video generation.

AI.tldr: Argus is a Wan-based video generation framework that replaces point-reference identity (single static image) with Stacked Multi-View Identity Mosaic Injection (SMII), turning identity into a dynamic distribution, achieving SOTA on subject-preserving video generation benchmarks including HardID-Celeb.
AI.motivation: Current subject-preserving video generation relies on frontal-face similarity and point-reference paradigms (single static observation), which collapse identity with pose, lighting, accessories, etc., and fail under large viewpoint changes, occlusion, and scale variation. The authors argue this is the central bottleneck.
AI.method: Argus introduces SMII (Stacked Multi-View Identity Mosaic Injection) which converts MLLM-selected identity evidence into a 3x3 stacked mosaic, synchronized with diffusion time and injected as negative-time read-only memory in Wan's token space. Additional components: MLLM Identity Director for selecting informative moments and resolving condition conflicts; no-cross-pair counterfactual training; Temporal Identity Annealing; Adaptive Self-Likeness Guidance.
AI.result: SOTA on OpenS2V-Eval Human-Domain: 64.38 Total Score, 71.86 FaceSim, 51.62 NexusScore, 79.14 NaturalScore. On HardID-Celeb: 76.80 FaceSim, YawScore +12.60, OccScore +15.10 over strongest baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic identity memory and large-scale counterfactual self-supervision are highly effective for subject-preserving video generation, overcoming limitations of static point-reference identity paradigms.

title: Parameter-Efficient Adapter Tuning for Tabular-Image Multimodal Learning
summary: Tabular-image multimodal learning aims to improve predictive modeling by jointly using structured tabular attributes and visual data. Although pretrained encoders provide strong modality-specific representations, full fine-tuning can be computationally expensive, while keeping encoders frozen may limit task-specific adaptation. We propose the Tabular-Image Adapter (TI-Adapter), a modality-specific adapter-based fine-tuning framework for efficient multimodal adaptation. TI-Adapter freezes the pretrained tabular encoder and learns an adapter after the extracted tabular embedding, while adapting the image branch with embedding-level and bottleneck-level adapters instead of full fine-tuning. Experiments on 20 tabular-image datasets show that TI-Adapter achieves competitive or better predictive performance than full fine-tuning while using substantially fewer trainable parameters. Ablation studies further demonstrate the importance of adapter placement for balancing performance and practical efficiency.

AI.tldr: TI-Adapter is an efficient adapter-based fine-tuning framework for tabular-image multimodal learning that freezes the tabular encoder and uses adapters for the image branch, achieving competitive performance with full fine-tuning using fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Full fine-tuning of pretrained encoders in tabular-image multimodal learning is computationally expensive, while keeping encoders frozen limits task-specific adaptation.
AI.method: TI-Adapter freezes the pretrained tabular encoder and adds an adapter after the tabular embedding, while adapting the image branch using embedding-level and bottleneck-level adapters instead of full fine-tuning.
AI.result: On 20 tabular-image datasets, TI-Adapter achieves competitive or better predictive performance than full fine-tuning with substantially fewer trainable parameters.
AI.conclusion: Adapter placement is crucial for balancing performance and practical efficiency in multimodal adaptation.

title: Reason, Then Re-reason: Cross-view Revisiting Improves Spatial Reasoning
summary: Spatial reasoning from egocentric videos is inherently challenging because the observable evidence is constrained by the camera trajectory. Existing methods rely on single-turn inference, forcing models to resolve geometric ambiguity through semantic priors rather than verifiable evidence. We argue that spatial reasoning should be revisitable: conclusions formed under limited evidence should remain open to revision when complementary viewpoints become available. Building on this insight, we propose Reason, then Re-reason (ReRe), a training-free, inference-time framework with two phases: in the Reason Phase, an MLLM forms a spatial hypothesis from the original video; in the Re-reason Phase, it verifies or revises the hypothesis by observing a synthesized novel-view video. To enable effective cross-view revisiting, we design a Geometry-to-Video pipeline that renders strategically complementary novel views from predicted 3D geometry. These views feature an elevated, oblique perspective with scene-spanning coverage, while preserving the MLLM's native video interface without architectural modifications. Extensive evaluations on VSI-Bench and STI-Bench demonstrate that ReRe substantially boosts open-source MLLMs to rival proprietary state-of-the-art performance. Project page: https://zhenjiemao.github.io/ReRe/

AI.tldr: ReRe is a training-free, inference-time framework that improves spatial reasoning in egocentric videos by first forming a hypothesis from the original video, then revising it using synthesized novel-view videos rendered from predicted 3D geometry.
AI.motivation: Spatial reasoning from egocentric videos is limited by camera trajectory constraints, and existing single-turn methods force models to rely on semantic priors rather than verifiable evidence. The authors argue spatial reasoning should be revisitable when new evidence becomes available.
AI.method: A two-phase framework: (1) Reason Phase - MLLM forms a spatial hypothesis from the original video; (2) Re-reason Phase - MLLM verifies or revises the hypothesis by observing a synthesized novel-view video. A Geometry-to-Video pipeline renders strategically complementary novel views from predicted 3D geometry with elevated, oblique perspectives.
AI.result: On VSI-Bench and STI-Bench, ReRe substantially boosts open-source MLLMs to rival proprietary state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: Revisitable spatial reasoning is effective; the training-free inference-time framework significantly improves spatial reasoning capabilities of open-source MLLMs without architectural modifications.

title: DroneShield-AI: A Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion Framework for Real-Time Autonomous Drone Threat Detection, Behavioral Intent Classification, and Swarm Intelligence in Contested Airspace
comment: 23 pages, 6 figures, 11 tables. Code available at https://github.com/bayizeremarius/DroneShield-AI
summary: Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) threats have emerged as a defining security challenge of the 21st century. This paper presents DroneShield-AI, a unified open framework integrating six processing layers: RF signal classification, acoustic motor-signature detection, YOLOv8-based visual detection, evidence-weighted sensor fusion, a Behavioral Intent Classification Engine (BICE), and a Graph Neural Network Swarm Intelligence Module (GNN-SIM). BICE introduces the first systematic six-class threat taxonomy for drone flight patterns, enabling predictive operator alerts with a 30-second advance-warning horizon. GNN-SIM is the first open framework for adversarial multi-drone formation analysis using Graph Attention Networks. Evaluated on three publicly available real-world datasets, the fused pipeline achieves 96.1% detection accuracy, 3.2% false alarm rate, AUC-ROC: 0.981, and 142ms end-to-end latency on commodity CPU-class hardware at approximately $500-$780 USD total system cost. All code, model weights, and simulation datasets are publicly released at submission.

AI.tldr: DroneShield-AI is a unified open framework for UAV threat detection that integrates six processing layers (RF, acoustic, visual, sensor fusion, behavioral intent classification, and swarm intelligence), achieving 96.1% detection accuracy on commodity hardware at low cost.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the emerging security challenge of UAV threats in the 21st century, aiming to provide a comprehensive, open, and affordable detection framework.
AI.method: The framework integrates six layers: RF signal classification, acoustic motor-signature detection, YOLOv8 visual detection, evidence-weighted sensor fusion, a Behavioral Intent Classification Engine (BICE) with a six-class threat taxonomy, and a Graph Neural Network Swarm Intelligence Module (GNN-SIM) using Graph Attention Networks.
AI.result: 96.1% detection accuracy, 3.2% false alarm rate, AUC-ROC of 0.981, 142ms end-to-end latency on commodity CPU-class hardware, with total system cost of approximately $500-$780 USD.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates high-performance, low-latency drone threat detection at a low cost, with all code, model weights, and datasets publicly released to advance open research.

title: RankVR: Low-Rank Structure Perception and Value Recalibration for Robust Composed Image Retrieval
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) constitutes a pivotal paradigm requiring models to perform joint reasoning on reference images and modification texts. However, the prevalence of Noisy Triplet Correspondence (NTC) in large-scale datasets severely constrains model performance. Existing denoising methods either target binary mismatches or rely on scalar-based point-wise estimation, neglecting rich global structural correlations among sample populations and dynamic value variations during training, thereby yielding suboptimal results. This paper identifies two critical unresolved challenges: Global Structural Inconsistency of Semantic Correlations and Hard Sample Discrimination Uncertainty. To address these, we propose RankVR, a framework designed to construct a robust CIR model via global structure consistency and dynamic value perception. Specifically, we introduce the Global Structure Consistency Perception (GSCP) module, which utilizes the Effective Rank of the Correlation Matrix to decouple clean samples from structural noise. By measuring rank difference, GSCP identifies samples disrupting macroscopic semantic symmetry. Furthermore, we develop the Adaptive Semantic Value Calibration (ASVC) module to distinguish high-value hard clean samples. By integrating training potential and reliability, it dynamically quantifies the semantic value of each triplet, ensuring effective utilization of hard samples while suppressing noise characterized by logical conflicts. Extensive experiments on the FashionIQ and CIRR benchmark datasets demonstrate that RankVR significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, validating its superior robustness in noisy environments.

AI.tldr: RankVR addresses noisy triplet correspondence in Composed Image Retrieval by using global structure consistency and dynamic value perception, outperforming SOTA methods on FashionIQ and CIRR.
AI.motivation: Noisy Triplet Correspondence in large-scale CIR datasets leads to suboptimal model performance. Existing denoising methods neglect global structural correlations and dynamic value variations, leaving two challenges unresolved: Global Structural Inconsistency of Semantic Correlations and Hard Sample Discrimination Uncertainty.
AI.method: Proposes RankVR with two modules: (1) Global Structure Consistency Perception (GSCP) using Effective Rank of the Correlation Matrix to decouple clean samples from structural noise via rank difference; (2) Adaptive Semantic Value Calibration (ASVC) to dynamically quantify each triplet's semantic value by integrating training potential and reliability, distinguishing high-value hard clean samples while suppressing noisy ones.
AI.result: RankVR significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on FashionIQ and CIRR benchmark datasets, demonstrating superior robustness in noisy environments.
AI.conclusion: Global structure consistency and dynamic value perception are effective strategies for handling noisy triplet correspondence in Composed Image Retrieval, validating the proposed RankVR framework's superiority.

title: MedCTA: A Benchmark for Clinical Tool Agents
comment: Project Page: https://ivul-kaust.github.io/MedCTA/ Code: https://github.com/IVUL-KAUST/MedCTA Data: https://huggingface.co/datasets/IVUL-KAUST/MedCTA
summary: To make clinically grounded decisions, medical AI agents are expected to go beyond simple recognition and be capable of tool retrieval, evidence acquisition, and integration. Existing benchmarks largely evaluate isolated perception or single-turn question answering, and therefore provide limited visibility into failures of planning, tool recruitment, and rollout reliability. We introduce MedCTA, a benchmark for evaluating medical tool agents on clinician-validated, step-implicit tasks grounded in realistic multimodal clinical inputs, including radiology images, pathology slides, and reports. MedCTA comprises 107 real-world clinical tasks with clinician-verified executable trajectories over 5 deployed tools, and supports process-aware evaluation of tool selection, argument validity, execution stability, trajectory fidelity, and outcome quality. We benchmark 18 open- and closed-source multimodal models and find that even frontier systems remain brittle in multi-step clinical tool use: autonomous rollouts are dominated by protocol failures, premature stopping, and incorrect tool recruitment, while gold-standard tool routing yields large but still incomplete gains. These results show that strong backbone perception does not translate into reliable agentic behavior in clinical settings. MedCTA provides a rigorous testbed for auditing, diagnosing, and advancing trustworthy medical AI agents. The dataset and evaluation suite are available at https://ivul-kaust.github.io/MedCTA/

AI.tldr: MedCTA is a new benchmark with 107 real-world clinical tasks to evaluate medical AI agents on multi-step tool use involving radiology images, pathology slides, and reports. Testing 18 multimodal models, it reveals that even top systems fail at planning, tool selection, and execution reliability—showing strong perception does not guarantee trustworthy agentic behavior in clinical settings.
AI.motivation: Existing medical AI benchmarks focus on isolated perception or single-turn QA, failing to reveal failure modes in planning, tool recruitment, and rollout reliability that are essential for clinically grounded decision-making.
AI.method: MedCTA comprises 107 clinician-validated, step-implicit clinical tasks over 5 deployed tools, with process-aware evaluation covering tool selection, argument validity, execution stability, trajectory fidelity, and outcome quality. 18 open- and closed-source multimodal models were benchmarked.
AI.result: Autonomous rollouts are dominated by protocol failures, premature stopping, and incorrect tool recruitment. Even with gold-standard tool routing, gains remain incomplete. Frontier systems remain brittle in multi-step clinical tool use.
AI.conclusion: Strong backbone perception does not translate into reliable agentic behavior in clinical settings. MedCTA provides a rigorous testbed for auditing, diagnosing, and advancing trustworthy medical AI agents.

title: ERN-Net : Evolving Reason Node-Net for Document Binarization
summary: This paper presents ERN-Net, an Evolving Reason Node-Net for efficient document image binarization. ERN-Net enhances degradation-sensitive regions, such as faint strokes, broken characters, and noisy backgrounds, through evolving reason nodes and multi-scale reasoning. We further compare ResNet-101, ConvNeXt-Tiny, and ConvNeXt-Base, and find that ConvNeXt-Tiny provides the best practical trade-off between accuracy and memory usage. In addition, DIBCO-based pretraining improves binarization performance without increasing model memory consumption, requiring only about 1.5 additional training hours. Experiments on DIBCO-style benchmarks show that ERN-Net is effective under low-data and low-memory settings.

AI.tldr: ERN-Net uses evolving reason nodes and multi-scale reasoning to enhance document image binarization, with ConvNeXt-Tiny offering the best accuracy-memory trade-off and DIBCO-based pretraining improving performance with minimal extra training time.
AI.motivation: To address degradation in document images (faint strokes, broken characters, noisy backgrounds) and achieve efficient binarization under low-data and low-memory constraints.
AI.method: ERN-Net employs evolving reason nodes and multi-scale reasoning to focus on degradation-sensitive regions. It compares ResNet-101, ConvNeXt-Tiny, and ConvNeXt-Base backbones, and uses DIBCO-based pretraining.
AI.result: ConvNeXt-Tiny provides the best accuracy-memory trade-off. DIBCO-based pretraining improves performance without extra memory cost, requiring ~1.5 additional training hours. ERN-Net is effective under low-data and low-memory settings.
AI.conclusion: ERN-Net is a practical and efficient solution for document image binarization, particularly suitable for resource-constrained scenarios.

title: Ouroboros-Spatial: Closing the Data-Model Loop for Spatial Reasoning
summary: Spatial reasoning remains a persistent challenge for multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Existing approaches largely rely on large-scale, statically curated datasets, where all training samples are treated uniformly regardless of the model's evolving capabilities. This static paradigm is inherently data-inefficient: training capacity is often spent on samples that are either trivial or overly difficult for the model at its current stage. To address this limitation, we propose Ouroboros-Spatial, a self-evolving training framework in which the model plays dual roles as a proposer and a solver. In each iteration, a frozen proposer generates spatial question-answer (QA) pairs from 3D scene metadata and raw video frames, together with executable code for deriving reliable ground truth. A learnable solver is then fine-tuned on the accepted samples, and its per-sample prediction confidence is used as a difficulty signal. This signal is fed back to the proposer in the next iteration, guiding it to generate questions better matched to the solver's current capabilities. Through this closed-loop design, the training distribution co-evolves with model ability, reducing redundant trivial examples while filtering out ambiguous or uninformative samples with limited learning value. Across six spatial reasoning benchmarks, Ouroboros-Spatial substantially improves Qwen3-VL-4B and Qwen3-VL-8B while using an order of magnitude fewer training examples than recent large-scale curated datasets. On VSI-Bench, it yields absolute gains of 9.9 and 6.8 points for the 4B and 8B models, respectively, enabling both to outperform a wide range of strong open-source and proprietary baselines.

AI.tldr: Ouroboros-Spatial is a self-evolving training framework where a model acts as both proposer (generating spatial QA pairs) and solver (learning from them), using prediction confidence as feedback to iteratively align training difficulty with the model's current capability, achieving state-of-the-art results on spatial reasoning benchmarks with far fewer training examples.
AI.motivation: Existing spatial reasoning approaches rely on large-scale, statically curated datasets that treat all samples uniformly regardless of the model's evolving capabilities, leading to data inefficiency—training capacity is wasted on samples that are either too trivial or too difficult for the model at its current stage.
AI.method: A self-evolving framework with dual roles: a frozen proposer generates spatial QA pairs from 3D scene metadata and raw video frames with executable code for ground truth; a learnable solver fine-tunes on accepted samples, and its per-sample prediction confidence serves as a difficulty signal fed back to the proposer to generate better-matched questions in the next iteration, creating a closed-loop co-evolution of training distribution and model ability.
AI.result: Ouroboros-Spatial substantially improves Qwen3-VL-4B and Qwen3-VL-8B across six spatial reasoning benchmarks while using an order of magnitude fewer training examples than recent large-scale curated datasets. On VSI-Bench, it achieves absolute gains of 9.9 and 6.8 points for 4B and 8B models, enabling both to outperform strong open-source and proprietary baselines.
AI.conclusion: The closed-loop, self-evolving training paradigm effectively addresses data inefficiency in spatial reasoning by dynamically aligning training difficulty with the model's evolving capabilities, achieving superior performance with significantly fewer training examples.

title: Multi-View In-Cabin Monitoring System for Public Transport Vehicles
summary: We introduce a multi-view in-cabin monitoring dataset for public transportation with synchronized RGB and depth images from four inward-facing cameras and a rotating LiDAR covering the vehicle interior of a digitalized and partly automated German city bus. The dataset contains 9.136 synchronized samples with annotations and is accompanied by a calibration and pseudo-labeling pipeline that generates 3D human pose estimates and oriented 3D bounding boxes for occupants. We further provide a nuScenes-format conversion and benchmark representative multi-view 3D detection models (e.g., Lift-Splat-Shoot and BEVFusion), supporting comparative evaluation and small-scale training of multi-view in-cabin perception models. The dataset and tools are available at https://github.com/EvgenyGorelik/multiview_incabin_dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-view in-cabin monitoring dataset for public transportation, featuring synchronized RGB, depth, and LiDAR data from a German city bus, with 9,136 annotated samples, calibration/pseudo-labeling pipelines for 3D human pose and bounding boxes, and benchmark evaluations of multi-view 3D detection models.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of public multi-view datasets for in-cabin monitoring in public transportation, enabling research on occupant perception, safety, and automation in buses.
AI.method: Collected synchronized RGB and depth images from four inward-facing cameras plus a rotating LiDAR in a digitized German city bus; created 9,136 annotated samples; developed calibration and pseudo-labeling pipelines for 3D human pose and oriented 3D bounding boxes; provided nuScenes-format conversion and benchmarked models like Lift-Splat-Shoot and BEVFusion.
AI.result: A publicly available multi-view in-cabin dataset with 9,136 synchronized samples, annotations, calibration tools, pseudo-labeling pipelines, and benchmark results for representative multi-view 3D detection models.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and tools enable comparative evaluation and small-scale training of multi-view in-cabin perception models, supporting research in public transportation safety and automation.

title: UniReason-Med: A Shared Grounded Reasoning Interface for 2D-to-3D Transfer in Medical VQA
summary: We study whether grounded reasoning supervision from abundant 2D medical images can improve 3D medical VQA when both input types are aligned through a common reasoning interface. We introduce UniReason-Med, a single-checkpoint framework that processes either a 2D image or a slice-serialized 3D volume at inference time, generating interleaved textual reasoning and localized visual evidence through shared box syntax, region-token injection, and a common grounded reasoning policy. To train this interface, we construct UniMed-CoT, a 220K instruction-tuning dataset with interleaved textual reasoning and grounded visual evidence, including 170K 2D and 50K 3D samples. Through supervised fine-tuning followed by outcome-level reinforcement learning, UniReason-Med learns to generate grounded reasoning traces without IoU/Dice-based localization rewards during RL. Data-mixture and component ablations show that joint 2D+3D grounded supervision substantially improves 3D reasoning over 3D-only training, while grounding and region-token injection consistently benefit both 2D and 3D tasks. These results suggest that a shared grounded reasoning interface can transfer reasoning structure from 2D images to slice-serialized volumetric medical understanding. The code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/IQuestLab/unireason-med.

AI.tldr: UniReason-Med is a unified framework that uses grounded reasoning supervision from 2D medical images to improve 3D medical VQA, achieving better 3D reasoning than 3D-only training through a shared reasoning interface with interleaved text and visual evidence.
AI.motivation: To leverage abundant 2D medical image annotations to improve 3D medical Visual Question Answering (VQA), which suffers from scarce training data, by aligning both modalities through a common grounded reasoning interface.
AI.method: A single-checkpoint framework (UniReason-Med) processing 2D or slice-serialized 3D volumes with shared box syntax, region-token injection, and grounded reasoning policy; trained on a 220K instruction-tuning dataset (UniMed-CoT) via supervised fine-tuning and outcome-level reinforcement learning.
AI.result: Joint 2D+3D grounded supervision substantially improves 3D reasoning over 3D-only training; grounding and region-token injection benefit both 2D and 3D tasks; effective without IoU/Dice-based localization rewards during RL.
AI.conclusion: A shared grounded reasoning interface can effectively transfer reasoning structure from 2D images to slice-serialized volumetric medical understanding, suggesting a promising direction for alleviating 3D medical data scarcity.

title: From Prompts to Tokens: Internalizing Causal Supervision in Vision-Language Model for Multi-Image Causal Reasoning
summary: Visual causal reasoning is essential for understanding and intervening in the physical world, requiring identification of causal variables from visual inputs and reasoning over intervention effects. Despite recent progress, large vision--language models (VLMs) remain brittle at such tasks, especially for interventional and counterfactual queries over multi-image inputs. Most existing explorations inject causal knowledge via textual prompts, leaving causal mechanisms external to model execution and limiting reliable control during inference. To address this problem, we propose BridgeVLM, which internalizes visual causal reasoning by inducing a causal graph from multi-image inputs and converting it into structured Causal Tokens executed by RAMP layers injected into the LLM decoder for causal message passing. We further introduce a unified training interface M3S for fine-grained causal supervision from different granularities (local/global level). BridgeVLM achieves 54.4% accuracy on intervention tasks on CausalVLBench (vs. 33.2% with prompt-level supervision), improves results on Causal3D from 43.6% to 49.0%, and substantially improves causal structure learning on CausalVLBench ($F_1$: 33.4% $\rightarrow$ 75.1%).

AI.tldr: BridgeVLM internalizes visual causal reasoning by inducing causal graphs from multi-image inputs into structured Causal Tokens with RAMP layers, achieving significant improvements over prompt-based methods on intervention tasks and causal structure learning.
AI.motivation: Large VLMs remain brittle at visual causal reasoning, especially for interventional and counterfactual queries over multi-image inputs, because existing methods inject causal knowledge via textual prompts, leaving causal mechanisms external to model execution and limiting reliable inference-time control.
AI.method: BridgeVLM induces a causal graph from multi-image inputs, converts it into structured Causal Tokens, and executes them via RAMP layers injected into the LLM decoder for causal message passing. A unified training interface M3S provides fine-grained causal supervision from local and global granularities.
AI.result: BridgeVLM achieves 54.4% accuracy on intervention tasks on CausalVLBench (vs. 33.2% with prompt-level supervision), improves Causal3D from 43.6% to 49.0%, and substantially improves causal structure learning on CausalVLBench (F1: 33.4% → 75.1%).
AI.conclusion: Internalizing causal reasoning via structured tokens and message-passing layers significantly outperforms external prompt-based causal injection for visual causal reasoning tasks.

title: AnchorEdit: Maintaining Temporal Consistency in Multi-turn Image Editing via Causal Memory
summary: Multi-turn image editing is essential for iterative design, yet current models often struggle with identity drift and error accumulation over successive steps. While existing research leverages video priors for consistency, their reliance on bidirectional attention is fundamentally misaligned with the causal, sequential nature of interactive editing. In this paper, we propose AnchorEdit, the first autoregressive (AR) diffusion-based framework designed specifically for high-resolution, long-term multi-turn editing. AnchorEdit bridges the gap between video priors and causal inference through a three-stage training curriculum: identity-preserving sing-turn pretraining, causal AR forcing fine-tuning with a novel self-rollout strategy to mitigate exposure bias, and consistency distillation for efficient 4-step generation. During inference, we introduce a memory mechanism to anchor the initial subject identity and ensure stable extrapolation across extended editing trajectories. To evaluate performance, we provide a new high-resolution multi-turn editing benchmark designed to stress-test long-horizon stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AnchorEdit achieves state-of-the-art results, maintaining exceptional subject fidelity and instruction following even over 10+ interaction rounds.

AI.tldr: AnchorEdit is an autoregressive diffusion framework for multi-turn image editing that uses a three-stage training curriculum (identity-preserving pretraining, causal AR forcing with self-rollout, and consistency distillation) plus a memory mechanism to maintain identity consistency across 10+ editing rounds, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-turn image editing models suffer from identity drift and error accumulation. Video prior approaches use bidirectional attention, which is fundamentally misaligned with the causal, sequential nature of interactive editing.
AI.method: Proposes AnchorEdit, an autoregressive diffusion framework with: (1) three-stage training curriculum (sing-turn pretraining, causal AR forcing with self-rollout to mitigate exposure bias, consistency distillation for efficient 4-step inference); (2) memory mechanism to anchor initial subject identity; (3) new high-resolution multi-turn editing benchmark.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results with exceptional subject fidelity and instruction following across 10+ interaction rounds.
AI.conclusion: Autoregressive diffusion with causal modeling is better aligned with interactive editing than bidirectional attention, and the proposed curriculum + memory effectively solves long-horizon stability issues.

title: Battery detection of XRay images using transfer learning
comment: Published at the European Symposium on Artificial Neural Networks (ESANN 2022)
summary: The need for detecting and sorting batteries is drastically increasing for many applications. This study proves the potential of transfer learning in predicting whether the image contains a battery or not, the location and identifying three types of batteries, namely: prismatic, pouch, and cylindrical Lithium-Ion Batteries (LIB). Particularly, it focuses on the transfer learning method in two applications: Training a large-scale dataset to detect electronic devices using a pre-trained YOLOv5m, then using these latter trained weights to detect and classify the batteries. The precision of battery detection achieves 94%, which outperforms the pretrained YOLOv5m weights with 5%, in 22 ms inference time.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that transfer learning using YOLOv5m achieves 94% precision in detecting and classifying three types of Lithium-Ion Batteries (prismatic, pouch, cylindrical), outperforming pretrained weights by 5% with 22ms inference time.
AI.motivation: The increasing need for detecting and sorting batteries across various applications motivates this work, as efficient and accurate battery identification is becoming critically important.
AI.method: The authors employ transfer learning by first training a YOLOv5m model on a large-scale dataset for electronic device detection, then fine-tuning the trained weights specifically for battery detection and classification into three types (prismatic, pouch, cylindrical LIBs).
AI.result: The proposed transfer learning approach achieves 94% precision in battery detection, which is 5% higher than using pretrained YOLOv5m weights, with a fast inference time of 22 ms.
AI.conclusion: Transfer learning proves effective for battery detection and type classification, significantly improving precision over standard pretrained models while maintaining efficient inference speed.

title: Seeing What Matters: Perceptual Wrapper with Common Randomness for 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) achieves impressive real-time rendering, it frequently struggles to synthesize high-frequency textures, a limitation heavily exacerbated in memory-constrained and rate-distortion-optimized (RDO) pipelines. To address this, we propose a versatile 2D perceptual wrapper that enhances the rendered outputs of existing 3DGS representations in a content- and view-dependent manner. Our method leverages a lightweight synthesis network conditioned on pseudo-random Gaussian noise to synthesize perceptually plausible textures. Supervised by Wasserstein Distortion, the network learns to match local feature statistics rather than strictly enforcing pixel-wise reconstruction fidelity, effectively mitigating the blurriness inherent in standard frameworks. We demonstrate the broad applicability of our plug-and-play approach across vanilla, memory-constrained, and RDO 3DGS methods. Comprehensive subjective and objective experiments confirm that our method significantly improves over existing baselines, yielding superior perceptual quality at sharply reduced file or model sizes.

AI.tldr: A 2D perceptual wrapper using lightweight synthesis network and Wasserstein Distortion to enhance texture quality in 3D Gaussian Splatting across vanilla, memory-constrained, and rate-distortion-optimized pipelines.
AI.motivation: 3DGS struggles with high-frequency textures, especially under memory constraints and rate-distortion optimization, leading to blurry outputs.
AI.method: Lightweight synthesis network conditioned on pseudo-random Gaussian noise, supervised by Wasserstein Distortion to match local feature statistics rather than pixel-wise fidelity.
AI.result: Superior perceptual quality with significantly reduced file/model sizes compared to existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: The plug-and-play 2D wrapper effectively improves perceptual quality across various 3DGS methods while maintaining compact model sizes.

title: A Comprehensive Ecosystem for Open-Domain Customized Video Generation
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables. Accepted by ICASSP 2026
summary: Recent progress in video generation has shown impressive visual synthesis capabilities. However, open-domain customized video generation remains limited by the lack of large-scale, annotated datasets capturing diverse identity-specific attributes. To address this, we introduce PexelsCustom-1M, the first publicly available million-scale dataset for identity-preserving video generation, containing one million curated <identity, text, video> triplets across 8,000+ categories. Leveraging this, we propose CustoMDiT, a parameter-efficient framework that adapts a pretrained multimodal Diffusion Transformer into a customized video generator with only 8% additional learnable parameters. Our method surpasses prior state-of-the-art. However, benchmarks such as DreamBooth cover only 100 classes, which is insufficient for real-world applications. To overcome this, we construct OpenCustom, a new benchmark with 1,000+ categories, created via cross-dataset knowledge fusion from ImageNet and MS-COCO. Extensive experiments confirm the advantages of both our dataset and model. We will open-source the entire ecosystem--including dataset, pipeline, benchmark, and implementations--to support further research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PexelsCustom-1M, a million-scale dataset for identity-preserving video generation, and CustoMDiT, a parameter-efficient model that achieves state-of-the-art results with only 8% additional learnable parameters. They also propose OpenCustom, a new benchmark with over 1,000 categories.
AI.motivation: Open-domain customized video generation is limited by the lack of large-scale, annotated datasets capturing diverse identity-specific attributes, and existing benchmarks like DreamBooth cover only 100 classes, which is insufficient for real-world applications.
AI.method: They create PexelsCustom-1M dataset (1M <identity, text, video> triplets, 8,000+ categories) and propose CustoMDiT, a parameter-efficient framework adapting a pretrained multimodal Diffusion Transformer with only 8% additional learnable parameters. They also construct OpenCustom benchmark via cross-dataset knowledge fusion from ImageNet and MS-COCO.
AI.result: CustoMDiT surpasses prior state-of-the-art in customized video generation, and the OpenCustom benchmark with 1,000+ categories provides a more comprehensive evaluation framework.
AI.conclusion: Both the dataset (PexelsCustom-1M) and model (CustoMDiT) demonstrate clear advantages. The entire ecosystem will be open-sourced to support further research.

title: MultiToP: Learning to Patch Visual Tokens to Mitigate Hallucinations in Video Large Multimodal Models
summary: Video Large Multimodal Models have achieved remarkable progress in video understanding, yet they remain prone to hallucinations, where generated responses are not faithfully supported by the input video. In this paper, we propose MultiToP, a multimodal-context-aware visual token patching framework that mitigates hallucinations by refining unreliable visual tokens before language generation. MultiToP introduces a lightweight Visual Token Patcher to predict token-level replacement distributions and selectively substitute unreliable visual tokens with a dynamic global patch token. To train the patcher effectively, we further propose information-guided rank calibration, which uses answer-conditioned frame-level information cues derived from the backbone to guide token replacement. Combined with ground-truth answer supervision and sparsity regularization, MultiToP enables localized visual evidence refinement without modifying the original model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MultiToP effectively reduces hallucinations on Vript-HAL with negligible inference overhead, improving the F1 scores of Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct by 50.60% over the vanilla model. Meanwhile, MultiToP preserves general video understanding ability, yielding an 18.58% relative accuracy gain on ActivityNet-QA for Video-LLaVA-7B.

AI.tldr: MultiToP is a lightweight visual token patching framework that reduces hallucinations in Video Large Multimodal Models by selectively replacing unreliable visual tokens with a dynamic global patch token before language generation, achieving significant F1 score improvements (50.60%) and accuracy gains (18.58%) across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Video Large Multimodal Models suffer from hallucinations—generating responses not faithfully supported by input video content—which undermines their reliability in video understanding tasks.
AI.method: MultiToP introduces a lightweight Visual Token Patcher that (1) predicts token-level replacement distributions, (2) selectively substitutes unreliable visual tokens with a dynamic global patch token, (3) uses information-guided rank calibration with answer-conditioned frame-level cues from the backbone, and (4) combines ground-truth answer supervision with sparsity regularization—all without modifying the original model.
AI.result: MultiToP improves F1 scores of Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct by 50.60% on Vript-HAL and yields an 18.58% relative accuracy gain on ActivityNet-QA for Video-LLaVA-7B, with negligible inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: MultiToP effectively reduces hallucinations in video LMMs while preserving general video understanding ability, offering a lightweight and model-agnostic solution for localized visual evidence refinement.

title: TextHOI-3D: Text-to-3D Hand-Object Interaction via Discrete Multi-View Generation and Joint Mesh Optimization
summary: Text-conditioned 3D generation has progressed rapidly for images and isolated objects, but producing a hand-object mesh remains challenging: the output must preserve language semantics, cross-view consistency, object geometry, articulated hand shape, and physically plausible contact. We present TextHOI-3D, a staged framework that uses generated multi-view observations as an explicit interface between text-conditioned visual generation and geometry-aware hand-object recovery. TextHOI-3D learns a compact VQ token space for fixed-camera hand-object observations, predicts multi-view visual tokens from text with a CLIP-conditioned visual autoregressive model, and recovers a unified hand-object mesh through prior initialization, multi-view joint optimization, and anti-penetration refinement. The design separates semantic generation from geometric recovery while keeping both stages connected by a discrete multi-view representation. On HO3D-derived evaluations, the multi-view setting reduces object CD from 17.26 mm to 4.92 mm and penetration volume from 5.3721 cm^3 to 0.2193 cm^3 compared with a single-view counterpart, while improving hand errors and surface F-scores. These results support multi-view visual tokens as an effective intermediate representation for text-driven 3D hand-object mesh creation.

AI.tldr: TextHOI-3D is a staged framework for text-to-3D hand-object mesh generation that uses multi-view visual tokens as an intermediate representation between semantic generation and geometric recovery, significantly outperforming single-view baselines on HO3D evaluations.
AI.motivation: Existing text-conditioned 3D generation works well for images and isolated objects but fails for hand-object meshes due to the complex requirements of preserving language semantics, cross-view consistency, object geometry, articulated hand shape, and physical contact plausibility.
AI.method: The framework uses three stages: (1) learning a compact VQ token space for fixed-camera hand-object observations; (2) predicting multi-view visual tokens from text using a CLIP-conditioned visual autoregressive model; (3) recovering a unified hand-object mesh through prior initialization, multi-view joint optimization, and anti-penetration refinement. This design separates semantic generation from geometric recovery while keeping them connected via discrete multi-view representation.
AI.result: On HO3D-derived evaluations, multi-view setting reduces object Chamfer Distance from 17.26mm to 4.92mm and penetration volume from 5.3721cm³ to 0.2193cm³ compared to single-view, while improving hand errors and surface F-scores.
AI.conclusion: Multi-view visual tokens serve as an effective intermediate representation for text-driven 3D hand-object mesh creation, successfully bridging semantic generation and geometric recovery.

title: LASA: A Weak Supervision Method for Open-Vocabulary Scene Sketch Semantic Segmentation
summary: Open-vocabulary scene sketch semantic segmentation aims to assign dense semantic labels to sparse line drawings based on flexible category vocabularies specified at inference time, without relying on pixel-level annotations during training. Unlike natural images, sketches lack texture and color cues, making semantic understanding heavily dependent on stroke layout and spatial configuration, a challenge that renders single-layer vision-language features inherently unstable. Our key observation is that attention maps from different Vision Transformer layers encode complementary spatial cues: shallow layers capture global structural layouts, while deeper layers focus on local stroke intersections and object parts. This suggests that cross-layer aggregation provides a more robust structural prior than any individual layer alone. Leveraging this insight, we propose a structure-aware framework built upon \textbf{L}ayer-wise \textbf{A}ccumulated \textbf{S}tructural \textbf{A}ttention (\textbf{LASA}), which aggregates multi-layer attention to guide hierarchical semantic alignment under weak supervision and refine predictions during inference. Experiments on FS-COCO, SFSD, and FrISS show that LASA improves mIoU by $+3.43$, $+8.01$, and $+15.74$ over the prior weakly supervised baselines, demonstrating consistent gains in both segmentation accuracy and spatial coherence. Our source code will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes LASA (Layer-wise Accumulated Structural Attention), a structure-aware framework that aggregates multi-layer Vision Transformer attention maps to achieve open-vocabulary semantic segmentation on sketches without pixel-level supervision, achieving significant mIoU improvements across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Sketches lack texture and color cues, making single-layer vision-language features unstable for semantic understanding. The authors observe that different ViT layers encode complementary spatial information (shallow layers capture global structure, deep layers capture local details), motivating the need for cross-layer aggregation.
AI.method: LASA (Layer-wise Accumulated Structural Attention) aggregates multi-layer attention maps from Vision Transformers to provide robust structural priors, guiding hierarchical semantic alignment under weak supervision and refining predictions during inference.
AI.result: On FS-COCO, SFSD, and FrISS benchmarks, LASA improves mIoU by +3.43, +8.01, and +15.74 respectively over prior weakly supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: Cross-layer aggregation of attention provides more robust structural priors than any single layer, enabling effective open-vocabulary sketch segmentation without pixel-level annotations.

title: Plan-and-Verify Video Reward Reasoning with Spatio-Temporal Scene Graph Grounding
summary: Reward models for text-to-video (T2V) generation guide post-training but often fail at fine-grained semantic alignment. We trace this to two structural weaknesses in existing reasoning-based reward models: they do not systematically verify every condition described in the prompt, and the visual evidence supporting each judgment remains implicit in their free-form reasoning. We propose SG-PVR, a video reward model that addresses these limitations through plan-and-verify reasoning grounded in spatio-temporal scene graphs. The verification plan decomposes the prompt into atomic claims, ensuring every requirement is checked. The spatio-temporal scene graph, encoding entities, attributes, and temporally-grounded relations, is extracted from the video and maintained as a persistent structured visual reference throughout reasoning. Each claim is verified against both the video and the scene graph, anchoring judgments in explicit visual evidence. SG-PVR achieves strong performance on semantic alignment, including fine-grained temporal semantics. As a test-time reranker, it further enhances compositional alignment in T2V generation.

AI.tldr: SG-PVR is a video reward model that uses plan-and-verify reasoning with spatio-temporal scene graphs to systematically verify prompt conditions against explicit visual evidence, improving fine-grained semantic alignment in text-to-video generation.
AI.motivation: Existing reward models for T2V generation fail at fine-grained semantic alignment because they don't systematically verify every condition in the prompt and rely on implicit visual evidence in free-form reasoning.
AI.method: SG-PVR decomposes prompts into atomic claims (plan) and extracts spatio-temporal scene graphs (entities, attributes, temporally-grounded relations) from videos as persistent structured visual references, then verifies each claim against both the video and the scene graph.
AI.result: SG-PVR achieves strong performance on semantic alignment, especially fine-grained temporal semantics, and enhances compositional alignment in T2V generation as a test-time reranker.
AI.conclusion: Structured plan-and-verify reasoning grounded in explicit scene graphs significantly improves reward model accuracy for video generation quality assessment.

title: Scene-Adaptive Nonlinear Tone Curves for Pseudo Ground-Truth Generation in Low-Light 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Low-light novel view synthesis is challenging because dark multi-view images contain noise, weak structural detail, and compressed dynamic range. Recent 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) methods address these challenges by generating pseudo ground-truth (pseudo-GT) images as supervision targets when paired normal-light references are unavailable. Existing pseudo-GT methods apply a uniform linear gain to all pixels, which clips bright regions while providing insufficient enhancement in dark regions, limiting reconstruction quality. We observe that nonlinear tone mappings, long established in 2D low-light enhancement, have not been explored for pseudo-GT generation in 3D reconstruction. Accordingly, we propose a scene-adaptive nonlinear tone-curve framework that replaces linear pseudo-GT with nonlinear alternatives. The framework introduces percentile-based normalisation for scene-agnostic curve application, a scene-adaptive offset for automatic black-level adjustment, and two complementary curves: Adaptive SoftExp (ASE), a bounded exponential curve, and Adaptive Poly3 (AP3), a data-driven cubic polynomial. The module changes only the pseudo-GT computation and leaves the 3DGS backbone unchanged. Experiments on three benchmarks covering 21 scenes show that both curves consistently outperform the linear baseline with PSNR improvements up to +4.34 dB on LOM and +3.25 dB on RealX3D. Both curves achieve similar performance despite their different mathematical forms, suggesting the improvement is curve-agnostic. Code is available at https://github.com/lvmingzhe/adaptiveToneCurve

AI.tldr: This paper proposes replacing the uniform linear gain used for pseudo-GT generation in low-light 3D Gaussian Splatting with scene-adaptive nonlinear tone curves (Adaptive SoftExp and Adaptive Poly3), achieving up to +4.34 dB PSNR improvement without changing the 3DGS backbone.
AI.motivation: Low-light novel view synthesis suffers from noise, weak structural detail, and compressed dynamic range. Existing 3DGS pseudo-GT methods use a uniform linear gain that clips bright regions and under-enhances dark areas. Nonlinear tone mappings, successful in 2D low-light enhancement, have not been explored for 3D reconstruction pseudo-GT generation.
AI.method: The framework introduces: (1) percentile-based normalization for scene-agnostic curve application, (2) a scene-adaptive offset for automatic black-level adjustment, and (3) two complementary nonlinear curves — Adaptive SoftExp (ASE, bounded exponential) and Adaptive Poly3 (AP3, data-driven cubic polynomial). Only the pseudo-GT computation module is changed; the 3DGS backbone remains untouched.
AI.result: Experiments on 21 scenes across three benchmarks show both curves consistently outperform the linear baseline. PSNR improvements up to +4.34 dB on LOM and +3.25 dB on RealX3D. Both curves achieve similar performance despite differing mathematical forms, indicating the improvement is curve-agnostic.
AI.conclusion: Scene-adaptive nonlinear tone curves are an effective drop-in replacement for linear pseudo-GT in low-light 3DGS, significantly improving reconstruction quality without modifying the underlying 3DGS architecture. The improvement stems from the nonlinear mapping itself, not the specific curve form.

title: SheafStain: Sheaf-Theoretic Schrödinger Bridge for Spatially and Biologically Coherent Virtual Staining
summary: Current virtual staining approaches offer the potential for time- and cost-efficient biomarker quantification in cancer diagnostics and prognostics. However, patch-wise inference for gigapixel whole slide images (WSIs) fails to maintain spatial continuity, yielding artifacts that cause catastrophic mismatches with ground-truth images. Although pathology Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) offer rich representations, their self-attention causes varying global contexts to produce inconsistent embeddings for the same physical region. We formalize and validate this ``context contamination'' as a sheaf-theoretic problem where these embeddings form a presheaf that violates the gluing axiom. To address this, we propose SheafStain, a new approach that reinterprets VFM features as sheaf-like sections for spatially and biologically coherent virtual staining. Specifically, SheafStain integrates class and patch tokens into a Schrödinger Bridge framework as sheaf-like sections. While the class token anchors biological consistency, patch tokens form a per-position spatial map. A backbone co-pretrained on Hematoxylin \& Eosin (H\&E) and Immunohistochemistry (IHC) yields non-degenerate cross-stain stalks, so a single VFM feature space supervises both input conditioning and output stain alignment. Departing from prior work that evaluates on isolated $256 \times 256$ patches and either random-crops or resizes the $1024 \times 1024$ ground truth, we translate at $256 \times 256$ and evaluate on the stitched $1024 \times 1024$ outputs across HER2, ER, PR, and Ki-67. SheafStain demonstrates promising results against six prior methods while mitigating patch-boundary stitching artifacts. Code will soon be released.

AI.tldr: SheafStain introduces a sheaf-theoretic approach to virtual staining that preserves spatial continuity in whole slide images by reformulating Vision Foundation Model features as sheaf-like sections, integrating them into a Schrödinger Bridge framework to eliminate patch-boundary artifacts and achieve coherent biomarker quantification.
AI.motivation: Current virtual staining methods for WSIs suffer from patch-wise inference artifacts and "context contamination" where VFM self-attention produces inconsistent embeddings for the same physical region due to varying global contexts, causing mismatches with ground-truth images.
AI.method: SheafStain reformulates VFM features as sheaf-like sections (violating gluing axiom → presheaf → sheaf fix), integrates class tokens (biological consistency anchoring) and patch tokens (spatial map) into a Schrödinger Bridge framework, uses a co-pretrained backbone on H&E and IHC for non-degenerate cross-stain stalks, enabling a single VFM feature space to supervise both input conditioning and output stain alignment.
AI.result: Evaluated on stitched 1024×1024 outputs (not isolated patches) across HER2, ER, PR, and Ki-67 biomarkers. SheafStain outperforms six prior methods while mitigating patch-boundary stitching artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The sheaf-theoretic perspective on context contamination provides a principled solution for spatially coherent virtual staining, promising more reliable biomarker quantification for cancer diagnostics.

title: Task-Aware Structured Memory for Dynamic Multi-modal In-Context Learning
summary: Multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) depend on in-context learning (ICL) for rapid task adaptation, but their scalability is severely limited by finite context windows and the growing cost of key-value (KV) caches in long multi-modal sequences. Existing memory compression approaches typically rely on rigid token removal or sample-dependent importance estimation, which introduces bias, disrupts semantic structure, particularly for visual representations, and yields static memories that cannot adapt to new queries. We introduce TASM (Task-Aware Structured Memory), a training-free framework that addresses these limitations through task-aware, structure-preserving, and dynamically accessible memory construction. TASM employs task-vector guided compression to replace sample-specific signals with a task-level direction that captures shared relevance across demonstrations. To preserve the underlying manifold, it applies semantics-aware token merging via bipartite graph matching, aggregating tokens without destructive pruning. Finally, TASM structures memory into a hierarchy comprising a compact Core Memory and a Latent Bank, facilitating query-adaptive dynamic retrieval. Evaluations confirm TASM maintains high performance under heavy compression, effectively balancing efficiency with adaptability.

AI.tldr: TASM is a training-free framework that improves MLLM in-context learning by using task-vector guided compression, semantics-aware token merging via bipartite graph matching, and a hierarchical memory structure (Core Memory + Latent Bank) to enable dynamic, query-adaptive retrieval while preserving semantic structure.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs struggle with ICL scalability due to limited context windows and growing KV cache costs in long multi-modal sequences. Current memory compression methods use rigid token removal or biased importance estimation, disrupting semantic structure (especially for visual data) and producing static memories that cannot adapt to new queries.
AI.method: TASM employs three key components: (1) Task-vector guided compression, which replaces sample-specific signals with a shared task-level direction; (2) Semantics-aware token merging via bipartite graph matching, to aggregate tokens without destructive pruning; (3) A hierarchical memory structure (Core Memory + Latent Bank) for query-adaptive dynamic retrieval.
AI.result: TASM maintains high performance under heavy compression, effectively balancing efficiency with adaptability, as confirmed by evaluations.
AI.conclusion: TASM provides a training-free solution to overcome context window and KV cache limitations in MLLMs, preserving semantic structure and enabling dynamic adaptation to queries through structured memory, all while maintaining strong performance under compression.

title: SG2Loc: Sequential Visual Localization on 3D Scene Graphs
comment: The code will be available at https://github.com/DmblnNicole/sg2loc
summary: Visual localization in complex indoor environments remains a critical challenge for robotics and AR applications. Sequential localization, where pose estimates are refined over time, is important for autonomous agents. However, traditional methods often require storing extensive image databases or point clouds, leading to significant overhead. This paper introduces a novel, lightweight approach to sequential visual localization using 3D scene graphs. Our method represents the environment with a compact scene graph, where nodes represent objects (with coarse meshes) and edges encode spatial relationships. For each image in the localization phase, we extract per-patch semantic features, predicting object identities. Localization is performed within a particle filter framework. Each particle, representing a camera pose, projects the coarse object meshes from the scene graph into the image, assigning object identities to patches based on visibility. The similarity of the per-patch features, in the input image, and object features from the scene graph determines the weight of a particle. Subsequent images are incorporated sequentially, refining the pose estimate. By leveraging a compact scene graph and efficient semantic matching, our method significantly reduces storage while maintaining performance on real-world datasets. The code will be available at https://github.com/DmblnNicole/sg2loc.

AI.tldr: A lightweight sequential visual localization method using compact 3D scene graphs (objects + spatial relationships) with particle filter and semantic patch matching, reducing storage while maintaining real-world performance.
AI.motivation: Visual localization in complex indoor environments is challenging, and traditional methods require storing extensive image databases or point clouds, leading to significant storage overhead.
AI.method: Represents environment with compact 3D scene graph (object nodes with coarse meshes, spatial edges). Extracts per-patch semantic features from input images. Uses particle filter where each particle projects coarse object meshes into the image, assigns object identities to patches, and weights particles by feature similarity. Sequential images refine the pose estimate.
AI.result: Significantly reduces storage requirements while maintaining performance on real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The compact scene graph approach enables efficient sequential localization without large-scale storage, suitable for robotics and AR applications.

title: Image Quality Assessment of Identity Cards Using Measures from Open Face Image Quality
comment: Presented on IWBF 2026 (14th International Workshop on Biometrics and Forensics)
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of assessing image quality in ID cards in remote verification systems by applying capture-related quality measures from the Open Face Image Quality (OFIQ) standard to ID card images. Our preprocessing pipeline includes corner detection, perspective normalization, and comprehensive foreground masking to ensure accurate and unbiased quality measure computation. We evaluate the effectiveness of these measures by analyzing their correlation with the performance of three presentation attack detection (PAD) algorithms across four diverse ID card datasets, where two datasets contain bona fide, i.e. pristine, images and two contain printed mock ID cards.
