Our results suggest that quality assessment based on some OFIQ measures can significantly improve PAD performance.

AI.tldr: This paper applies OFIQ quality measures to ID card images for remote verification, using preprocessing like corner detection and perspective normalization. Results show certain quality measures can improve Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) performance.
AI.motivation: To improve ID card image quality assessment in remote verification systems and enhance Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) performance.
AI.method: Preprocessing pipeline (corner detection, perspective normalization, foreground masking) + applying OFIQ quality measures to ID card images + evaluating correlation with PAD algorithm performance across 4 datasets.
AI.result: Quality assessment based on some OFIQ measures can significantly improve PAD performance.
AI.conclusion: Applying OFIQ-based quality measures to ID card images is effective for improving PAD in remote verification systems.

title: Task-Aligned Stability Analysis of Vision-Language Models for Autonomous Driving Hazard Detection
comment: 8 pages (5 main body + 3 references / appendices). ICML 2026 Workshop on Combining Theory and Benchmarks (CTB)
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used for scene understanding in autonomous driving, but robustness analysis often relies on task-agnostic embedding stability alone. We study whether corruption-induced embedding drift predicts changes in a task-aligned hazard score derived from CLIP image-text similarities. Using controlled corruptions on BDD100K road scenes, we compare embedding drift against margin drift, defined as the change in hazard score under perturbation. The relationship is highly corruption-dependent: some families exhibit strong coupling between representation drift and decision drift, while others induce hazardous decision instability despite relatively modest embedding change. Furthermore, corruption families differ in failure direction: most suppress hazard detections via false negatives, while occlusion instead triggers false alarms, suggesting that benchmark design should account for asymmetric failure modes, not just overall instability rates. These results suggest that robustness benchmarks should include task-aligned stability measures in addition to embedding-level perturbation statistics.

AI.tldr: VLMs used in autonomous driving show that corruption-induced embedding drift does not consistently predict task-aligned hazard score changes; different corruptions cause asymmetric failures (false negatives vs. false alarms), suggesting benchmarks need task-aligned stability measures beyond embedding-level analysis.
AI.motivation: Current robustness analysis for VLMs in autonomous driving relies primarily on task-agnostic embedding stability, which may not capture task-relevant decision changes under corruptions.
AI.method: Applied controlled corruptions on BDD100K road scenes, compared embedding drift against margin drift (change in CLIP-based hazard score), and analyzed failure direction (false negatives vs. false alarms) across corruption families.
AI.result: The relationship between embedding drift and decision drift is corruption-dependent. Some corruptions show strong coupling, while others cause large decision changes with modest embedding drift. Failures are asymmetric: most corruptions cause false negatives, but occlusion triggers false alarms.
AI.conclusion: Robustness benchmarks for VLMs should incorporate task-aligned stability measures alongside embedding-level perturbation statistics to capture asymmetric, task-relevant failure modes.

title: Wild3R: Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting from Unconstrained Sparse Photo Collection
summary: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) removes the need for time-consuming per-scene optimization required by traditional 3DGS. However, existing feed-forward approaches struggle with real-world photo collections that include diverse lighting conditions and transient objects. In this paper, we present Wild3R, a feed-forward approach for unconstrained sparse photo collections. The main bottleneck is the lack of training data that provides multiple viewpoints, a variety of illuminations, and transient variations necessary for learning robust scene representations. To address this, we introduce the WildCity dataset, which comprises 200 scenes, 170 lighting conditions, and transient objects, resulting in 337,500 images in total. By leveraging the dataset, our model learns appearance consistency across viewpoints conditioned on reference views, while removing transient content. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms existing feed-forward approaches and achieves results competitive with prior per-scene optimization-based methods.

AI.tldr: Wild3R is a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting approach for unconstrained sparse photo collections, trained on the new WildCity dataset (200 scenes, 170 lighting conditions, 337,500 images), that handles diverse lighting and transient objects while achieving results competitive with per-scene optimization methods.
AI.motivation: Feed-forward 3DGS struggles with real-world photo collections containing diverse lighting conditions and transient objects due to lack of appropriate training data.
AI.method: Introduces the WildCity dataset (200 scenes, 170 lighting conditions, 337,500 images) and learns appearance consistency across viewpoints conditioned on reference views while removing transient content.
AI.result: Outperforms existing feed-forward approaches and achieves results competitive with prior per-scene optimization-based methods.
AI.conclusion: With sufficient diverse training data, feed-forward 3DGS can handle unconstrained real-world photo collections effectively.

title: From Content to Knowledge: Lightning Fast Long-Video Understanding with Neural Knowledge Representations
summary: We propose a new paradigm for long video understanding by treating a long video as a Neural Knowledge Representation (NKR). NKR represents video contents neither as a stream of tokens nor pre-organized databases, but as an individual small portion of network weights attached to the VLM backbone. The NKR weights are optimized to encapsulate the video's semantic content via a novel Agentic Knowledge Distillation (AKD) process, where an agent automatically synthesizes dense descriptions and question-answer pairs to distill the video's knowledge into the NKR. While AKD serves as a comprehensive, one-time encoding phase, the resulting NKR transforms the video into a portable, reusable asset. At inference, the lightweight NKR is mounted onto a frozen Vision-Language Model (VLM), enabling direct, query-based understanding without reloading or re-encoding the original video. This approach decouples video length from inference cost, offering high amortized efficiency for multi-turn video understanding. Experiments on the LVBench benchmark show our method achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art approaches while reducing end-to-end latency by over two orders of magnitude, opening new possibilities for interactive long-video understanding.

AI.tldr: A new paradigm for long video understanding that encodes video content as lightweight neural network weights (NKR) attached to a VLM, achieving massive efficiency gains through agentic knowledge distillation.
AI.motivation: Long video understanding suffers from high inference costs because videos must be reloaded/re-encoded for each query. Existing approaches treat videos as token streams or databases, leading to latency that scales with video length.
AI.method: Proposes Neural Knowledge Representation (NKR) - video content encoded as small network weights attached to a VLM backbone. Uses Agentic Knowledge Distillation (AKD) where an agent auto-generates dense descriptions and QA pairs to distill video knowledge into NKR weights. At inference, NKR is mounted onto frozen VLM for direct query-based understanding.
AI.result: Achieves performance comparable to SOTA on LVBench benchmark while reducing end-to-end latency by over two orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: The NKR approach decouples video length from inference cost, enabling highly efficient multi-turn interactive long-video understanding with amortized efficiency.

title: Corpus Augmentation for Sign Language Translation via LLM-Guided Video Stitching
summary: Sign language translation (SLT) converts sign language video into spoken language text and holds significant promise for improving accessibility and enabling communication between signing and non-signing communities. While large weakly-aligned datasets have enabled pre-training at scale and gloss-free methods have reduced reliance on expert annotation, high-quality parallel sign video-text pairs for fine-tuning remain scarce, limiting generalisation on long-tail vocabulary and unseen constructions. We propose a corpus augmentation approach that requires no additional human annotation, external sign-language video corpora, or generative video models, relying only on the existing gloss-annotated training corpus and an LLM for sentence generation: per-gloss clips are extracted from training videos via CTC forced-alignment, novel gloss-sentence pairs are generated by a corpus-anchored LLM, and synthetic sequences are assembled through random sentence sampling and clip assignment. The resulting synthetic RGB video-text pairs are architecture-agnostic at the downstream training stage and can be consumed directly by RGB-based SLT models, or converted into pose or feature representations by pipelines that derive such inputs from video. Sincan et al. re-evaluated five recent gloss-free methods under strictly identical conditions; the largest verified gain over the GFSLT-VLP baseline was only 0.98 BLEU-4. Our augmentation, applied within the same framework, achieves +2.92 BLEU-4 without any change to architecture or training protocol. We further identify that synthetic data harms vision-language pretraining despite improving its objectives, and that optimising clip transitions for visual smoothness is counter-productive under L2-based criteria; we propose that abrupt boundaries may act as a form of implicit regularisation. Code is available at https://github.com/robizso/slt-datagen.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a corpus augmentation method for sign language translation that uses CTC forced-alignment to extract per-gloss clips from existing training data and an LLM to generate novel gloss-sentence pairs, creating synthetic RGB video-text pairs without additional human annotation or external data. The method achieves +2.92 BLEU-4 improvement over GFSLT-VLP baseline and reveals that synthetic data harms vision-language pretraining while abrupt clip transitions may serve as implicit regularization.
AI.motivation: High-quality parallel sign video-text pairs for fine-tuning are scarce, limiting generalization on long-tail vocabulary and unseen constructions in sign language translation.
AI.method: (1) Per-gloss clips extracted from training videos via CTC forced-alignment; (2) Novel gloss-sentence pairs generated by a corpus-anchored LLM; (3) Synthetic sequences assembled through random sentence sampling and clip assignment to create RGB video-text pairs.
AI.result: Achieves +2.92 BLEU-4 improvement over GFSLT-VLP baseline without any architecture changes, compared to the largest previous gain of only 0.98 BLEU-4.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic data harms vision-language pretraining despite improving its objectives, and optimizing clip transitions for visual smoothness is counter-productive; abrupt boundaries may act as implicit regularization.

title: Feature extraction for plant growth estimation
summary: Precision agriculture requires the estimation of plant growth stages in real-time. When the plant growth stage is known, the wastage of resources in cultivation, such as nutrients and water, is reduced as only the required resources need to be supplied. Plants at different growth stages, however, have similar morphological features, which can make autonomous growth stage estimation difficult. This paper presents two feature extraction methods for growth stage estimation: one that uses a bank of Gabor filters and morphological operations, and the other that uses pre-trained convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transfer learning. We test these methods on a publicly available plant growth stage dataset (``bccr-segset``) for two species, canola and radish, grown and captured under indoor conditions. The two proposed feature extraction methods are compared, using support vector machines and boosted trees as classifiers. We find that both methods are suitable for real-time applications, and that CNN features outperform the hand-crafted features, both with regard to speed and accuracy. The best system (VGG-19 features, classified with a radial basis function support vector machine) obtained an accuracy of 98.4% for both species, processing an image in 0.08 seconds.

AI.tldr: This paper compares Gabor filter-based handcrafted features vs. CNN-based features (VGG-19 with transfer learning) for real-time plant growth stage estimation, finding CNN features achieve 98.4% accuracy with 0.08s per image.
AI.motivation: Precision agriculture needs real-time plant growth stage estimation to reduce resource wastage (nutrients, water), but similar morphological features across stages make autonomous estimation difficult.
AI.method: Two feature extraction methods: (1) Gabor filter bank with morphological operations, (2) pre-trained CNNs (VGG-19) with transfer learning. Classifiers: SVM (RBF kernel) and boosted trees. Tested on bccr-segset dataset (canola & radish).
AI.result: CNN features outperformed handcrafted features in both speed and accuracy. Best system (VGG-19 + RBF SVM) achieved 98.4% accuracy for both species, processing each image in 0.08 seconds. Both methods suitable for real-time.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning features with transfer learning are more effective than handcrafted features for real-time plant growth stage estimation in precision agriculture.

title: SpecLoR: Spectral Lookahead Rectification for Motion-Coherent Text-to-Video Generation
summary: Flow Matching has enabled robust text-to-video generation via latent ODE sampling. However, velocity approximation and numerical discretization errors inevitably accumulate, causing sampling trajectories to drift. Consequently, generated videos often suffer from severe spatiotemporal inconsistencies. Nevertheless, directly correcting these drifted, noisy latents is challenging: (i) timestep-dependent noise obscures reliable structural cues; (ii) spatial interventions risk disrupting intricate local geometry while incurring heavy computational costs. To address this, we propose Spectral Lookahead Rectification (SpecLoR), a plug-and-play inference method that bypasses noise via lookahead prediction, and circumvents spatiotemporal entanglement by shifting corrections to the frequency domain, where universal statistical priors of natural videos are readily available. First, during early sampling stages, SpecLoR looks ahead to estimate the clean latent $z_{t,0}$ and computes its 3D spatiotemporal spectrum. Next, SpecLoR rectifies the amplitude spectrum to match the prior, leaving the phase intact. Finally, the corrected state is re-noised to resume ODE integration. Experiments on Wan2.2 demonstrate that SpecLoR significantly reduces physical artifacts and enhances motion coherence across multiple benchmarks with minimal computational overhead (4 additional NFEs).

AI.tldr: SpecLoR is a plug-and-play inference method that reduces spatiotemporal inconsistencies in text-to-video generation by using lookahead prediction and frequency-domain correction with minimal computational cost.
AI.motivation: Flow Matching-based text-to-video generation suffers from sampling trajectory drift caused by velocity approximation and numerical discretization errors, leading to severe spatiotemporal inconsistencies in generated videos.
AI.method: SpecLoR works by: (1) looking ahead to estimate clean latent and compute its 3D spatiotemporal spectrum, (2) rectifying the amplitude spectrum to match natural video priors while preserving phase, and (3) re-noising the corrected state to resume ODE integration.
AI.result: Experiments on Wan2.2 show SpecLoR significantly reduces physical artifacts and enhances motion coherence across multiple benchmarks with only 4 additional NFEs.
AI.conclusion: SpecLoR effectively addresses spatiotemporal inconsistencies in text-to-video generation with minimal overhead, through frequency-domain correction that bypasses noise challenges and avoids heavy spatial intervention costs.

title: ParseFixer: An Agentic Framework for Document Parsing via Selective Multimodal Correction
summary: In this report, we present our third-place solution for the DataMFM Challenge Track 1: Document Parsing. This track requires models to recover structured Markdown documents from document page images while preserving textual content and document structure. To address the complementary requirements of accurate content recovery and faithful structure reconstruction, we propose ParseFixer, an agentic framework for backbone parsing and selective correction. ParseFixer consists of two key modules: Full-Page Backbone Parsing (FBP) and Agentic Selective Correction (ASC). FBP produces stable initial Markdown outputs with MinerU2.5 Pro, while ASC detects high-value parsing failures and repairs them through a verify-and-rollback correction process. By placing selective multimodal correction after open-source backbone parsing, ParseFixer improves the recovery of key document elements without rewriting reliable backbone predictions. On the test set, our final system achieves an overall score of 61.78 and ranks third in Track 1, demonstrating its effectiveness for accurate document parsing. Our code will be released at: https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/CVPRW26-ParseFixer.

AI.tldr: ParseFixer is a third-place solution for the DataMFM Challenge Document Parsing track, using a two-module agentic framework (FBP with MinerU2.5 Pro + ASC with selective correction) to achieve 61.78 overall score.
AI.motivation: To address the complementary challenges of accurate content recovery and faithful structure reconstruction in document parsing from page images to structured Markdown.
AI.method: Two-module agentic framework: (1) Full-Page Backbone Parsing (FBP) using MinerU2.5 Pro for stable initial Markdown, and (2) Agentic Selective Correction (ASC) that detects high-value parsing failures and repairs them via verify-and-rollback, placing selective correction after backbone parsing without overwriting reliable predictions.
AI.result: Achieved overall score of 61.78 on the test set, ranking third in Track 1 of the DataMFM Challenge.
AI.conclusion: The selective correction approach (ASC) after backbone parsing improves key document element recovery without degrading reliable predictions, demonstrating effectiveness for accurate document parsing.

title: From Nominal Intensity to Equivalent Rainfall: A Path-Based Credibility Evaluation Framework for Simulated Rainfall in Autonomous-Driving Perception Tests
summary: Credible simulated-rainfall conditions are essential for identifying perception-system boundaries and supporting SOTIF-oriented risk assessment in automated driving. However, closed-field tests are often described only by nominal rainfall intensity or single-point measurements, making it difficult to align simulated rain fields with real rainfall and map test results to real-world scenarios. This paper proposes a path-based credibility evaluation method for simulated rainfall in autonomous-driving perception tests. Using the drop size and velocity joint distribution of real rainfall as the reference, each candidate path is represented by path-equivalent rainfall intensity, an uncertainty band, and a path-averaged Realism of Raindrop Distribution (RRD) score. Lidar target point-cloud count and mean reflectivity are further used for perception-consistency correction, quantifying the proxy capability of each simulated-rainfall path for real-rainfall perception effects. Experiments are conducted using about 10,000 real-rainfall raindrop-spectrum samples, 728 RainSense perception samples, and 45 spatial sampling points in a 2.4 m x 7.2 m simulated-rainfall area. Results show that spatial non-uniformity remains under the same nominal condition, confirming the need for path-based evaluation. The method identifies Path IV and Path VI as preferable candidates, with results of 11.54 +/- 0.31 mm/h, RRD = 0.43, and 8.28 +/- 0.34 mm/h, RRD = 0.46, respectively. These paths show more balanced performance in rainfall-intensity stability, raindrop-spectrum realism, and perception consistency. The proposed method supports path selection, condition description, and credible interpretation of autonomous-driving perception tests under rainfall.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a path-based credibility evaluation method for simulated rainfall in autonomous-driving perception tests, using real-rainfall raindrop distributions as reference, and identifies optimal test paths (Path IV and Path VI) that balance rainfall intensity stability, raindrop realism, and perception consistency.
AI.motivation: Closed-field simulated rainfall tests for autonomous driving lack credible evaluation methods—they rely on nominal intensity or single-point measurements, making it hard to align simulated conditions with real rainfall and map test results to real-world scenarios for SOTIF-oriented risk assessment.
AI.method: The method uses real-rainfall drop size and velocity joint distribution as reference. Each test path is characterized by path-equivalent rainfall intensity, uncertainty band, and path-averaged Realism of Raindrop Distribution (RRD) score. Lidar target point-cloud count and mean reflectivity are used for perception-consistency correction, quantifying how well simulated rainfall proxies real-rainfall perception effects.
AI.result: Using ~10,000 real-rainfall raindrop samples, 728 RainSense perception samples, and 45 spatial sampling points in a 2.4m x 7.2m area, the method shows spatial non-uniformity persists under the same nominal condition. Path IV (11.54±0.31 mm/h, RRD=0.43) and Path VI (8.28±0.34 mm/h, RRD=0.46) are identified as preferable candidates.
AI.conclusion: The proposed path-based credibility evaluation method effectively supports path selection, condition description, and credible interpretation of autonomous-driving perception tests under simulated rainfall conditions.

title: FitVTON: Fit-aware Virtual Try-On via Body-Garment Size Control
summary: While diffusion-based virtual try-on has achieved impressive visual realism, most methods treat the task as 2D inpainting, prioritizing texture preservation over physical plausibility. Consequently, they often produce plausible-looking images that fail to reflect authentic garment fit across diverse body shapes. We present FitVTON, a Fit-aware virtual try-on model on different bodies in the wild. FitVTON encodes garment-body size through structured text prompts, and learn from simulated try-on triplets from parameterized garment model. To improve the fitting effects over garment silhouettes, we introduce two auxiliary head to predict the masks for both the garment and the exposed body. We further introduce a texture rectification stage to improve realistic appearance from simulated data. To evaluate the fitting fidelity, we curate a real-world dataset, FittingEffect3K, combining VLM-based scoring protocol. Both subjective and quantitive experiments show that FitVTON demonstrate authentic fitting fidelity, with significant sizing accuracy and shape preservation over state-of-the-art methods while maintaining competitive image quality. Project Page: https://zenoning.github.io/FitVTON/.

AI.tldr: FitVTON is a fit-aware virtual try-on model that uses structured text prompts, simulated garment-body triplets, auxiliary mask prediction heads, and a texture rectification stage to achieve physically plausible garment fitting across diverse body shapes, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in sizing accuracy and shape preservation.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based virtual try-on methods treat the task as 2D inpainting, which prioritizes texture preservation over physical plausibility. This leads to images that look realistic but fail to reflect authentic garment fit across diverse body shapes.
AI.method: (1) Encodes garment-body size through structured text prompts; (2) Learns from simulated try-on triplets using parameterized garment models; (3) Introduces two auxiliary heads to predict masks for both garment and exposed body; (4) Uses a texture rectification stage to improve realistic appearance from simulated data; (5) Curates FittingEffect3K dataset with VLM-based scoring for evaluation.
AI.result: FitVTON demonstrates authentic fitting fidelity, significant sizing accuracy and shape preservation over state-of-the-art methods, while maintaining competitive image quality. Validated through both subjective and quantitative experiments.
AI.conclusion: FitVTON effectively addresses the physical plausibility gap in virtual try-on by explicitly modeling garment-body fit, proving that fit-aware approaches can outperform pure texture-preservation methods.

title: ViT-FREE: Efficient Face Recognition via Early Exiting and Synthetic Adaptation
comment: Accepted at the 20th IEEE International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (2026)
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have gained significant attention in computer vision and shown strong potential for face recognition (FR). However, their high computational cost makes deployment on resource-constrained devices challenging, motivating the need for methods that balance efficiency and accuracy. In this work, we investigate early exiting in pretrained ViTs as a simple yet effective training-free strategy for efficient FR inference. Leveraging the uniform feature dimensionality across transformer encoder blocks, we introduce ViT-FREE, a multi-exit framework that enables face verification directly from intermediate representations without modifying or retraining the backbone model, and thus, reducing inference cost. Empirically, we show that patch embeddings and attention maps evolve progressively across depth, exhibiting high similarity between consecutive ViT blocks and increasing alignment with the final representation. This indicates gradual feature refinement and attention convergence, suggesting that intermediate layers already provide stable and discriminative representations suitable for early exiting. Through extensive experiments on multiple FR benchmarks, we systematically analyze the accuracy-efficiency trade-off across exit depths. Our results demonstrate that later exits achieve a highly favorable balance, with exiting at layer 10 yielding up to a 20% speedup while incurring only a 1.5 drop in verification performance on benchmarks such as IJB-C. Also, we propose ViT-FREE_FT, a lightweight exit-specific fine-tuning strategy that adapts only the projection layers using a small synthetic dataset while keeping the transformer backbone frozen. This approach improves the performance of shallow exits while preserving the efficiency benefits and leaving deeper exits largely unaffected.

AI.tldr: ViT-FREE introduces a training-free early exiting framework for Vision Transformers in face recognition, enabling up to 20% speedup at layer 10 with only a 1.5% accuracy drop on IJB-C, plus a lightweight fine-tuning variant (ViT-FREE_FT) for shallow exits.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers (ViTs) show strong potential for face recognition but have high computational costs, making deployment on resource-constrained devices difficult. The authors aim to balance efficiency and accuracy without modifying or retraining the backbone model.
AI.method: The paper introduces ViT-FREE, a multi-exit framework that leverages uniform feature dimensionality across ViT blocks to perform face verification from intermediate representations. It uses early exiting as a training-free strategy. Additionally, ViT-FREE_FT fine-tunes only the projection layers on a small synthetic dataset while keeping the backbone frozen.
AI.result: Exiting at layer 10 achieves up to 20% speedup with only a 1.5% drop in verification performance on IJB-C. Patch embeddings and attention maps show progressive refinement and high similarity between consecutive blocks, supporting early exiting feasibility.
AI.conclusion: Later exits achieve a highly favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off. ViT-FREE_FT improves shallow exit performance without affecting deeper exits or sacrificing efficiency.

title: SpikeTAD: Spiking Neural Networks for End-to-End Temporal Action Detection
summary: Video understanding is a crucial part of computer vision, with numerous application scenarios. With the increasing popularity of mobile devices, an increasing number of efforts are trying to deploy video understanding models on them. However, existing video understanding models are difficult to deploy due to their large size and prohibitive power consumption. Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) have shown bioplausibility and low power advantages over Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), especially on neuromorphic chips which are regarded as essential components of future mobile devices. However, excessively long conversion time-steps and severe performance degradation problems limit their application. To solve the problems above, we explore the application of SNNs on temporal action detection (TAD), which is an important task in video understanding, and propose the first SNN-based end-to-end TAD architecture coined as SpikeTAD. While maintaining extremely low power consumption, SpikeTAD achieves an average mAP of 67.2% in THUMOS14 and 37.42% in ActivityNet-1.3, demonstrating the feasibility of a low-power TAD model. Our code is available at https://github.com/MCG-NJU/SpikeTAD.

AI.tldr: SpikeTAD is the first SNN-based end-to-end temporal action detection architecture, achieving competitive performance (67.2% mAP on THUMOS14, 37.42% on ActivityNet-1.3) with ultra-low power consumption, addressing conversion time-step and performance degradation issues in SNNs for video understanding.
AI.motivation: Existing video understanding models are too large and power-hungry for mobile devices. SNNs offer low-power advantages but suffer from long conversion time-steps and performance degradation. No prior work has applied SNNs to temporal action detection (TAD).
AI.method: The paper proposes SpikeTAD, the first end-to-end SNN-based architecture for temporal action detection, designed to address the conversion time-step and performance degradation issues while maintaining low power consumption.
AI.result: SpikeTAD achieves 67.2% average mAP on THUMOS14 and 37.42% on ActivityNet-1.3, demonstrating competitive performance with extremely low power consumption.
AI.conclusion: SpikeTAD demonstrates the feasibility of deploying low-power SNN-based temporal action detection models, opening up new possibilities for video understanding on mobile devices.

title: Vision Transformers for Face Recognition Need More Registers
comment: Accepted at the 20th IEEE International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (2026)
summary: Recent advances in Vision Transformers (ViTs) for face recognition (FR) have moved beyond the standard CLS-token paradigm. In this paradigm, a special classification token (CLS) is prepended to the patch embeddings and used as a representation of the input for downstream tasks. An alternative approach, Concatenated Patch Embeddings (CPE), instead leverages all patch tokens by concatenating them into a single vector, which is then projected into a compact face representation. CPE has been shown to improve recognition performance in comparison to CLS-based ones, but our qualitative analysis of attention maps showed the presence of artifacts that limit their interpretability. To address this issue, we incorporate register tokens, learnable tokens concatenated to the initial patch embeddings, and processed jointly through the ViT encoder blocks. This mechanism has been shown to produce more structured and interpretable attention maps compared to baseline ViT. We empirically demonstrate that these artifacts consistently appear across various ViT backbones, including small and large models, and that introducing register tokens effectively mitigates them. Adding four or eight registers significantly enhances interpretability, with eight registers providing the highest verification accuracies and smoothest attention structures. Our resulting model, ViT-8R, corresponds to a CPE-based ViT-B architecture augmented with eight register tokens achieves state-of-the-art performance among ViT-based FR models on large-scale IJB-B and IJB-C benchmarks. Also, ViT-8R produces substantially clearer attention maps compared with the baseline model, which offer deeper insight into the model's attention behavior (https://github.com/TaharChettaoui/ViT-FR-Registers)

AI.tldr: This paper improves Vision Transformers for face recognition by adding register tokens to eliminate attention map artifacts in the Concatenated Patch Embeddings (CPE) approach. The resulting ViT-8R model achieves state-of-the-art performance on IJB-B and IJB-C benchmarks with clearer, more interpretable attention maps.
AI.motivation: While CPE-based ViTs outperform CLS-token ViTs for face recognition, they produce attention map artifacts that limit interpretability. The authors aim to address these artifacts to improve both performance and model transparency.
AI.method: Register tokens (learnable tokens concatenated to initial patch embeddings) are incorporated into CPE-based ViT backbones. The model processes these tokens jointly through ViT encoder blocks, with analysis conducted across small and large backbones to evaluate artifact mitigation.
AI.result: ViT-8R (CPE-based ViT-B with 8 register tokens) achieves state-of-the-art performance among ViT-based FR models on IJB-B and IJB-C benchmarks. The register tokens effectively eliminate attention artifacts and produce substantially clearer, more structured attention maps.
AI.conclusion: Adding four or eight register tokens significantly enhances interpretability, with eight registers providing the highest verification accuracies and smoothest attention structures. The register token mechanism is effective across various ViT backbone sizes.

title: Metadata-Aware Multi-Prompt Reasoning for Zero-Shot Accident Understanding
comment: Accepted at the AUTOPILOT Workshop, CVPR 2026 (non-archival). Workshop Paper ID 15
summary: In this paper, we address the problem of zero-shot understanding of accidents from surveillance videos by identifying when an impact event occurs, what type of impact it is, and where in the frame it occurs using natural language. We propose a three-stage pipeline that decomposes the accident understanding into when, what, and where. The first stage extracts a short temporal window around the impact using vision-language similarity. In the second stage, we perform metadata-driven multi-prompt reasoning with five complementary views (baseline, motion, geometry, contrast, and tiebreaker) and resolve disagreement via an entropy-gated pairwise adjudicator. Finally, we localize the impact of an open-vocabulary detector queried on the predicted accident type and scene layout, and aggregate detections across keyframes using a score-weighted centroid. Our pipeline achieves a substantial improvement in the harmonic-mean score over a centre-of-frame baseline on the zero-shot ACCIDENT @ CVPR benchmark. We show that decomposing zero-shot video understanding into temporal localization, semantic classification, and spatial grounding enable more reliable reasoning with vision-language models than direct prompting alone.

AI.tldr: A three-stage pipeline for zero-shot accident understanding from surveillance videos decomposes the problem into temporal localization (when), semantic classification (what), and spatial grounding (where) using vision-language models, outperforming a centre-of-frame baseline.
AI.motivation: To enable zero-shot understanding of accidents in surveillance videos by identifying impact events, their types, and locations using natural language, without requiring task-specific training data.
AI.method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) temporal window extraction via vision-language similarity, (2) metadata-driven multi-prompt reasoning with five complementary views and entropy-gated pairwise adjudicator, (3) open-vocabulary detector localization with score-weighted centroid aggregation across keyframes.
AI.result: Substantial improvement in harmonic-mean score over a centre-of-frame baseline on the zero-shot ACCIDENT @ CVPR benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing zero-shot video understanding into temporal localization, semantic classification, and spatial grounding enables more reliable reasoning with vision-language models than direct prompting alone.

title: MFEN:Multi-Frequency Expert Network for Visible-Infrared Person Re-ID
summary: Visible-infrared person re-identification (VI-ReID) is challenging due to the large modality discrepancy between visible and infrared images. We contend that this discrepancy is largely related to differing lighting conditions, including differences in light wavelength and light source type. Recently, frequency-based VI-ReID approaches have achieved notable success because frequency information can better extract identity-relevant contours and details while excluding irrelevant lighting and color. However, existing methods either do not distinguish different frequency bands or focus on only one band, which is insufficient under diverse lighting conditions. To perform comprehensive frequency domain learning, we propose a Multi-Frequency Expert Network (MFEN) that enables multi-frequency modulation and adaptively combines different bands through a mixture-of-experts design. We further introduce Random Frequency Augmentation (RFA) and Frequency Auxiliary Optimization (FAO) to better train MFEN. The three modules are complementary and jointly capture critical frequency-domain details for robust representation learning. Extensive experiments on three VI-ReID datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: Proposes MFEN, a multi-frequency expert network with mixture-of-experts design, along with RFA and FAO modules, to address modality discrepancy in visible-infrared person re-identification through comprehensive frequency domain learning.
AI.motivation: Existing VI-ReID methods struggle with modality discrepancy caused by differing lighting conditions. Frequency-based approaches help but either fail to distinguish different frequency bands or focus on only one band, which is insufficient under diverse lighting conditions.
AI.method: 1) Multi-Frequency Expert Network (MFEN) with mixture-of-experts design for multi-frequency modulation and adaptive band combination. 2) Random Frequency Augmentation (RFA) for data augmentation. 3) Frequency Auxiliary Optimization (FAO) for better training. The three modules work complementarily.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three VI-ReID datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
AI.conclusion: Multi-frequency domain learning with adaptive expert combination effectively addresses lighting-induced modality discrepancy, outperforming single-band or undifferentiated frequency approaches.

title: Performance Analysis of YOLOv11 and YOLOv8 for Mixed Traffic Object Detection under Adverse Weather Conditions in Developing Countries
summary: In modern vehicular systems, robust performance under harsh conditions has become a critical problem of autonomous driving. Our study delivers a comprehensive evaluation of the newest iteration of the YOLO series, which is YOLOv11 Nano architecture benchmarked against the widely adopted YOLOv8 Nano as a baseline on a custom fused dataset that combines the Indian Driving Dataset (IDD) [1] and Berkeley Deep Drive Dataset (BDD100K) [2]. We have analyzed the trade-offs among detection accuracy, inference speed, and computational efficiency in high-entropy scenarios involving dense mixed traffic, rain, and low-light conditions. Specifically, YOLOv11n achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP@50) of 46.6%, with a notable 3.2% improvement in Precision over the baseline, effectively reducing false positives in cluttered scenes. Furthermore, the proposed model exhibits enhanced energy efficiency, requiring 22% fewer FLOPs (6.3G vs. 8.1G) while maintaining real-time inference speed of 70.9 FPS on a Tesla T4 GPU, offering an optimal trade-off for safety-critical edge deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates YOLOv11 Nano against YOLOv8 Nano on a fused Indian Driving Dataset (IDD) and Berkeley Deep Drive (BDD100K) dataset. YOLOv11n achieves 46.6% mAP@50 with 3.2% better Precision, 22% fewer FLOPs (6.3G vs 8.1G), and real-time 70.9 FPS on Tesla T4, showing improved efficiency for autonomous driving under harsh conditions.
AI.motivation: To address the critical need for robust object detection in autonomous driving under harsh, high-entropy conditions (dense traffic, rain, low-light) by evaluating the latest YOLOv11 Nano architecture.
AI.method: Benchmarking YOLOv11 Nano against YOLOv8 Nano on a custom fused dataset combining IDD and BDD100K, analyzing trade-offs among accuracy, speed, and efficiency.
AI.result: YOLOv11n achieves 46.6% mAP@50, 3.2% Precision improvement over baseline, 22% fewer FLOPs (6.3G vs 8.1G), and 70.9 FPS real-time inference on Tesla T4.
AI.conclusion: YOLOv11 Nano offers an optimal trade-off for safety-critical edge deployment in autonomous driving systems under challenging conditions.

title: Tac-DINO: Learning Vision-Tactile Features with Patch Alignment
summary: Touch is the primary medium through which humans interact with the environment. Currently, tactile learning mainly focuses on image-level pretraining or alignment. However, tactile signals correspond to local object contact, while research into scale alignment and holographic matching remains limited and proper datasets and benchmarks also lack. To bridge this gap, we first construct a data collection system to acquire a large-scale tactile dataset, with over 20 K tactile contacts from 505 real-world objects. Building on this dataset, we design a Vis-Tac Holographic Matching Benchmark to evaluate vision-tactile local-to-global alignment ability. Then we propose Vision-Tactile Patch Alignment (VTPA) methods for vision-tactile representation learning. Experiments demonstrate that these exceed the performance of methods without alignment and align with whole-object images.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a large-scale tactile dataset (20K+ contacts from 505 objects), a Vis-Tac Holographic Matching Benchmark for vision-tactile local-to-global alignment, and a Vision-Tactile Patch Alignment (VTPA) method that outperforms baselines without alignment.
AI.motivation: Current tactile learning focuses on image-level pretraining but lacks local-to-global scale alignment between tactile signals and visual object information; proper datasets and benchmarks are also missing.
AI.method: (1) Built a data collection system to gather 20K+ tactile contacts from 505 real objects. (2) Designed a Vis-Tac Holographic Matching Benchmark. (3) Proposed Vision-Tactile Patch Alignment (VTPA) for representation learning.
AI.result: VTPA methods exceed the performance of methods without alignment and achieve alignment with whole-object images.
AI.conclusion: The proposed benchmark and VTPA method effectively bridge the gap in tactile-visual local-to-global alignment, demonstrating superior performance.

title: World Model Self-Distillation: Training World Models to Solve General Tasks
summary: Pretrained video generators are promising visual world models that exhibit emergent task-solving abilities; however, their reliance on detailed textual descriptions limits their direct use for planning and decision-making. Existing approaches either outsource this reasoning to language or vision-language models, or rely on supervised fine-tuning with paired task-execution videos, which are costly to collect and difficult to scale. We propose a scalable framework that elicits task-solving ability in such models by combining self-distillation with reinforcement learning. Given an unlabeled scene image, a vision-language model generates a candidate task and a detailed step-by-step solution. The solution conditions a pretrained video diffusion model, the Demonstrator; we distill its behavior into an Executor conditioned only on the image and a short task prompt. This transfers execution knowledge from caption-guided generation to instruction-conditioned task solving without curated task-video supervision. We further improve the Executor with reinforcement learning from VLM feedback, exploiting the asymmetry between judging whether a sampled video satisfies a task and generating the solution. Experiments on our proposed WorldTasks-Benchmark and the DreamGen robotics benchmark show that the Executor surpasses the Demonstrator under our VLM-based evaluation protocol and transfers competitively to robotic tasks.

AI.tldr: A scalable framework that combines self-distillation with reinforcement learning to elicit task-solving abilities from pretrained video generators, transferring knowledge from caption-guided generation to instruction-conditioned task solving without curated task-video supervision.
AI.motivation: Pretrained video generators show emergent task-solving abilities but require detailed textual descriptions, limiting direct use for planning/decision-making. Existing approaches rely on costly supervised fine-tuning with paired task-execution videos or outsourcing reasoning to external models.
AI.method: (1) A VLM generates a candidate task and step-by-step solution from an unlabeled scene image. (2) A pretrained video diffusion model (Demonstrator) conditions on the solution. (3) Self-distillation transfers knowledge to an Executor conditioned only on image + short task prompt. (4) RL from VLM feedback further improves the Executor.
AI.result: On WorldTasks-Benchmark and DreamGen robotics benchmark, the Executor outperforms the Demonstrator under VLM-based evaluation and transfers competitively to robotic tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully elicits task-solving abilities in video generators without expensive task-video paired data, leveraging the asymmetry between judging and generating task solutions.

title: Non-frontal face recognition using GANs and memristor-based classifiers
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures, 1 Supplementary (22 pages, 16 figures, 6 tables, 4 supplementary notes)
summary: Face recognition systems have advanced significantly through deep learning techniques, delivering high performance and robustness in complex scenarios. However, these approaches incur substantial computational overhead, limiting their in situ applicability in resource-constrained platforms such as drones, where they can address challenges including non-frontal facial imagery. Memristor-based neuromorphic systems have emerged as a compelling approach for edge AI applications, combining biologically inspired processing with efficient and scalable computation. In this work, we propose a facial recognition framework that addresses non-frontal pose variations by integrating lightweight generative adversarial network (GAN)-based pose frontalisation with memristor-based neuromorphic recognition. The experimental results on two datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of combining adversarial learning with memristive technology, achieving up to 96% identification accuracy. The proposed approach alleviates the computational bottlenecks of conventional AI and offers a scalable, efficient solution for face recognition in dynamic real-world environments.

AI.tldr: A facial recognition framework combining lightweight GAN-based frontalisation with memristor-based neuromorphic computing achieves up to 96% accuracy on non-frontal faces, offering efficient edge AI for resource-constrained platforms like drones.
AI.motivation: Deep learning face recognition systems have high computational overhead, limiting deployment on resource-constrained edge platforms (e.g., drones), especially for challenging non-frontal facial imagery.
AI.method: Integrates lightweight GAN-based pose frontalisation to correct non-frontal faces, followed by memristor-based neuromorphic recognition for efficient, biologically inspired computation at the edge.
AI.result: Achieved up to 96% identification accuracy on two datasets, demonstrating effectiveness of combining adversarial learning with memristive technology.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach alleviates computational bottlenecks of conventional AI and offers a scalable, efficient solution for face recognition in dynamic real-world environments.

title: ISAP-3D: Identity-Slot Aligned Part-Aware 3D Generation
summary: Part-aware 3D generation aims to synthesize structured objects with semantically meaningful components, yet often suffers from structural ambiguity due to identity-layout entanglement. Existing methods either infer part identity and spatial layout implicitly, which can lead to unstable part allocation (e.g., slot swapping or part merging), or rely on strong layout conditions that are difficult to obtain in practice. We attribute this ambiguity to identity-slot permutation freedom: without explicit identity-slot alignment, the correspondence between semantic parts and generation slots is not identifiable during training, allowing multiple slot assignments to fit the same supervision and leading to inconsistent decomposition. Based on this insight, we argue that stable part-aware generation requires identity-aligned one-to-one slot modelling. We therefore propose an identity-slot aligned framework, ISAP-3D, which anchors each part with semantic identity tokens and performs identity-conditioned one-to-one layout prediction, followed by layout-conditioned geometry synthesis. Structured local-global conditioning maintains identity alignment across semantic, spatial, and geometric stages. We also construct a part-level dataset with a unified semantic protocol to enable learnable and consistent identity-slot alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate improved structural stability, controllability, and robustness over state-of-the-art part-aware generation baselines.

AI.tldr: ISAP-3D addresses identity-layout entanglement in part-aware 3D generation by anchoring each part with semantic identity tokens and performing identity-conditioned one-to-one layout prediction, achieving stable and controllable structural decomposition.
AI.motivation: Existing part-aware 3D generation suffers from structural ambiguity caused by identity-layout entanglement and identity-slot permutation freedom, where multiple slot assignments fit the same supervision, leading to inconsistent decomposition (e.g., slot swapping or part merging).
AI.method: ISAP-3D anchors each part with semantic identity tokens, performs identity-conditioned one-to-one layout prediction, then layout-conditioned geometry synthesis. Structured local-global conditioning maintains identity alignment across semantic, spatial, and geometric stages. A part-level dataset with unified semantic protocol is constructed for learnable identity-slot alignment.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate improved structural stability, controllability, and robustness over state-of-the-art part-aware generation baselines.
AI.conclusion: Stable part-aware generation requires identity-aligned one-to-one slot modelling; the proposed ISAP-3D framework effectively resolves structural ambiguity by enforcing explicit identity-slot correspondence.

title: MSUE: Multi-Modal Soccer Understanding Expert
summary: This paper presents our solution to the 2026 SoccerNet VQA Challenge. We first develop a cost-effective data synthesis pipeline driven by a Vision-Language Model (VLM), which systematically restructures raw domain data into diverse VQA samples, including concise answers and long-form responses. Second, we propose MSUE, a multi-expert question answering architecture that employs a Large Language Model (LLM) to dynamically dispatch questions to text, image, and video experts. These experts are instantiated as a strong text baseline Gemini3-Flash, a fine-tuned Qwen3-VL, and an external knowledge base, respectively, working collaboratively to enhance VQA performance. MSUE achieves an accuracy of \textbf{0.95} on the challenge benchmark, securing third place in the leaderboard.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a VLM-based data synthesis pipeline and a multi-expert QA architecture (MSUE) that achieves 0.95 accuracy, securing 3rd place in the 2026 SoccerNet VQA Challenge.
AI.motivation: To address the SoccerNet VQA Challenge by developing a cost-effective data synthesis pipeline and a multi-expert architecture that combines text, image, and video experts to improve VQA performance.
AI.method: (1) A VLM-driven data synthesis pipeline that restructures raw domain data into diverse VQA samples. (2) MSUE architecture using an LLM to dynamically dispatch questions to three experts: Gemini3-Flash (text), fine-tuned Qwen3-VL (image), and an external knowledge base (video).
AI.result: Achieves 0.95 accuracy on the challenge benchmark, securing third place on the leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: The proposed data synthesis and multi-expert architecture are effective for the SoccerNet VQA Challenge, as evidenced by the third-place result.

title: Q-Fold: Query-Aware Focus-Context Spatio-Temporal Folding for Long Video Understanding
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures, 8 tables. Code will be made publicly available
summary: Long-video understanding remains challenging for multimodal large language models, because temporally extended videos often contain thousands of frames and are therefore expensive to process exhaustively. Existing methods usually construct compact visual inputs from long videos under a limited visual budget. However, most of them still follow a frame-centric paradigm and apply similar representations to retained content regardless of its importance. This makes it difficult to preserve both high-fidelity visual evidence and broad temporal coverage. To address this issue, we propose Q-Fold, a training-free input construction framework for long-video understanding. Instead of treating isolated frames as the basic modeling unit, Q-Fold operates on contiguous temporal segments and constructs a heterogeneous Focus--Context representation under query guidance. Query-relevant segments are preserved as high-fidelity Focus Frames, while less relevant segments are folded into chronology-preserving contextual layouts. In this way, Q-Fold preserves critical visual evidence and broad temporal coverage, while better maintaining local temporal continuity within short segments. Experiments on four long-video benchmarks with multiple Video-MLLMs show that Q-Fold consistently improves performance without increasing the input budget. Notably, it achieves gains of up to 9.1 percentage points on an ultra-long video benchmark. Code will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: Q-Fold is a training-free framework that constructs heterogeneous Focus-Context representations from contiguous temporal segments, preserving both high-fidelity evidence and broad temporal coverage for long-video understanding, achieving up to 9.1% improvement on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing methods follow a frame-centric paradigm with uniform representations regardless of frame importance, making it hard to balance high-fidelity visual evidence and broad temporal coverage under limited visual budgets.
AI.method: Q-Fold operates on contiguous temporal segments and builds a Focus-Context representation: query-relevant segments become high-fidelity Focus Frames, less relevant segments are folded into chronology-preserving contextual layouts. It is training-free.
AI.result: Consistent improvement on four long-video benchmarks with multiple Video-MLLMs without increasing input budget, including up to 9.1 percentage points gain on an ultra-long video benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Q-Fold effectively balances critical visual detail and temporal coverage for long-video understanding via heterogeneous segment-level representation, outperforming existing approaches.

title: AGE-MIL: Anchor-Guided Evidence Learning for Patient-Level Prediction
summary: Existing computational pathology methods predominantly operate within whole-slide image (WSI)-level multiple instance learning (MIL) paradigms, while patient-level modeling remains underexplored. In routine pathological practice, however, pathologists derive diagnostic and prognostic conclusions by integrating evidence across multiple WSIs rather than relying on any single slide. This discrepancy creates a fundamental misalignment when patient-level supervision is directly imposed on conventional MIL frameworks, often leading to unstable optimization and degraded predictive reliability. To address this issue, we propose Anchor-Guided Evidence MIL (AGE-MIL), a weakly supervised framework for patient-level prediction. AGE-MIL constructs a patient-level anchor from slide representations to capture global pathological context and guide the retrieval and integration of diagnostically relevant local patches, enabling robust patient-level modeling. Patient-level risk is further modeled as an evidence accumulation process, promoting stable optimization under weak supervision. AGE-MIL is evaluated on six clinically relevant patient-level prediction tasks from two independent cohorts. Experimental results show that the proposed framework consistently outperforms eight state-of-the-art MIL methods. Code is available at https://github.com/wodeniua/AGE-MIL.

AI.tldr: AGE-MIL is a weakly supervised framework that improves patient-level prediction in computational pathology by constructing a patient-level anchor from multiple whole-slide images, modeling risk as evidence accumulation, and outperforming eight state-of-the-art MIL methods on six clinical tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing MIL methods operate at the WSI level, but pathologists diagnose patients by integrating evidence across multiple slides. Applying patient-level supervision directly to MIL frameworks causes unstable optimization and degraded reliability.
AI.method: AGE-MIL constructs a patient-level anchor from slide representations to capture global context, guides retrieval of diagnostically relevant patches, and models patient-level risk as an evidence accumulation process for stable weak supervision.
AI.result: Evaluated on six patient-level prediction tasks from two independent cohorts, AGE-MIL consistently outperforms eight state-of-the-art MIL methods.
AI.conclusion: AGE-MIL effectively bridges the WSI-level to patient-level gap in computational pathology, achieving robust prediction through anchor-guided evidence accumulation.

title: Time-Conditioned and Multi-Time Survival Prediction from 2D PET/CT Projections in Lung Cancer
summary: Accurate prediction of overall survival (OS) from positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) can support personalized treatment and follow-up strategies in oncology. However, the impact of temporal modeling on imaging-based survival prediction remains insufficiently explored. We investigate how different temporal formulations influence survival prediction by developing two complementary approaches: Attention-guided Time-Conditioned Survival (ATCS) and Multi-Time Survival (MTS). We retrospectively analyzed pre-treatment PET/CT images from 848 patients with non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), including 556 for model development and 292 for held-out testing. A previously proposed Time-Conditioned Survival (TCS) model was used as a baseline. Models were trained using 5-fold cross-validation and evaluated on the test set using time-dependent area under the curve (AUC) at 6-month intervals from 0.5 to 5 years. Both ATCS and MTS outperformed the baseline TCS model, achieving mean AUCs of 0.794 and 0.793, respectively, compared to 0.767. ATCS performed better at earlier time points (0.5-3 years), whereas MTS performed better at later intervals (3.5-5 years). Combining tumor-specific and tissue-wise PET/CT features improved performance over either input alone. Finer temporal discretization improved short-term prediction, while coarser intervals provided more stable long-term estimates. These findings demonstrate that temporal modeling and input design influence PET/CT-based survival prediction. The proposed approaches enable time-specific survival estimation from pre-treatment imaging and may support improved risk stratification and clinical decision-making.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how temporal modeling affects PET/CT-based survival prediction in NSCLC patients. Two novel approaches (ATCS and MTS) outperform the baseline TCS model, with ATCS excelling at early time points (0.5-3 years) and MTS at later intervals (3.5-5 years). Combining tumor-specific and tissue-wise features improves performance.
AI.motivation: Accurate OS prediction from PET/CT can support personalized oncology treatment, but the impact of temporal modeling on survival prediction remains insufficiently explored.
AI.method: Two temporal modeling approaches (ATCS and MTS) were developed and compared to baseline TCS. Models were trained on 848 NSCLC patients (556 training, 292 testing) using 5-fold cross-validation, with evaluation via time-dependent AUC at 6-month intervals from 0.5 to 5 years.
AI.result: ATCS (mean AUC 0.794) and MTS (0.793) outperformed TCS (0.767). ATCS performed better at 0.5-3 years; MTS at 3.5-5 years. Combined tumor-specific and tissue-wise features outperformed either alone. Finer temporal discretization improved short-term prediction; coarser intervals gave more stable long-term estimates.
AI.conclusion: Temporal modeling and input design significantly influence PET/CT-based survival prediction. The proposed approaches enable time-specific survival estimation and may improve risk stratification and clinical decision-making in NSCLC.

title: TopoCap: Learning Topology-Agnostic Motion Priors for Monocular Video-to-Animation
summary: The explosion of generative 3D assets has created a massive demand for animation, yet current motion capture methods remain brittle, restricted to species-specific templates (e.g., SMPL) or requiring labor-intensive manual rigging. We introduce TopoCap, the first unified framework capable of extracting motion from monocular video and retargeting it onto characters with arbitrary, unseen skeletal topologies, i.e., from bipeds to hexapods and inanimate objects, without test-time optimization. Our key insight is that while skeletal structures are combinatorial and discrete, the underlying physics of motion occupy a continuous, low-dimensional manifold. We materialize this insight via a two-stage generative pipeline. First, we learn a Universal Motion Manifold using a Graph CVAE that compresses heterogeneous kinematic chains into a shared, fixed-length latent code. By explicitly conditioning the decoder on a structural embedding of the target rig, we disentangle motion dynamics from skeletal topology. Second, we treat video-to-animation as a conditional flow matching problem, predicting these topology-agnostic codes from visual features. To learn this generalized prior, we introduce Mobjaverse, a massive-scale dataset curated from Objaverse-XL. Comprising over 5,000 unique skeletal topologies and 2 million frames, it exceeds the structural diversity of existing datasets by two orders of magnitude. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \MethodMotion outperforms specialist models on human and quadruped benchmarks while enabling zero-shot retargeting for the long tail of 3D creatures. Dataset is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/duckduckplz/Mobjaverse.

AI.tldr: TopoCap is a unified framework that extracts motion from monocular video and retargets it onto characters with arbitrary skeletal topologies (bipeds to hexapods) without test-time optimization, using a Universal Motion Manifold (Graph CVAE) and conditional flow matching, trained on the massive-scale Mobjaverse dataset (5,000+ topologies, 2M frames).
AI.motivation: Current motion capture methods are brittle, limited to species-specific templates (e.g., SMPL) or requiring manual rigging. There is no unified framework capable of handling arbitrary, unseen skeletal topologies for animation.
AI.method: Two-stage generative pipeline: (1) Universal Motion Manifold via Graph CVAE that compresses heterogeneous kinematic chains into a fixed-length latent code, disentangling motion dynamics from skeletal topology using structural embeddings. (2) Video-to-animation via conditional flow matching that predicts topology-agnostic codes from visual features. Trained on Mobjaverse (5,000+ skeletons, 2M frames).
AI.result: Outperforms specialist models on human and quadruped benchmarks, enables zero-shot retargeting for the long tail of 3D creatures (including inanimate objects).
AI.conclusion: TopoCap achieves the first unified framework for extracting and retargeting motion across arbitrary skeletal topologies from monocular video, enabled by the key insight that motion dynamics live on a continuous manifold despite discrete skeletal structures.

title: OpenMedReason: Scientific Reasoning Supervision for Medical Vision-Language Models
comment: 42 pages, 9 figures, 24 tables. Dataset and code: https://huggingface.co/datasets/neginb/OpenMedReason
summary: High-stakes clinical use of large vision-language models (LVLMs) requires reasoning that is grounded in visual evidence and clinical knowledge, not just correct final answers. We introduce OpenMedReason, a large-scale, open multimodal medical reasoning corpus comprising approximately 450K image-question-answer instances whose reasoning traces are primarily derived from curated biomedical, human-authored scientific articles. OpenMedReason provides high-fidelity supervision beyond synthetic chains of thought, covering diverse medical domain vision modalities such as radiological scans, microscopic images, visible light photographs, charts, and others. We complement it with OpenMedReason-Bench, a held-out benchmark that allows fine-grained evaluation of LVLMs along three complementary axes of capability, including perception, medical knowledge, and rationale, enabling diagnostic evaluation beyond final-answer accuracy. OpenMedReason is a rich training resource that exhibits its effectiveness in both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement-based alignment. Training with OpenMedReason yields a 20% average improvement in VQA accuracy over the base model and achieves performance within 4.2% of the strongest comparable-scale medical LVLMs. Fine-grained performance analysis confirms that the gains are not concentrated in any single axis: OpenMedReason improves perception, medical knowledge, and rationale jointly, and its reasoning traces are preferred over those of the base model in 86.1% of pairwise comparisons. We release the code and dataset at huggingface.co/datasets/neginb/OpenMedReason.

AI.tldr: OpenMedReason is a large-scale (~450K instances) open multimodal medical reasoning corpus derived from human-authored scientific articles, paired with a benchmark (OpenMedReason-Bench) for evaluating LVLMs across perception, medical knowledge, and rationale. Training on it yields 20% VQA accuracy gains over base models and achieves near state-of-the-art performance on comparable-scale medical LVLMs.
AI.motivation: High-stakes clinical use of LVLMs requires reasoning grounded in visual evidence and clinical knowledge, but existing approaches lack high-fidelity reasoning traces beyond synthetic chains of thought and fail to provide fine-grained diagnostic evaluation beyond final-answer accuracy.
AI.method: 1) Curated ~450K image-question-answer instances with reasoning traces derived from biomedical, human-authored scientific articles. 2) OpenMedReason-Bench for evaluating perception, medical knowledge, and rationale separately. 3) Validated via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement-based alignment.
AI.result: 20% average improvement in VQA accuracy over base model; performance within 4.2% of the strongest comparable-scale medical LVLMs; reasoning traces preferred over base model in 86.1% of pairwise comparisons; gains jointly improve perception, medical knowledge, and rationale.
AI.conclusion: OpenMedReason provides a rich, high-fidelity training resource that significantly improves LVLM reasoning across multiple axes, and the benchmark enables more diagnostic evaluation than final-answer accuracy alone.

title: Beyond Dark Knowledge: Mixup-Based Distillation for Reliable Predictions
summary: Knowledge Distillation (KD) and mixup have proven effective at inducing smoothness in class boundaries; KD captures inherent class relationships in probability distributions, and mixup enforces them through convex combinations of inputs. Their interaction, however, remains poorly understood, particularly when mixup is applied only during student training. In this setting, the teacher is queried on inputs drawn from a vicinal distribution it never saw during training, a controlled mismatch whose effect on knowledge transfer has not been characterised. We show that this mismatch causes the teacher's supervisory signal to be dominated by distributional confusion rather than inter-class structure. Despite it, the student does not merely imitate the teacher: it independently acquires greater linearity in the vicinal region, a structural property that the teacher lacks, and goes beyond dark-knowledge transfer. KD with mixup consistently improves student accuracy and reduces overconfidence by an order of magnitude relative to the baseline, across CIFAR and ImageNet with varying-capacity teachers. Crucially, calibration propagates from teacher to student independently of accuracy transfer, and temperature scaling governs a measurable accuracy-calibration trade-off that becomes more pronounced under vicinal training. These results reframe mixup distillation not as a degraded version of standard KD, but as a richer transfer channel that simultaneously shapes discriminative performance, uncertainty estimation, and representational geometry.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the underexplored interaction between Knowledge Distillation (KD) and mixup, revealing that applying mixup only during student training creates a teacher-student input mismatch that, surprisingly, benefits the student beyond mere imitation—improving accuracy, reducing overconfidence, and enabling independent acquisition of linear decision boundaries, while showing calibration transfer is separable from accuracy transfer.
AI.motivation: To understand the poorly characterized interaction between KD and mixup when mixup is applied only during student training, specifically the effect of teacher-student input distribution mismatch on knowledge transfer.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of KD with mixup applied solely during student training across CIFAR and ImageNet datasets, using varying-capacity teachers, measuring accuracy, calibration, overconfidence, and representational geometry.
AI.result: Teacher suffers from distributional confusion due to vicinal inputs; student independently learns greater linearity beyond teacher's knowledge; KD+Mixup consistently improves accuracy and reduces overconfidence by an order of magnitude; calibration propagates independently from accuracy; temperature scaling governs a measurable accuracy-calibration trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Mixup distillation should be reframed as a richer transfer channel, not a degraded version of standard KD, as it simultaneously shapes discriminative performance, uncertainty estimation, and representational geometry.

title: DynaTok: Token-Based 4D Reconstruction from Partial Point Clouds
summary: We address 4D reconstruction from partial point cloud sequences, where depth-sensor observations are incomplete, unordered, and lack explicit temporal correspondences. This geometry-only setting is challenging due to missing observations and ambiguous dynamics. While recent progress has largely relied on image-based methods, existing point-based approaches typically focus on single objects, assume relatively complete inputs, or require explicit correspondences. To address these limitations, we propose DynaTok, a point-based framework for correspondence-free 4D reconstruction from partial point cloud sequences without images. DynaTok encodes frames into compact latent tokens, aggregates incomplete observations over time with a Transformer-based spatiotemporal encoder, and decouples geometry and motion through residual tokens in a unified model. A flow-matching decoder then reconstructs complete, temporally consistent 4D point-cloud sequences conditioned on the latent tokens. Experiments on object- and scene-level benchmarks demonstrate improved reconstruction quality and temporal coherence from partial point cloud observations. Project page: https://wrchen530.github.io/dynatok/.

AI.tldr: DynaTok is a point-based framework for correspondence-free 4D reconstruction from partial point cloud sequences, using Transformer-based spatiotemporal encoding and flow-matching decoding to achieve improved reconstruction quality and temporal coherence.
AI.motivation: Existing point-based 4D reconstruction methods focus on single objects, assume complete inputs, or require explicit correspondences, and cannot handle the challenging setting of partial, unordered, and correspondence-free point cloud sequences without images.
AI.method: DynaTok encodes frames into compact latent tokens, uses a Transformer-based spatiotemporal encoder to aggregate incomplete observations over time, decouples geometry and motion via residual tokens, and employs a flow-matching decoder to reconstruct complete, temporally consistent 4D point-cloud sequences.
AI.result: Experiments on both object- and scene-level benchmarks demonstrate improved reconstruction quality and temporal coherence from partial point cloud observations.
AI.conclusion: DynaTok effectively addresses the limitations of prior point-based methods by enabling correspondence-free 4D reconstruction from partial, incomplete point cloud sequences without relying on images.

title: InternVideo3: Agentify Foundation Models with Multimodal Contextual Reasoning
summary: Recent progress in foundation models has shifted toward agentic behavior involving multi-step reasoning and tool use. However, open-source efforts largely focus on text-dominant settings, leaving long-horizon multimodal tasks underexplored. This gap is evident in video tasks requiring sustained temporal understanding and iterative interaction. We present InternVideo3, a framework enhancing these capabilities via Multimodal Contextual Reasoning (MCR). MCR treats understanding as a closed-loop process over a shared, evolving context containing observations, instructions, reasoning, tool actions, and memory. This frames long-video understanding as evidence accumulation and verification. To ensure efficiency, we introduce Multimodal Multi-head Latent Attention (M^2LA), a token-preserving reparameterization compressing KV-cache states while retaining the full token stream. Our staged training includes continued pretraining, short-to-long supervised fine-tuning, rule-based reinforcement learning, and on-policy distillation. Experiments show InternVideo3 achieves strong performance on benchmarks like Video-MME, MLVU, and EgoSchema. We further instantiate the model as a video agent with retrieval tools, demonstrating robust evidence-grounded behavior. Our results suggest that efficient context handling and closed-loop reasoning are vital for adapting open multimodal models toward long-horizon visually grounded agency.

AI.tldr: InternVideo3 enhances foundation models for long-horizon multimodal video tasks using Multimodal Contextual Reasoning (MCR) which treats understanding as a closed-loop process over evolving context, and introduces Multimodal Multi-head Latent Attention (M^2LA) for efficient KV-cache compression.
AI.motivation: Open-source foundation models lack capabilities for long-horizon multimodal tasks (e.g., video understanding) that require sustained temporal reasoning and iterative interaction, as most efforts focus on text-dominant settings.
AI.method: Proposes (1) Multimodal Contextual Reasoning (MCR) - closed-loop reasoning over shared evolving context (observations, instructions, reasoning, tool actions, memory); (2) Multimodal Multi-head Latent Attention (M^2LA) - token-preserving reparameterization for KV-cache compression; (3) Staged training: continued pretraining, short-to-long SFT, rule-based RL, on-policy distillation.
AI.result: Achieves strong performance on Video-MME, MLVU, and EgoSchema benchmarks. When instantiated as a video agent with retrieval tools, demonstrates robust evidence-grounded behavior.
AI.conclusion: Efficient context handling and closed-loop reasoning are vital for adapting open multimodal models toward long-horizon visually grounded agency.

title: SHERPA: Seam-aware Harmonized ERP Adaptation for Open-Domain 360$^\circ$ Panorama Generation
summary: Panoramic imagery is increasingly used in world-generation, games, and simulation, where users may need not only photorealistic scenes but also stylized and non-photorealistic environments. Large-scale text-to-image diffusion and flow models provide broad style and semantic priors for this goal, but planar image training misaligns them with the wrap-around topology and polar regions of $360^\circ$ panoramas represented in equirectangular projection (ERP). We present SHERPA, a lightweight adaptation framework that combines frequency-selective Circular RoPE, Circular Latent Encoding/Decoding, image-side FFN adapters, and a Dual-Path Training Scheme. Circular RoPE replaces only the seam-sensitive high-frequency horizontal RoPE band with integer-periodic harmonics while preserving the pretrained lower-frequency spectrum. The Paired Panorama Path supervises geometry, while the Unpaired Style Path uses self-supervised yaw consistency for target-free stylized prompts. As a result, SHERPA generates $360^\circ$ panoramas across both photorealistic panorama domains and open-domain stylized prompts.

AI.tldr: SHERPA is a lightweight adaptation framework that enables text-to-image diffusion/flow models to generate 360° panoramic imagery in both photorealistic and stylized domains by adding circular-aware components (Circular RoPE, Circular VAE, adapters) and a dual-path training scheme.
AI.motivation: Panoramic imagery is needed for world-generation, games, and simulation, but existing text-to-image models are trained on planar images and thus misaligned with the wrap-around topology and polar distortion of 360° equirectangular projections (ERP).
