AI.method: SHERPA introduces: (1) frequency-selective Circular RoPE replacing high-frequency horizontal bands with integer-periodic harmonics; (2) Circular Latent Encoding/Decoding; (3) image-side FFN adapters; and (4) a Dual-Path Training Scheme (Paired Panorama Path for geometry supervision + Unpaired Style Path with self-supervised yaw consistency).
AI.result: SHERPA generates 360° panoramas across both photorealistic panorama domains and open-domain stylized prompts.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight adaptation of pretrained diffusion/flow models can effectively bridge the gap between planar training and panoramic generation without full retraining, supporting both realism and stylization.

title: MLT-Dedup: Efficient Large-Scale Online Video Deduplication via Multi-Level Representations and Spatial-Temporal Matching
summary: The explosive growth of user-generated video content on online platforms is accompanied by the emergence of numerous near-duplicate videos--videos that are identical or highly similar but differ by partial edits. These duplicates degrade user experience and increase storage and bandwidth costs, making large-scale video deduplication a critical task. Existing video deduplication frameworks face a fundamental challenge in retrieving sufficient high-quality candidates under a limited index budget, as well as trade-offs between efficiency and precision. To address these issues, we propose MLT-Dedup, an efficient large-scale online video deduplication framework with Multi-Level representations and spatial-Temporal matching. Our approach employs a Multi-Level Video Encoder (ML-VE) to extract both fine-grained frame-level and sparse clip-level embeddings: sparse embeddings support efficient candidate retrieval, while fine-grained embeddings are loaded for precise pairwise matching. During matching, we introduce DiF-SiM, a Differential Feature-enhanced Similarity Module capable of locating duplicated temporal segments and providing reliable similarity evidence to support policy-driven deduplication decisions. Extensive experiments on a real-world large-scale platform demonstrate that MLT-Dedup reduces online repetition rates by 91% at 90% precision. Furthermore, our sparse retrieval design achieves a 5x increase in indexing capacity, enabling broader candidate coverage in real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: MLT-Dedup is an efficient large-scale online video deduplication framework using multi-level representations (fine-grained frame-level and sparse clip-level embeddings) and spatial-temporal matching (DiF-SiM module) to reduce online repetition rates by 91% at 90% precision while increasing indexing capacity by 5x.
AI.motivation: Near-duplicate videos on online platforms degrade user experience and increase storage/bandwidth costs. Existing deduplication frameworks struggle to retrieve sufficient high-quality candidates under limited index budgets and face trade-offs between efficiency and precision.
AI.method: Proposes MLT-Dedup with: (1) Multi-Level Video Encoder (ML-VE) extracting both fine-grained frame-level and sparse clip-level embeddings; (2) sparse embeddings for efficient candidate retrieval; (3) fine-grained embeddings for precise pairwise matching; (4) DiF-SiM (Differential Feature-enhanced Similarity Module) to locate duplicated temporal segments and provide similarity evidence for policy-driven decisions.
AI.result: Reduces online repetition rates by 91% at 90% precision. Sparse retrieval design achieves a 5x increase in indexing capacity, enabling broader candidate coverage in real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: MLT-Dedup effectively addresses the efficiency-precision trade-off in large-scale video deduplication through multi-level representations and temporal matching, with strong real-world platform validation.

title: Making Foresight Actionable: Repurposing Representation Alignment in World Action Models
summary: World Action Models (WAMs) offer a promising route for robot manipulation by using video generation models to model future scene evolution before producing control actions. However, our empirical observations reveal a phenomenon: generating plausible visual futures does not always guarantee the extraction of accurate actions. To diagnose this failure, we conduct action-head attention analysis and causal interventions. We find that the action decoder fails to focus on task-relevant interaction regions and remains sensitive to perturbations in task-irrelevant areas. This reveals a representation mismatch: hidden states optimized for visual reconstruction are not inherently organized in a form useful for low-level action control. In this paper, we propose AGRA, an Action-Grounded Representation Alignment objective that regularizes the world-action interface by aligning intermediate video diffusion features with spatially coherent semantic representations from a foundation visual encoder. We evaluate AGRA on real-world manipulation tasks. Experiments show that AGRA makes world model representations more action-grounded: by focusing the action decoder on the correct interaction regions, it improves object localization accuracy and affordance understanding, and makes the policy more robust to perturbations in task-irrelevant regions. As a result, AGRA consistently improves both in-distribution performance and out-of-distribution generalization over the baseline world action model.

AI.tldr: AGRA addresses the representation mismatch in World Action Models by aligning video diffusion features with semantic representations, improving action grounding and robustness in robot manipulation.
AI.motivation: WAMs generate plausible visual futures but often fail to extract accurate actions because action decoders ignore task-relevant regions and are sensitive to task-irrelevant perturbations, revealing a representation mismatch between visual reconstruction and action control.
AI.method: AGRA proposes an Action-Grounded Representation Alignment objective that regularizes the world-action interface by aligning intermediate video diffusion features with spatially coherent semantic representations from a foundation visual encoder.
AI.result: AGRA improves object localization accuracy, affordance understanding, and policy robustness to task-irrelevant perturbations, consistently improving in-distribution performance and out-of-distribution generalization on real-world manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Aligning world model representations with action-grounded semantic features is essential for bridging the gap between visual prediction and effective action control in robot manipulation.

title: Adapting Prithvi-EO for Fallow Detection for Food-Water Nexus: ViT-Adapter Necks and Parameter-Efficient Backbone tuning of Geospatial Foundation Model
comment: 10 pages, 6 figures. Preprint. Submitted to ACM SIGSPATIAL 2026
summary: Understanding spatial distribution of fallow land is important for optimizing the food-water (FW) nexus, given fallowing's role in crop rotation and water conservation. Fallow is a low accuracy class in USDA Cropland Data Layer (CDL). Geospatial foundation model (GFM), Prithvi-EO has shown strong transferability across computer vision tasks. However, its Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone produces features at a single spatial scale that are ill-suited for the multi-scale features required by object detection heads. Existing approaches synthesise multi-scale pyramids through scaling of single stride tokens, sacrificing spatial heterogeneity, and full backbone fine-tuning is computationally prohibitive for GFMs. We evaluate a fallow detection pipeline combining two parameter-efficient fine tuning (PEFT) schemes: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and a hybrid PEFT, with three neck designs: pseudo multi-scale, Lite ViT-Adapter, and Full ViT-Adapter. Our best configuration, Lite ViT-Adapter with a one-stage head, achieves a mAP@50 of 0.9479 with the Diou loss, suggesting the effectiveness of center-aware localization for irregular fallow field detection. ViT-Adapter free one-stage detection under LoRA improves the adapter-free anchor-based approach by 6.42%, and the best configuration improves baseline adapter-free anchor-based approach by 25.70%. These results demonstrate that lightweight spatial prior fusion and selective backbone unfreezing enable Prithvi-EO to capture local fallow patterns more effectively, outperforming approaches that rely on reshaped single-stride ViT tokens.

AI.tldr: This paper improves fallow land detection using Prithvi-EO (a geospatial foundation model) by combining parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA & hybrid PEFT) with multi-scale neck designs (Lite/Full ViT-Adapter). The best configuration achieves mAP@50 of 0.9479, outperforming baselines by 25.70%.
AI.motivation: Fallow land detection is important for food-water nexus optimization, but is a low-accuracy class in USDA CDL. Existing ViT-based GFMs produce single-scale features ill-suited for multi-scale object detection, and full fine-tuning is computationally prohibitive.
AI.method: Combines PEFT schemes (LoRA, hybrid PEFT) with three neck designs (pseudo multi-scale, Lite ViT-Adapter, Full ViT-Adapter) for fallow detection using Prithvi-EO. Evaluates center-aware localization (DIOU loss) with one-stage detection heads.
AI.result: Best config (Lite ViT-Adapter + one-stage head) achieves mAP@50 of 0.9479. LoRA improves adapter-free anchor-based approach by 6.42%; best config improves baseline by 25.70%.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight spatial prior fusion with selective backbone unfreezing (via PEFT) enables Prithvi-EO to capture local fallow patterns more effectively than reshaped single-stride ViT tokens, offering a computationally efficient path for multi-scale feature extraction from GFMs.

title: An Electric Potential-Augmented Benchmark Dataset for Physics-Guided Image Reconstruction of Electrical Capacitance Tomography
summary: While deep learning has significantly advanced image reconstruction of Electrical Capacitance Tomography (ECT), most data-driven methods map directly between capacitance and permittivity distribution, treating the sensor as a black box. This overlooks the electric potential field -- the fundamental physical link governing the nonlinear and ill-posed ``soft-field'' effect. To address this, we propose an electric potential-augmented ECT benchmark dataset designed to explicitly integrate latent physics behind ECT into the learning process. Generated via a COMSOL-MATLAB pipeline for an eight-electrode sensor as an example, the dataset comprises 20,000 randomized samples across four typical flow patterns. Crucially, alongside the conventional capacitance vectors and permittivity distributions depicted as images, each sample preserves eight excitation-wise full-field potential maps. Beyond data release, we provide illustrative evaluation protocols for both forward and inverse problems of ECT. Through comprehensive testing on both in-distribution (IID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios, we systematically demonstrate how the inclusion of electric potential maps enhances modeling accuracy and robustness. Fundamentally, the explicit inclusion of latent field information significantly lowers the barrier to integrating physical laws into ECT modeling, thereby establishing a standardized foundation for future physics-guided machine learning of ECT image reconstruction.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a benchmark dataset for Electrical Capacitance Tomography (ECT) that includes not only conventional capacitance-permittivity pairs but also full electric potential field maps. Generated via COMSOL-MATLAB for an eight-electrode sensor, the dataset supports physics-guided deep learning for ECT image reconstruction, demonstrating that adding potential field information improves model accuracy and robustness in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods for ECT treat the sensor as a black box, directly mapping capacitance to permittivity while ignoring the electric potential field — the fundamental physical link governing the nonlinear, ill-posed "soft-field" effect. The authors aim to bridge this gap by explicitly integrating latent physics into the learning process.
AI.method: The authors construct a benchmark dataset using a COMSOL-MATLAB pipeline for an eight-electrode sensor, generating 20,000 randomized samples across four typical flow patterns. Each sample includes: (1) conventional capacitance vectors, (2) permittivity distribution images, and (3) eight excitation-wise full-field potential maps. Illustrative evaluation protocols for both forward and inverse ECT problems are also provided.
AI.result: Comprehensive testing on both in-distribution (IID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios shows that including electric potential maps enhances modeling accuracy and robustness compared to methods that ignore this latent field information.
AI.conclusion: The explicit inclusion of electric potential field information lowers the barrier to integrating physical laws into ECT modeling, establishing a standardized foundation for future physics-guided machine learning of ECT image reconstruction.

title: Damage-TriageFormer: A Foundation-Model Framework for Typology-Based Building Damage Assessment from Mono-Temporal Imagery
summary: Decision-relevant building damage assessment is critical for prioritizing resources and recovery after a disaster, yet most automated methods either flatten damage into a single severity scale (no damage, minor, major, destroyed) or require paired pre- and post-event imagery that is often unavailable for emerging hazards. This paper presents Damage-TriageFormer, a single-image, post-event, footprint-conditioned model that produces a damage typology rather than a severity scale. We contribute: (1) DamageTriage-Bench, a new benchmark built from NOAA Emergency Response Imagery across Hurricane Michael (2018), Hurricane Helene (2024), and the 2025 Los Angeles wildfire complex, with five typology classes that distinguish roof damage from structural damage and, within each, partial from total extent; and (2) Damage-TriageFormer, which extends a DINOv3 ViT-L backbone with a Simple Feature Pyramid for higher-resolution instance pooling, a two-stage gated damage head, and an auxiliary severity-regression objective. Our model achieves macro F1 of 0.624 on validation and 0.619 on a held-out stratified test set, performing strongest where operational triage needs it most, with per-class F1 of 0.91 and 0.84 on undamaged buildings and total structural collapse, respectively. While the rare Total Roof Damage class remains difficult due to its limited examples and an inherently ambiguous label boundary, our results show that single-image post-event imagery can support actionable building damage typing, enabling targeted emergency response and resource allocation without a pre-event reference.

AI.tldr: Damage-TriageFormer is a single-image, post-event model that classifies building damage into a detailed typology (e.g., roof vs. structural, partial vs. total) rather than a simple severity scale, using a new benchmark (DamageTriage-Bench) and achieving strong performance on undamaged buildings and total structural collapse.
AI.motivation: Existing automated damage assessment methods either reduce damage to a coarse severity scale (no damage, minor, major, destroyed) or require paired pre/post-event imagery, which is often unavailable for emerging hazards. This limits actionable decision-making for emergency response.
AI.method: Damage-TriageFormer extends a DINOv3 ViT-L backbone with a Simple Feature Pyramid for higher-resolution instance pooling, a two-stage gated damage head, and an auxiliary severity-regression objective, trained on the new DamageTriage-Bench built from NOAA imagery.
AI.result: Macro F1 of 0.624 on validation and 0.619 on test set; per-class F1 of 0.91 (undamaged) and 0.84 (total structural collapse). Total Roof Damage class remains challenging due to limited examples and ambiguous label boundaries.
AI.conclusion: Single-image post-event imagery can support actionable building damage typing without needing pre-event references, enabling targeted emergency response and resource allocation.

title: Bridging Day and Night: Unsupervised Cross-Domain Re-Identification with Synergistic Prompt and Prototype Learning
summary: Cross-domain day-night re-identification (ReID) is fundamentally challenged by the substantial visual appearance discrepancies between daytime and nighttime scenes. Existing fully supervised methods rely heavily on labor-intensive annotations, which are costly and exhibit limited generalization across domains. In this work, we investigate unsupervised day-night ReID and propose a novel framework that synergistically combines prompt learning and prototype-based representation learning to associate identities across domains without requiring manual labels. Our approach follows a progressive two-stage training strategy. In the first stage, we exploit the vision-language model to generate instance-specific textual prompts in an annotation-free manner. We employ an instance-level alignment mechanism to embed visual features and textual prompts into a unified semantic space, aligning unlabeled day/night images with learnable prompts via instance-aware dynamic-bias adaptation. In the second stage, we construct domain-specific prototype memory banks and introduce two complementary modules: i) an intra-domain identity association module to enhance feature discriminability within each domain, and ii) a cross-domain prototype matching module to reliably identify positive and negative prototype pairs, thereby establishing robust identity correspondences across day and night. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our method. Under the unsupervised setting, our framework attains Rank-1 accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art fully supervised methods.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an unsupervised day-night person re-identification framework combining prompt learning and prototype-based representation learning, achieving performance comparable to supervised methods without requiring manual annotations.
AI.motivation: Existing day-night ReID methods rely on costly labeled data and struggle with domain generalization. The authors aim to remove the dependency on manual annotations for this challenging cross-domain task.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) Uses vision-language models to generate instance-specific textual prompts without annotations, aligning visual features and prompts via instance-aware dynamic-bias adaptation. (2) Builds domain-specific prototype memory banks with intra-domain identity association and cross-domain prototype matching modules to establish identity correspondences across day/night domains.
AI.result: Under unsupervised setting, the method achieves Rank-1 accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art fully supervised methods on public benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unsupervised framework effectively addresses day-night ReID by leveraging vision-language models and prototype learning, eliminating the need for expensive manual annotations while maintaining competitive performance.

title: VOID: Defeating Unauthorized Mimicry in Latent Diffusion Models
comment: To appear in the 35th USENIX Security Symposium (USENIX Security 2026)
summary:
While Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) have revolutionized visual synthesis, they are increasingly exploited for unauthorized mimicry of individuals. Existing defenses inject deceptive perturbations to steer the generated images toward irrelevant targets. However, this approach hinges on an ungrounded assumption: subtle perturbations can maintain their deceptive efficacy throughout an LDM's extensive generation process. In reality, the model's innate restoration mechanism will remove such perturbations and cause individual identities to re-emerge in the images generated.
  We propose VOID, a defense framework that overcomes this conundrum by manipulating an LDM's intrinsic stochasticity. VOID perturbs the diffusion pipeline in two novel ways: 1) amplifying the latent encoding errors to shatter an image's semantic structure, and 2) counteracting the target guidance signals to suppress the model's restoration capabilities. This results in a semantic corruption that thwarts any unauthorized mimicry. Notably, the security gain does not come at the price of visual utility, as VOID simultaneously manages to confine perturbations to human-imperceptible regions of protected images. Our comprehensive evaluation of 24 state-of-the-art defenses against 10 mimicry attacks on 5 datasets demonstrates VOID's unprecedented protection power: it increases the average Frechet Inception Distance (FID) from 113 to 365, a 223% improvement over the strongest defense to date.

AI.tldr: VOID is a defense framework against unauthorized mimicry by LDMs that manipulates diffusion stochasticity via amplifying latent encoding errors and counteracting guidance signals, achieving 223% improvement in FID over prior defenses while preserving visual utility.
AI.motivation: Existing defenses against unauthorized mimicry by LDMs rely on injecting deceptive perturbations, but these fail because the model's innate restoration mechanism removes such subtle perturbations, causing identities to re-emerge in generated images.
AI.method: VOID perturbs the diffusion pipeline in two ways: (1) amplifying latent encoding errors to shatter semantic structure, and (2) counteracting target guidance signals to suppress the model's restoration capabilities, while confining perturbations to human-imperceptible regions.
AI.result: Evaluated on 24 defenses against 10 mimicry attacks on 5 datasets, VOID increases average FID from 113 to 365, achieving 223% improvement over the strongest prior defense.
AI.conclusion: VOID provides unprecedented protection against unauthorized mimicry by LDMs without sacrificing visual utility, significantly outperforming existing defense methods.

title: Finding Sparse Subnetworks in One Training Cycle via Progressive Magnitude-Based Pruning
summary: Neural network pruning reduces model size by removing less important parameters while aiming to preserve predictive performance. Although the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (LTH) shows that sparse subnetworks can match dense networks when trained from suitable initializations, its iterative pruning procedure requires multiple complete training cycles. This work evaluates progressive magnitude-based pruning as a single-cycle alternative. The method gradually increases sparsity during training using a linear schedule and updates pruning masks based on active weight magnitudes. We conduct systematic experiments on CIFAR-10 and MNIST across ResNet, VGG-style, and LeNet architectures, comparing the proposed method with representative iterative and initialization-based pruning baselines, including LTH, SNIP, and GraSP. On CIFAR-10, the method achieves 95.12\% accuracy on ResNet-18 at 72.9\% sparsity, compared with 90.5\% reported for LTH. At extreme sparsity, it achieves 93.13\% accuracy on a VGG-like architecture at 97\% sparsity, compared with approximately 92.0\% for SNIP, and 93.44\% accuracy on VGG-19 at 97.97\% sparsity, compared with 92.19\% for GraSP at 98\% sparsity. A sparsity-accuracy analysis on ResNet-18 further shows that accuracy remains within 0.1 percentage points of the dense baseline across 70--85\% sparsity. These results indicate that progressive magnitude-based pruning provides an effective single-cycle approach for neural network sparsification under the evaluated settings.

AI.tldr: Progressive magnitude-based pruning (gradually increasing sparsity during a single training cycle) outperforms LTH, SNIP, and GraSP across multiple architectures on CIFAR-10 and MNIST, achieving higher accuracy at comparable or higher sparsity levels (e.g., 95.12% on ResNet-18 at 72.9% sparsity vs 90.5% for LTH).
AI.motivation: LTH achieves sparse subnetworks but requires multiple complete training cycles (iterative pruning), which is computationally expensive. The authors seek a single-cycle alternative that is more efficient while maintaining competitive performance.
AI.method: Progressive magnitude-based pruning: gradually increases sparsity during a single training cycle using a linear schedule, updating pruning masks based on active weight magnitudes.
AI.result: On CIFAR-10, achieves 95.12% accuracy on ResNet-18 at 72.9% sparsity (vs 90.5% LTH); 93.13% on VGG-like at 97% sparsity (vs ~92.0% SNIP); 93.44% on VGG-19 at 97.97% sparsity (vs 92.19% GraSP at 98%). Accuracy stays within 0.1pp of dense baseline at 70-85% sparsity.
AI.conclusion: Progressive magnitude-based pruning offers an effective single-cycle alternative for neural network sparsification, matching or surpassing multi-cycle methods like LTH across evaluated settings.

title: CellNet -- Localizing Cells using Sparse and Noisy Point Annotations
comment: Conference poster at Biology at Scale: From Variants to Cellular Programs and Functions
summary: Counting living cells is an important step in many biological research workflows. Our collaborators at the Wellcome Sanger Institute study vital genes in humans via large scale saturation genome editing screening, which requires repeatedly counting cells a great number of times. Computer Vision based automation is crucial for high throughput and resource efficiency. In this work, we develop a regression-based deep learning computer vision algorithm to detect and count cells in phase-contrast microscopy images. To reduce annotation effort, which in practice often becomes a bottleneck, we focus on counting cells only using sparse point annotations, which are fast and easy to acquire. By comparison to state-of-the-art 0-shot methods, we show that regression-based counting is a promising alternative in low data regimes. Through developing methods to automatically count living cells in microscopy images, we contribute to valuable research on the human genome. The code is available at https://github.com/beijn/cellnet.

AI.tldr: A deep learning regression-based method for counting living cells in phase-contrast microscopy images using only sparse point annotations, enabling high-throughput automated cell counting for genome editing screening.
AI.motivation: To automate the labor-intensive process of counting living cells in large-scale saturation genome editing screening, reducing annotation effort and improving throughput.
AI.method: A regression-based deep learning computer vision algorithm that counts cells in phase-contrast microscopy images using only sparse point annotations, avoiding dense annotation requirements.
AI.result: The regression-based counting approach outperforms state-of-the-art 0-shot methods in low data regimes, demonstrating effectiveness with minimal annotation.
AI.conclusion: Regression-based cell counting with sparse annotations is a promising alternative for automated cell counting, contributing to human genome research efficiency.

title: Bridging the Modality Gap in Forensic Image Retrieval
comment: 23 pages, 5 figures, paper submitted to Elsevier journal
summary: Automated image retrieval plays an increasingly critical role in modern forensic analysis, supporting investigative workflows that rely on efficient comparison of visual evidence. While prior work has focused primarily on developing and optimizing multimodal retrieval systems, limited attention has been paid to evaluating the forensic applicability of these technologies across diverse real-world scenarios. In this study, we present a unified retrieval framework adapted to four key forensic tasks: (1) tattoo image retrieval given a tattoo query image; (2) tattoo retrieval guided by human-expert textual descriptions, modelling the common situation where a witness verbally describes a tattoo; (3) tattoo retrieval from hand-drawn sketches; and (4) face retrieval from forensic face sketches. Our system leverages a multimodal large language model (MLLM) to automatically generate structured textual descriptions for all queries and gallery images, followed by sentence-transformer embedding for text-based comparison. We evaluate retrieval using visual-only embeddings, text-only embeddings and a multimodal fusion strategy that combines text- and image-based similarity scores derived from state-of-the-art visual feature extractors relevant to each task. The fusion of modalities consistently improves retrieval precision and robustness, especially in scenarios where visual information is limited or noisy (e.g., sketches, partial tattoos, or fragmented witness statements). This work highlights the forensic value of a unified multimodal retrieval pipeline and demonstrates how modern MLLMs can operationalize challenging forensic tasks that traditionally rely on manual expert analysis. Our results position multimodal retrieval as a promising tool for supporting investigative workflows involving tattoos, facial composites, and witness descriptions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified multimodal retrieval framework using MLLMs and sentence-transformers for four forensic tasks (tattoo image-to-image, text-to-tattoo, sketch-to-tattoo, and face sketch retrieval), demonstrating that multimodal fusion of visual and textual embeddings improves retrieval robustness, especially under noisy or incomplete query conditions.
AI.motivation: Prior work has focused on developing and optimizing multimodal retrieval systems, but limited attention has been paid to evaluating their forensic applicability across diverse real-world scenarios. Manual expert analysis remains the norm for forensic tasks like tattoo and face sketch retrieval.
AI.method: A unified retrieval framework leveraging a multimodal large language model (MLLM) to automatically generate structured textual descriptions for queries and gallery images, followed by sentence-transformer embedding for text-based comparison. Retrieval is evaluated using visual-only, text-only, and multimodal fusion (combining text- and image-based similarity scores from state-of-the-art visual feature extractors).
AI.result: Fusion of modalities consistently improves retrieval precision and robustness, especially in scenarios with limited or noisy visual information (e.g., sketches, partial tattoos, fragmented witness statements).
AI.conclusion: The unified multimodal retrieval pipeline demonstrates forensic value, and modern MLLMs can operationalize challenging forensic tasks that traditionally rely on manual expert analysis. Multimodal retrieval is a promising tool for investigative workflows involving tattoos, facial composites, and witness descriptions.

title: Findings of the MAGMaR 2026 Shared Task
comment: Findings of the 2nd workshop on Multimodal Augmented Generation via Multimodal Retrieval (MAGMaR); Resources at this url: https://github.com/rekriz11/MAGMAR_2026
summary: This overview paper presents the results of the shared task for the second workshop on Multimodal Augmented Generation via Multimodal Retrieval (MAGMaR). In this shared task participants submitted systems focused on either (i) video retrieval or (ii) grounded generation of articles given retrieved videos. Teams could submit to either task. For the retrieval task, we had 2 participating teams that submitted a total of 17 systems -- all of which beat a baseline derived from the winner of last year's shared task. On the generation side, we had 4 teams submit 16 systems. All teams had at least one generated report that was labeled the best by a human annotator.

AI.tldr: This paper presents results from the MAGMaR shared task on multimodal retrieval and grounded generation, with 2 teams submitting 17 retrieval systems (all beating last year's baseline) and 4 teams submitting 16 generation systems.
AI.motivation: To benchmark and advance multimodal retrieval and grounded generation systems through a shared task competition.
AI.method: A shared task with two tracks: (i) video retrieval and (ii) grounded generation of articles from retrieved videos, with teams submitting systems to either or both tasks.
AI.result: Retrieval: 2 teams, 17 systems, all outperforming last year's baseline. Generation: 4 teams, 16 systems, with all teams having at least one top-rated human-annotated report.
AI.conclusion: The shared task successfully advanced multimodal retrieval and generation, with broad participation and strong improvements over previous baselines.

title: Natural-Language Temporal Grounding in Hour-Long Videos is a Search Problem: A Benchmark and Empirical Decomposition
summary: Temporal grounding--returning the interval $[t_s, t_e]$ for a natural-language query over a video--is the language interface to long-form video, yet has been studied on short videos; the dynamics of hour-scale natural-language grounding remain underexplored. We take the position that at hour-scale, the binding constraint is search, not recognition: Video-LLMs are bottlenecked not by localizing a nearby event, but--given a natural-language query--by searching for the relevant region of a long video. To test this, we release ExtremeWhenBench, the first open hour-scale grounding benchmark (2,273 queries over 194 videos, mean 75.7 min, max 9 hr) with an open-form query distribution. Every open Video-LLM collapses while a frame-level retrieval baseline outperforms them; a failure taxonomy attributes 85% of failures to search; and a retrieve-then-ground hybrid recovers 6.7x over the monolithic Video-LLM--mirroring retrieve-then-read in open-domain QA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ExtremeWhenBench, the first hour-scale temporal grounding benchmark, showing that current Video-LLMs fail primarily due to search limitations rather than recognition, and a retrieve-then-ground hybrid approach significantly outperforms monolithic models.
AI.motivation: Temporal grounding has only been studied on short videos; the dynamics of hour-scale natural-language grounding remain underexplored, and the authors hypothesize that search (not recognition) is the binding constraint at this scale.
AI.method: Release ExtremeWhenBench (2,273 queries over 194 videos, mean 75.7 min, max 9 hr); evaluate open Video-LLMs vs. frame-level retrieval baseline; conduct failure taxonomy analysis; propose a retrieve-then-ground hybrid approach.
AI.result: All open Video-LLMs collapse; frame-level retrieval outperforms them; 85% of failures attributed to search; retrieve-then-ground recovers 6.7x over monolithic Video-LLM.
AI.conclusion: At hour-scale, the bottleneck is search, not recognition, mirroring the retrieve-then-read paradigm in open-domain QA.

title: From 2D Grids to 1D Tokens: Reforming Shared Representations for Multimodal Image Fusion
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Multimodal image fusion aims to integrate complementary information from different modalities into a fused image that preserves rich local details while maintaining globally consistent appearance. Existing approaches build shared representations on 2D feature grids, which excel at modeling local structures but offer limited leverage over image-level global appearance factors. To balance these objectives, we introduce a compact 1D token interface based on a frozen pretrained image tokenizer for modeling non-local appearance/base factors. Rather than using the tokenizer as a reconstruction backbone, our design uses the 1D token space as a global carrier while retaining the 2D spatial pathway for local structure restoration. Specifically, we introduce Selective Token Editing (STE), which sparsely updates/replaces a small set of critical tokens, providing a lightweight mechanism to steer global appearance coherence while keeping the fusion backbone unchanged and avoiding extra losses. Experiments on four commonly used benchmarks show that our method achieves the best overall performance, with consistent, multi-metric improvements in both global coherence and local fidelity. Project page: https://zju-xyc.github.io/1D-Fusion-Project-Page/

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a 1D token interface (Selective Token Editing) for multimodal image fusion that balances local detail preservation and global appearance coherence by combining a frozen pretrained image tokenizer's 1D token space with a 2D spatial pathway, achieving state-of-the-art results on four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal image fusion methods use 2D feature grids that excel at local structures but struggle with global appearance factors. The goal is to balance local detail fidelity with globally consistent appearance.
AI.method: Introduces a compact 1D token interface using a frozen pretrained image tokenizer as a global carrier, while retaining a 2D spatial pathway for local structure. Selective Token Editing (STE) sparsely updates/replaces critical tokens to steer global appearance coherence without changing the fusion backbone or adding extra losses.
AI.result: Achieves best overall performance on four commonly used benchmarks, with consistent multi-metric improvements in both global coherence and local fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The 1D token interface approach effectively balances local and global objectives in multimodal image fusion, and the lightweight STE mechanism provides an efficient way to enhance global appearance without modifying the backbone.

title: Slots, Transitions, Loops: Learning Composable World Models for ARC
summary: ARC tests in-context rule induction: given a few input-output demonstrations, a model must infer the hidden rule and apply it to a new query. While many approaches express ARC rules through language, code, or symbolic programs, ARC itself is visual-symbolic: rules appear as grid transitions over objects, colors, shapes, and spatial relations. We introduce Loop-OWM, an object-centric world-modeling architecture that learns these rules as composable transitions over structured states. It combines color-prototype slots, demonstration-conditioned task summaries, and a looped transition model with dense propagation and slot-conditioned correction. On both ARC-1 and ARC-2, Loop-OWM outperforms non-looped and looped baselines with comparable or fewer parameters. These results suggest that ARC rules can be learned not only as language descriptions or searched programs, but also as transitions over visual-symbolic world states.

AI.tldr: Loop-OWM is an object-centric world-modeling architecture that learns ARC visual-symbolic rules as composable transitions over structured states, outperforming baselines on ARC-1 and ARC-2.
AI.motivation: ARC tests in-context rule induction from visual-symbolic grid demonstrations, yet most prior approaches express rules through language, code, or symbolic programs rather than learning them as visual-symbolic world transitions.
AI.method: Loop-OWM combines color-prototype slots, demonstration-conditioned task summaries, and a looped transition model with dense propagation and slot-conditioned correction.
AI.result: On both ARC-1 and ARC-2, Loop-OWM outperforms non-looped and looped baselines with comparable or fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: ARC rules can be learned not only as language descriptions or searched programs, but also as transitions over visual-symbolic world states.

title: Anatomically Conditioned Recurrent Refinement for Topology-Aware Circle of Willis Segmentation
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, 1 table. Accepted at EUSIPCO 2026
summary: Segmenting the Circle of Willis (CoW) from Magnetic Resonance Angiography (MRA) is challenging due to complex topology and thin vascular structures that are prone to fragmentation. Standard Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) often fail to capture these topological constraints, resulting in "broken vessel" artifacts. To address this, we propose the Anatomically Conditioned Recurrent Refinement U-Net (AC2RUNet). Our architecture decouples segmentation into two streams: a Static Stream that extracts invariant anatomical features and a lightweight Dynamic Stream that iteratively refines topological errors over time. We further introduce a dynamic curriculum learning strategy that transitions from high-recall geometric supervision to topology-aware constraints. Validated on the TopCoW dataset, AC2RUNet substantially reduces Hausdorff Distance (4.72 mm vs 9.17 mm) and Betti number errors (0.19 vs 0.40), improving topological connectivity over the nnU-Net baseline while maintaining comparable volumetric Dice.

AI.tldr: AC2RUNet improves Circle of Willis segmentation by using a dual-stream architecture (Static + Dynamic) with curriculum learning, reducing fragmentation and topological errors compared to nnU-Net.
AI.motivation: Standard CNNs produce "broken vessel" artifacts in CoW segmentation due to complex topology and thin structures; existing methods fail to enforce topological constraints.
AI.method: Proposes AC2RUNet with a Static Stream for invariant features and a Dynamic Stream for iterative topological refinement, plus dynamic curriculum learning shifting from geometric to topology-aware supervision.
AI.result: Reduced Hausdorff Distance (4.72 vs 9.17 mm) and Betti number errors (0.19 vs 0.40) over nnU-Net, with comparable Dice.
AI.conclusion: AC2RUNet effectively enforces topological connectivity in CoW segmentation, outperforming nnU-Net on connectivity metrics without sacrificing volumetric accuracy.

title: Echoes of the Prior: A Computational Phenomenology of Forgetting
summary: Memory is not merely the storage of data; it is the scaffolding of reality. When biological memory fades, the world does not simply turn black; it regresses into an unrecognizable chaos. Echoes of the Prior is an interactive installation that attempts to visualize this subjective phenomenology of forgetting. By inducing controlled synaptic decay within a Feed-Forward 3D Reconstruction model, we create an artistic analogy for the erosion of the brain's predictive priors. We position the Neural Network not as a tool for engineering, but as a cognitive proxy - a silicon brain whose structural degeneration evokes the disorienting, poetic, and terrifying experience of losing one's grip on the world. Ultimately, we offer this framework as a catalyst, inviting the wider community to explore the uncharted potential of neuromorphic aesthetics in visualizing the fragility of intelligence. Interactive demo see https://decart-4d.github.io/.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an interactive installation that uses a Feed-Forward 3D Reconstruction model with induced synaptic decay to artistically simulate the phenomenological experience of memory loss and forgetting.
AI.motivation: To visualize the subjective phenomenology of forgetting by creating an artistic analogy for the erosion of the brain's predictive priors, positioning neural networks not as engineering tools but as cognitive proxies.
AI.method: Inducing controlled synaptic decay within a Feed-Forward 3D Reconstruction model to simulate brain's predictive prior erosion, presented as an interactive installation.
AI.result: An interactive installation that evokes the disorienting, poetic, and terrifying experience of losing one's grip on the world through structural degeneration of a neural network.
AI.conclusion: Offers this framework as a catalyst for exploring neuromorphic aesthetics in visualizing the fragility of intelligence, inviting the wider community to explore this uncharted territory.

title: Atlas H&E-TME: Scalable AI-Based Tissue Profiling at Expert Pathologist-Level Accuracy
summary: Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining is the cornerstone of histopathology, yet scalable, quantitative analysis of H&E whole-slide images (WSIs) remains a central challenge in computational pathology. We present Atlas H&E-TME, an AI-based system built on the Atlas family of pathology foundation models that predicts tissue quality, tissue region, and cell type labels across multiple cancer types, yielding over 4,500 quantitative readouts per slide at cell-level resolution. A key challenge to validating such systems is overcoming morphological ambiguity inherent to H&E-only ground truth and the limited scalability of more informed references drawing on modalities such as immunohistochemistry (IHC). We address this with a dual validation framework combining biologically grounded depth with technical and morphological breadth. For depth, we propose an IHC-informed multi-pathologist consensus protocol that substantially improves inter-rater agreement over conventional H&E-only annotation. This yields a molecularly grounded reference against which we compare Atlas H&E-TME and pathologists working from H&E alone. For breadth, we benchmark Atlas H&E-TME on over 200,000 high-confidence H&E-only pathologist annotations across 1,500+ cases spanning eight cancer types and their most common metastatic sites, with subtypes covering >90% of clinical cases per cancer type, drawn from 25+ sources and 8+ scanner models. Benchmarked against the IHC-informed consensus, Atlas H&E-TME matches or exceeds pathologist H&E-only performance and generalizes consistently and robustly across this broad morphological and technical scope. In doing so, Atlas H&E-TME turns the H&E slide -- the most ubiquitous data in pathology -- into a scalable, quantitative window into the tumor and its microenvironment, laying a foundation for the next generation of tissue-based biomarkers in translational and clinical research.

AI.tldr: Atlas H&E-TME is an AI system that predicts tissue quality, region, and cell types from H&E whole-slide images across multiple cancers, validated via a dual framework combining IHC-informed expert consensus and large-scale H&E annotations, demonstrating performance matching or exceeding pathologists.
AI.motivation: Scalable quantitative analysis of H&E whole-slide images remains a central challenge in computational pathology, and morphological ambiguity in H&E-only ground truth limits validation of such systems.
AI.method: (1) Built on Atlas family of pathology foundation models; (2) Predicts tissue quality, region, and cell types at cell-level resolution; (3) Dual validation: IHC-informed multi-pathologist consensus protocol for depth, and 200,000+ high-confidence H&E annotations across 1,500+ cases for breadth.
AI.result: Atlas H&E-TME matches or exceeds pathologist H&E-only performance against IHC-informed consensus, and generalizes robustly across 8 cancer types, 25+ data sources, and 8+ scanner models.
AI.conclusion: The system turns H&E slides into scalable, quantitative windows into the tumor microenvironment, laying foundation for next-generation tissue-based biomarkers.

title: DepthMaster: Unified Monocular Depth Estimation for Perspective and Panoramic Images
summary: While monocular depth estimation has achieved significant progress, achieving generalized metric depth estimation for both narrow field-of-view (FoV) perspectives and $360^\circ$ panoramas remains an unsolved challenge. Existing methods are often tailored to specific camera types and struggle to produce accurate metric depth that generalizes across diverse settings. This limitation stems from two key challenges: the inherent geometric discrepancy between perspective and panoramic cameras, and the scarcity of panoramic training data with metric annotations. In this work, we introduce DepthMaster, a unified metric depth estimation framework. Rather than employing specialized networks to learn spherical distortions, we reformulate the problem by decomposing panoramic images into overlapping perspective patches. Crucially, distinct from prior projection-based methods that rely on ad-hoc architectural modifications to handle boundaries, we introduce a novel Correspondence Consistency Loss (CCL) and inject virtual projection cameras as geometric priors, allowing us to seamlessly stitch the patches while avoiding specialized operators and keeping the backbone largely compatible with standard Transformer designs. This strategy also resolves the geometric differences by unifying all inputs into a canonical perspective representation, and effectively circumvents data scarcity by directly unlocking powerful metric priors from vast perspective datasets. Trained on a mixed dataset that contains only one panorama dataset, DepthMaster achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on 13 diverse datasets, outperforming not only universal methods but also leading specialist models in both perspective and panoramic domains.

AI.tldr: DepthMaster is a unified framework for metric depth estimation that works for both perspective and 360° panoramic images. It decomposes panoramas into overlapping perspective patches, uses a Correspondence Consistency Loss and virtual projection cameras as geometric priors to stitch patches without specialized network modifications, achieving SOTA zero-shot performance on 13 datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing metric depth estimation methods are tailored to specific camera types and fail to generalize across both narrow FoV perspectives and 360° panoramas, due to geometric discrepancies between camera types and scarcity of panoramic training data with metric annotations.
AI.method: DepthMaster decomposes panoramic images into overlapping perspective patches, introduces Correspondence Consistency Loss (CCL) and virtual projection cameras as geometric priors to stitch patches seamlessly, keeps backbone compatible with standard Transformer designs, and trains on mixed datasets including panoramic and perspective data.
AI.result: State-of-the-art zero-shot performance on 13 diverse datasets, outperforming both universal methods and leading specialist models in perspective and panoramic domains, trained with only one panorama dataset mixed with perspective data.
AI.conclusion: Unifying panoramic and perspective depth estimation through patch decomposition and correspondence consistency is effective, and leveraging perspective priors can overcome panoramic data scarcity.

title: A Turbo-Inference Strategy for Object Detection and Instance Segmentation
comment: Preprint version of an article published in Computer Vision and Image Understanding
summary: Object detection and instance segmentation tasks are closely related. Existing top-down instance segmentation methods usually follow a detect-then-segment paradigm, where an initial detector is used to recognize and localize objects with bounding boxes, followed by the segmentation of an instance mask within each bounding box. In such methods, the detection accuracy directly influences the subsequent segmentation performance. However, previous research has seldom explored the impact of the instance segmentation task on object detection. In this paper, we present a turbo-inference strategy for the top-down methods that leverages the complementary information between detection and segmentation tasks iteratively. Specifically we design two modules: turbo-detection head and turbo-segmentation head, which facilitate communication between the tasks. The two modules form a closed loop that interlaces the detection and segmentation results without retraining the model. Comprehensive experiments on the COCO, iFLYTEK, and Cityscapes datasets demonstrate that our method substantially enhances both detection and segmentation accuracies with a certain increase in computational cost. The proposed method represents a tradeoff between prediction accuracy and inference speed. Codes are available at https://github.com/zhaozhen2333/Turbo-Learning.git.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a turbo-inference strategy for top-down instance segmentation that iteratively exchanges information between detection and segmentation tasks via two new modules (turbo-detection head and turbo-segmentation head), forming a closed loop without retraining the model, which substantially improves both detection and segmentation accuracy across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing top-down instance segmentation methods follow a detect-then-segment pipeline where detection accuracy directly impacts segmentation performance, but prior work has rarely explored how instance segmentation can reciprocally benefit object detection.
AI.method: Two modules are designed: a turbo-detection head and a turbo-segmentation head, which form a closed-loop inference system that iteratively exchanges complementary information between detection and segmentation tasks without requiring model retraining.
AI.result: Experiments on COCO, iFLYTEK, and Cityscapes show substantial improvements in both detection and segmentation accuracy, though with increased computational cost, representing a tradeoff between accuracy and inference speed.
AI.conclusion: The proposed iterative turbo-inference strategy effectively leverages the mutual benefits between detection and segmentation tasks, showing that segmentation information can indeed enhance detection performance in a closed-loop manner.

title: Illumination-Robust Camera-Based Heart-Rate Estimation for Physiological Sensing in Robots
summary: Physiological awareness is important for service, social, and assistive robots that interact with humans in everyday environments. Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) enables non-contact heart-rate (HR) estimation from an RGB camera, making it a promising sensing modality for robot-mounted vision systems. However, illumination variation remains a major barrier to robust deployment. This paper presents an end-to-end spatial-temporal transformer framework for remote HR estimation on a new dataset with varied illumination. Our estimator integrates PRNet-based 3D face alignment, clip-level illumination augmentation, the Residual Temporal Standardization Module, and controlled hybrid temporal-frequency supervision. The training objective combines a Soft-Shifted Pearson waveform loss with a spectral Kullback-Leibler divergence loss, where a tuned weight ($\mathbfβ$) controls the contribution of frequency-domain heart-rate guidance. Experiments on a static all-level mix protocol covering three illumination levels show that $\mathbfβ=5$ provides the strongest result among the tested beta settings, achieving a best-run HR mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.79 bpm and an HR correlation of 0.982. Compared with the PhysFormer baseline evaluated on our dataset, our estimator reduces HR MAE by 93.6 %, while increasing HR correlation from 0.088 to 0.982, making it usable when illumination varies.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an end-to-end spatial-temporal transformer for remote heart-rate estimation from RGB cameras (rPPG), robust to varying illumination conditions. The method integrates 3D face alignment, illumination augmentation, a Residual Temporal Standardization Module, and a dual-loss objective (waveform + spectral KL divergence). On a new varied-illumination dataset, it achieves 0.79 bpm MAE and 0.982 HR correlation, reducing error by 93.6% over the PhysFormer baseline.
AI.motivation: Physiological awareness (e.g., heart-rate sensing) is important for interactive robots, and rPPG offers a non-contact way to estimate HR from cameras. However, illumination variation remains a major barrier to robust real-world deployment of rPPG in robotics.
AI.method: An end-to-end spatial-temporal transformer framework consisting of: PRNet-based 3D face alignment, clip-level illumination augmentation, a Residual Temporal Standardization Module for temporal normalization, and controlled hybrid temporal-frequency supervision. The loss function combines Soft-Shifted Pearson waveform loss with spectral KL divergence loss, controlled by a tuned weight β.
AI.result: On a static all-level mix protocol with three illumination levels, β=5 yields the best result: best-run HR MAE of 0.79 bpm and HR correlation of 0.982. Compared to the PhysFormer baseline on the same dataset, HR MAE is reduced by 93.6% and HR correlation increased from 0.088 to 0.982.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework makes rPPG-based HR estimation usable under varying illumination, enabling robust physiological sensing for service, social, and assistive robots operating in everyday environments.

title: VLGA: Vision-Language-Geometry-Action Models for Autonomous Driving
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models can describe scenes and reason about them in language, yet still struggle to ground their actions in the dense 3D world around them. Existing approaches either inject features from a frozen 3D foundation model without an objective that ensures the policy uses them, or constrain geometry with sparse box and map losses that provide no dense spatial signal. We introduce VLGA, the first vision-language-action model supervised to reconstruct the dense 3D world it drives through. VLGA introduces geometry as a fourth modality alongside vision, language, and action through a dedicated expert supervised by a per-pixel pointmap regression loss against LiDAR. Extensive experiments conducted on challenging nuScenes and Bench2Drive datasets for open-loop and closed-loop evaluations, respectively, show the superiority of VLGA over counterpart VLA methods. In particular, on open-loop nuScenes, VLGA sets a new state of the art among VLA methods without ego status, with the lowest L2 (0.50\,m average) and 3-second collision rate (0.18\%). On closed-loop Bench2Drive, VLGA attains the state-of-the-art driving score of 79.08, +0.71 over the strongest prior VLA, at comparable efficiency and comfort.

AI.tldr: VLGA introduces a dense 3D geometry reconstruction objective (per-pixel pointmap regression) as a fourth modality in vision-language-action models for autonomous driving, achieving state-of-the-art results on nuScenes and Bench2Drive benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models struggle to ground actions in dense 3D space because they either inject frozen 3D features without a supervision objective, or use sparse box/map losses that provide no dense spatial signal.
AI.method: VLGA treats geometry as a fourth modality via a dedicated expert supervised by per-pixel pointmap regression loss against LiDAR, alongside vision, language, and action.
AI.result: On nuScenes open-loop: lowest L2 (0.50m avg) and 3-second collision rate (0.18%). On Bench2Drive closed-loop: state-of-the-art driving score 79.08 (+0.71 over prior best VLA).
AI.conclusion: Explicitly supervising dense 3D geometry reconstruction significantly improves VLA grounding and driving performance without sacrificing efficiency or comfort.

title: How Seemingly Inconsequential Design Choices Dictate Performance of LLMs in Pathology
summary: General-purpose large language models (LLMs) are routinely used as baselines when evaluating specialized pathology models on whole-slide images (WSIs). Because WSIs exceed contemporary model context limits, LLM baselines routinely use small, high-magnification patches processed independently via majority voting, without systematic evaluation of seemingly inconsequential design choices such as patch size, patch count, and magnification. Generalist LLMs have consistently underperformed specialized systems, reinforcing the perception that domain-specific training or architectural adaptation is necessary for pathology tasks involving WSIs. Here, we conduct a systematic factorial analysis of four input design factors: inference mode, patch size, magnification, and patch count. We demonstrate that prior studies have overstated the gap between specialized models and general-purpose LLMs by choosing non-optimized input configurations. On the MultiPathQA benchmark, switching to a single balanced configuration (large patches at lower magnification, processed jointly) raises GPT-5 from 15.1% to 39.5% on cancer-type classification (TCGA) and from 38.1% to 62.9% on organ classification (GTEx). Per-task optimization yields further gains up to 43.9% (TCGA) and 71.6% (GTEx). The same configuration generalizes to two other models and to a fully held-out CPTAC cohort, where it improves Gemini 3 Flash by 23.4 percentage points without any task-specific tuning.

AI.tldr: Simply optimizing input configuration (large patches, lower magnification, joint processing) boosts GPT-5 from 15.1% to 39.5% on cancer classification and from 38.1% to 62.9% on organ classification, showing prior studies overstated the gap between general-purpose LLMs and specialized pathology models.
AI.motivation: Prior work concluded general-purpose LLMs underperform specialized pathology models on WSI tasks, but these comparisons used non-optimized input designs (small patches, high magnification, independent processing). The true gap may be overstated due to suboptimal choices of patch size, count, magnification, and inference mode.
AI.method: Systematic factorial analysis of four input design factors: inference mode, patch size, magnification, and patch count. Tested on MultiPathQA benchmark using TCGA (cancer classification) and GTEx (organ classification), then validated across GPT-5, Gemini 3 Flash, and a held-out CPTAC cohort.
AI.result: Single balanced configuration (large patches, lower magnification, joint processing) raised GPT-5 from 15.1%→39.5% (TCGA) and 38.1%→62.9% (GTEx). Per-task optimization achieved 43.9% (TCGA) and 71.6% (GTEx). Configuration generalized to other models; improved Gemini 3 Flash by 23.4 points on CPTAC without tuning.
AI.conclusion: The performance gap between general-purpose LLMs and specialized pathology models has been overestimated. Simple input configuration changes can dramatically improve LLM performance on WSI tasks, suggesting generalist models may be more viable for pathology than previously thought.

title: Reroute, Don't Remove: Recoverable Visual Token Routing for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) project images into hundreds to thousands of visual tokens, making decoder inference expensive in both attention computation and KV-cache memory. Existing visual-token reduction methods largely follow a rank-and-remove paradigm: they score visual tokens, keep a compact subset, and permanently discard the rest. We show that this irreversible action is fragile because visual-token importance changes across decoder depth; tokens ranked low at one stage may become relevant in later layers, especially for grounding-sensitive queries. We propose Reroute, a training-free plug-in that replaces removal with recoverable routing. At each routing stage, selected vision tokens pass through decoder blocks, while deferred tokens bypass the stage and re-enter the candidate pool at the next routing decision. Reroute reuses existing attention-score ranking rules and stage-wise schedules, preserving the theoretical TFLOPs and KV-cache budget class of the pruning method it augments. Across FastV, PDrop, and Nüwa variants on LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen backbones, reroute improves grounding under aggressive token reduction while maintaining general VQA performance. These results suggest that VLM token reduction should not be viewed only as irreversible pruning, but also as recoverable routing. The code can be found here: https://github.com/elmma/mllm-reroute/

AI.tldr: Reroute is a training-free plug-in that replaces irreversible visual-token pruning with recoverable routing, allowing deferred tokens to re-enter later decoder stages, improving grounding performance under aggressive token reduction.
AI.motivation: Existing visual-token reduction methods permanently discard tokens rank low, but token importance changes across decoder depth, making irreversible pruning fragile, especially for grounding-sensitive queries.
AI.method: Reroute routes selected tokens through decoder blocks while deferred tokens bypass the stage and re-enter the candidate pool at the next routing decision, reusing existing attention-score ranking rules.
AI.result: Across FastV, PDrop, and Nüwa variants on LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen backbones, Reroute improves grounding under aggressive token reduction while maintaining general VQA performance.
AI.conclusion: VLM token reduction should not be viewed only as irreversible pruning, but also as recoverable routing.
